```
"Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <jud@sunvaley0.com> writes
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> military conquest since the 1500s?

Most of the 'contiguous 48 states'.  Alaska was purchased from Russia
(for about $9 million, IIRC), Long Island ('purchased' from the Indians
for trinkets) and the 'Louisiana Purchase' being some exceptions.

The 'conquest' of what is now the U.S. was a very different process than
what happened with Russia.  In the case of Russia, there were defined
neighboring countries that Russia decided to make war on to conquer for
the purposes of annexation.  In the case of the U.S., it was (generally)
the case of people gradually moving into areas not defined as countries,
and when conflict arose, fighting to keep the land the people already
occupied.  It was not all that clear cut, of course.  In the cases where the
U.S. went to war to obtain territory, it was (except for the Indians, who
fit under the above) the conquest of a 'possession', not an established
sovereign nation.

The one *really big* difference is that no state in the U.S. would choose
to be independent (except the Indian Territories which aren't technically
part of the 'states').  Even in the American Civil War, it wasn't really a
case of the southern states generally wanting to be independent, it was a
virtual single issue dispute that motivated the southern states to secede.
Not so of Russia.  There has never been a time when all of the parts of
Russia wanted to be Russian, and none of the east European countries
annexed by Russia after WWII wanted to be.  Chechnya being a recent
example of the former, and the rest of east Europe being recent examples
(actually, the whole set) of the latter.
```

```
"Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> Sorry Judson, it seems to me that you are resorting to verbal
> somersaults in order to excuse aggressive colonization of territories by
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> independent of the civilians. Please correct me.

I did not intend to give the impression that I was excusing it.  I have
made several clear statements in these threads that I definitely do not
justify what was done.  But surely you have to admit that settlers
encroaching on territory loosely inhabited by nomadic tribes is not the
same as deliberately crossing a formally established border.  Not that
all the Indian tribes were nomadic, of course.  And surely you would
admit that if Canada were to annex Greenland it would not be quite the
same thing as Canada invading Denmark, just as stealing your auto is
not the same as kidnapping you.  Not that either would be okay.  But I
(and probably anybody else) would rather have a neighbor who might
steal your garden hose or lawnmower than a neighbor who might kidnap
your children or yourself.  There are degrees of right and wrong, and
that was the point I was trying to make.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> But surely you have to admit that settlers
> encroaching on territory loosely inhabited by nomadic tribes is not the
> same as deliberately crossing a formally established border.

Stealing is stealing.

It may not be the same to _you_ but it is the same to those invaded.
Eventually the 'encroachment' takes _all_, or all the useful,
territory.  

One may also claim that killing off a few settlement farmers who had
enroached too far is 'not the same' as attacking a town or a fort.

In fact your claim is _EXACTLY_ the problem that is causing the
terrorism that resulted in 9/11.  The Isrealies are 'encroaching on
territory loosely inhabited' by Palestinians.  In fact they have lived
there for several generations.  Israel (backed by US) claims 'god gave
us this and dominion over all non-people, such as native inhabitants'
and thinks that they can just move settlers in.

The displaced natives fight back and are termed terrorists and
suppressed.  Others in support make somewhat more effective attacks such
as 9/11.

Americans didn't 'get it' with the Indians, and they obviously still
don't 'get it'.

>  And surely you would
> admit that if Canada were to annex Greenland it would not be quite the
> same thing as Canada invading Denmark,

Did you think that Argentina 'annexing' the Falkland Islands was OK, or
China 'annexing' Tibet, or Iraq 'annexing' Kuwait ?  Actually in the
last case Kuwait used to be part of Iraq so it was 'recovering' its
soverign territory.

> just as stealing your auto is
> not the same as kidnapping you. 

People live in Greenland.  To them it is kidnapping.  To the Indians is
was kidnapping, to the Palestinians is is kidnapping.

> Not that either would be okay.  But I
> (and probably anybody else) would rather have a neighbor who might
> steal your garden hose or lawnmower than a neighbor who might kidnap
> your children or yourself.  There are degrees of right and wrong, and
> that was the point I was trying to make.

And what if they just moved into your house from down the road and told
you to bugger off or they would shoot you ?  And what if this happened
several times after you had built new houses. Would you still be saying
how nice those people who now lived in your houses were ?
```

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D759E65.2278934C@Azonic.co.nz...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One of the real problems for the native peoples of North America is that
land is not considered "ownable".  That the actual land is part of the
people's heritage, and cannot be owned by any individual is, to me, a
culturally superior belief system.  The european tradition was that the land
was owned by a single person, the king, and most of the subsequent law
results from individuals attempting to get their share back.

To a very large extent, our current woes with the atmosphere are a direct
result of that idea ... that somehow each little chunk of the world is
"owned" by one specific individual, and that individual has a right to do
any damn thing they please, provided they stay inside the boundaries of
"their" property.

That one dwells on this earth as a guest of the earth, and that each of us
is steward for the plot we live on, and responsible for keeping it safe and
sound for our people, is not only closer to the truth, to my mind, but a
spiritually more solid basis for an ethical existance.

Yet, Judson, in the above paragraph, you are using that very idea as a
weapon to justify making the natives leave, so that whites could take the
land over.  While I can see it being justified by european law, I cannot see
it working for the forseeable future. Comments?

Donald
```

```
knowledge to micro-manage your home and farm.
```

- **Date:** 2002-09-05T14:00:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al7nvl$3g6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <giTa9.79375$%v4.3291143@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <RD3b9.4021$if4.955838@news20.bellglobal.com> <WI4b9.32347$Hd4.7298397@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0208281309.79f8a8ca@posting.google.com> <TFbb9.35054$Hd4.7593386@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0208301358.6107e05a@posting.google.com> <_c4c9.9068$FJ1.572354@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D71D6BB.A300CFD1@Azonic.co.nz> <G0dc9.6784$2E6.2863081@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0208312221.3f6eb9af@posting.google.com> <IVpc9.20800$FJ1.3067903@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <sihyyTAMxmc9EwqB@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <a4Lc9.45881$vY2.1109453@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <s9jiqLA7q6c9EwLc@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <1Bvd9.52846$0u4.879375@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```

On  4-Sep-2002, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:

> >>Unless the alternative is to give that power to the king.   We all know of
> >>"kings" who are not worthy of such trusts, or who don't have the detailed
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> ownership is purely European.

Not purely.

As long as the land is not densely used, this works.   When power comes from
controlling valuable land, the king will grab that power.  One way of limiting
this grab is to give certain powers and rights to the individuals who are using
that land.  Densely populated places that have moved away from dictator powers
have done so by establishing property rights for land, buildings, and
businesses.  It isn't absolute - but the state has to do more work to force you
off you home.

It has other effects - we won't go into debt for 20-30 years to build a house if
we don't see that we have rights of ownership of that house.  We sometimes give
up ownership of the minerals under the ground, but we want to believe we will
get our investment back.   Aborigines didn't require this security to get a
return on their house building investment.
```

```
On  5-Sep-2002, Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Some places have.  Others have done so by making making lands communal.
> The United States seems to operate on the principal that money is the
> only factor that works, and that the richest automatically have rights
> to do anything.

Not so much with land.  The whole concept with the book/movie Shane was that the
rich rancher had rights to lots of public land.  The small farmers got the
rights and by owning the land, his way of life was doomed.

Land in amounts to make the rich powerful is very, very expensive.  Sure Turner
has his buffalo ranch, but that is more of a toy than a way to acquire
money/power.  There is less and less reason for the rich to own vast amounts of
land all the time - and they don't.

But government land can still be abused, when people instead choose to lobby
non-owing politicians for the right to use "public" land.

Our entire western world seems to be of the opinion
> that poisening the water and air are perfectly acceptable if there is
> money to be made at it.  If we do not change that, we are all going to
> die, pure and simple.

The pollutants you are referring to have been lessening tremendously in rich
countries.  Developing countries produce far more poisons, and poor countries do
more ecological damage with slash and burning.  (by forcing Nike to shut down
its plants, their hope of making it out of the slash and burn ecology declines).

But the biggest pollutants by far (with the possible exception of mining the
oceans - which nobody "owns"), are buildings, roads, and farms.   Try to grow
something on a highway.  Measure the temperature above a plowed field.    These
aren't addressed in any accord because the world isn't about to give up the
wealth/power of population.  Even if it kills us.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  4-Sep-2002, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> return on their house building investment.

Did they have individual homes as we think of them? Many American 
Indians lived communally, e.g., Iroquois long houses, pueblos in the SW, 
etc. Plains Indians had individual teepees, but the village moved 
together as a unit. I don't think either group, US or Australian, would 
have understood the concept of a "return on their house building 
investment". AFAIK, that's a European concept, too.

My point was that the concept of property ownership was imposed on the 2 
native peoples by the Europeans. I wonder if anyone has set up a 
simulation to see what the US or Australia might be like now if the 
Europeans hadn't come?
```

```
"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
>
> IIRC the aborigines in Australia had/have the same belief. The notion
> of ownership is purely European.

Actually, it would be more accurate to say primitive cultures have not
had the need to develop the concepts of ownership of real property,
simply because they are primitive cultures.  No advanced society
could function under such a philosophy, because you must be able to
depend on stability of occupation to develop more than very crude
technology.  Even a mill to grind grain using water power requires
permanent occupation of the location.  Such things are developed by
individuals looking to better themselves, not by nomadic groups.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On  3-Sep-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the more important such safeguards are.

No. Rather, such safeguards need to be introduced along with other 
institutions, if those institutions are not to end up unbalanced. But before 
those institutions are introduced, you can get by without the safeguards - as 
long as you are also willing to get by without whatever positive things the 
institutions deliver.

That's all very abstract. But try this: in Europe, all those levels of 
society - EVEN commoners - got their particular privileges within a feudal 
framework. And in North America, people felt no need of special defences 
against central power in the days when that was an authority in name only; 
the American War of Independence was fought in defence of old practices, not 
of old principles. The principles were actually being trampled on by the 
rebels, only to be resurrected in a new form that was to flog them with 
scorpions. PML.
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> That the actual land is part of the people's heritage, and cannot be
> owned by any individual is, to me, a culturally superior belief system.

When the fellow down the street moves into your home, without your
permission, on that very basis, let us know how you enjoy it.  ;-)
Individual ownership of land is one of the most important ways to
make people free there is.

> The european tradition was that the land
> was owned by a single person, the king, and most of the subsequent law
> results from individuals attempting to get their share back.

The European system was no better, probably worse.  It is the
individual ownership of real property that is liberating.  This is why
it is one of the fundamental principles the U.S. was founded on.

> To a very large extent, our current woes with the atmosphere are a direct
> result of that idea ... that somehow each little chunk of the world is
> "owned" by one specific individual, and that individual has a right to do
> any damn thing they please, provided they stay inside the boundaries of
> "their" property.

That statement is not correct.  Ownership od a piece of land does not
give you the right to dump on the fellow next door.  It not the ownership
of land that is the problem, but callous disreguard to propriety and the
rights of others.

> That one dwells on this earth as a guest of the earth, and that each of us
> is steward for the plot we live on, and responsible for keeping it safe and
> sound for our people, is not only closer to the truth, to my mind, but a
> spiritually more solid basis for an ethical existance.

Except for the 'guest of the earth' part, I agree with the above. It is not a
'guest' who exercises stweardship, but one in authority, such as owner or
overseer.  A guest has no right to appropriate anything without permission
from the owner.  Historically, the idea that the earth is some sort of entity
drrives from the Gaia philosophy created by those who have practiced
witchraft for thousands of years.

> Yet, Judson, in the above paragraph, you are using that very idea as a
> weapon to justify making the natives leave, so that whites could take the
> land over.  While I can see it being justified by european law, I cannot
> see it working for the forseeable future. Comments?

Where in the world do you get the idea that I think the Europeans had
a right to "make the natives leave?"  Use the land along with the
Indians, yes.  Run them all off and kill them, no.  What I *am* trying to
say is this idea that the settlers came over here with the specific idea
of running the Indians off and killing them is a bunch of bunk.  That
was a situation that developed over time, in which the Indians and the
settlers both had blame.

You and I both have the same right to use a public park.  Neither of us
has the right to exclude the other.  This applied to the Indians, because
of their own view of the land not being owned by any individual.  This
does not apply to your property that you personally own.  Either the land
was for all to use, or it was owned by individuals.  If the land was for
all, not owned by anyone, then the Indians had no basis to exclude the
settlers.  The Indian philosophy about the land only works for small,
nomadic communities.  For any (technologically) more advanced culture
it is tantamount to anarchy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Gaia (was: Re: Presidential eligibility)

_(thread depth: 38)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-10T10:33:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<allad1$f1i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <giTa9.79375$%v4.3291143@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <RD3b9.4021$if4.955838@news20.bellglobal.com> <WI4b9.32347$Hd4.7298397@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0208281309.79f8a8ca@posting.google.com> <TFbb9.35054$Hd4.7593386@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0208301358.6107e05a@posting.google.com> <_c4c9.9068$FJ1.572354@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D71D6BB.A300CFD1@Azonic.co.nz> <G0dc9.6784$2E6.2863081@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0208312221.3f6eb9af@posting.google.com> <IVpc9.20800$FJ1.3067903@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <sihyyTAMxmc9EwqB@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <a4Lc9.45881$vY2.1109453@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <s9jiqLA7q6c9EwLc@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <DFVc9.55011$vY2.1335831@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D759E65.2278934C@Azonic.co.nz> <Wucd9.4851$zv.733122@news20.bellglobal.com> <6w5f9.57758$2L.3039925@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

<<That one dwells on this earth as a guest of the earth ... >>

to which Judson McClendon replied:

<<Except for the 'guest of the earth' part, I agree with the above ...
Historically, the idea that the earth is some sort of entity drrives from
the Gaia philosophy created by those who have practiced witchraft for
thousands of years.>>

I don't think I agree with this characterization of the Gaia hypothesis, or
its outright dismissal by this comment.

It seems to me that the traditional Judaeo-Christian writings that the
directive to subdue the earth and rule over all living creatures in it
(Genesis 1:28) implied a presumed quality of judgment and benevolence, and
an alignment with the will of the Creator, that no longer apply after
Genesis 3 (the Fall of Man).  Because of those latter events, I believe we
must avoid any hint of imposing *our own* will in the subduing of the earth
and in the ruling over all living things.  After the fall, the former will
by default lead to a "rape and pillage" attitude toward the earth, and the
latter to the wanton destruction of entire species of living things.  I
don't think either is what the author of this passage had in mind, and I do
think mankind's path, particularly in the last couple of centuries, is at
least in that direction.

And conversely I am not at all convinced that someone who treats the earth
as if it were a living thing (conscious or otherwise) is in violation of the
Judaeo-Christian writings on the subject.  I believe we are obligated by
those very points to bring a caution and care to that stewardship that has
been sadly lacking for a very long time.

There are two basic points to the Gaia philosophy:  (1) that the earth is a
living thing and (2) that all living creatures on it are its offspring.
While I personally do not agree with the second on theological/philosophical
grounds (just as there are dogmatic  perspectives of denominations within
Christianity with which I have serious disagreement), I think that treating
the earth with the respect one would give to a living thing is appropriate.

From my explorations of various Gaia websites (in particular
http://gaiawest.iinet.net.au/ ), it appears to me that the underlying tenets
are more similar to some Native American belief systems as well as Buddhism
and some other Eastern systems than to Wicca or other forms of witchcraft.
This website describes five unique features of the Earth that man is
systematically destroying, and while the uniqueness of these features and
the degree to which mankind has degraded them may be a matter of opinion, I
am not convinced they deserve outright dismissal.

Further, I am unconvinced that it is appropriate to dismiss the perspective
that (fallen) mankind has systematically exercized poor judgment in his
stewardship of the earth and runs the risk of planetary destruction simply
on the basis that the authors of "The Gaia Hypothesis" gained their
respective perspectives from witchcraft rather than (as historical reports
indicate) from their observations of Mars, Venus and Earth as atmospheric
chemist and microbiologist.   "Having some points in common with" is not the
same thing as "derives from".

Have you particular evidence that Drs. James Lovelock and Lynn Margulies
were practicing witchcraft when they published these hypotheses, or for that
matter for the thousands of years preceding the publication of their book?

What if "rape and pillage" the earth is not what the Creator meant by
"subdue"?   What if the behavior of a despotic dictator wasn't what the
Creator meant by "rule over"?   A great deal of harm has been done to this
planet in the last couple hundred years by mankind, and it is not at all
clear to me that Genesis 1:28 justifies that harm.  Certainly a perspective
out-of-hand that would encourage us to limit that harm deserves better
treatment than dismissal as witchcraft.  Do we as humans treat the earth,
and the living things upon it, as a gift from our Creator to be treasured
and to be changed and damaged as little as possible?  Is it antithetical to
Judaeo-Christian beliefs to *behave toward the earth* as if it were a living
being, even if we don't accept the premise that the earth *is* a living
being?   I for one think not.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stealing is stealing.

No it isn't.  Neither God's laws or manmade laws make such a claim
or assumption.  Stealing bread because you are starving is *not* the
same as stealing a person's pension fund because you are greedy.
In the case of pioneers setteling in what is for all intents and purposes
wild country with maybe one person per 100 square miles or less is
*not* the same as invading an established country with military.  It
is not the same morally or practically.

> It may not be the same to _you_ but it is the same to those invaded.
> Eventually the 'encroachment' takes _all_, or all the useful,
> territory.

Right now in these United Stated, this very day, you can own a piece
or property, and if somebody moves onto your duly titled land, if you
do not make a complaint with a period of time, you *lose* the title to
that land and the settler gets to keep it.  Your viewpoint is at odds
with hundreds of years of legal precedent which says that moving
onto land that is not monitored and kept *is* legal.  And you can't get
angry and start killing settlers over it, or you will be tried for murder.
By the laws that I live under right this minute, I would not have the
right to do what the American Indians did when they became angry
about established settlements, some of which were hundreds of years
old.  I'm not defending any barbarisms committed by the settlers *or*
the Indians.  But the idea that the American Indians were only victims
with no fault of their own is pure revisionist B.S.
```

```
>

This is confusing mitigation with justification.

there. "He who is faithful in little..."

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> onto land that is not monitored and kept *is* legal.

That "precedent" is a construct of precisely those whom it favoured, so we 
cannot take its history as any support of its moral basis. In fact, if I 
recall correctly, one of the grievances of the American colonists against the 
Crown was that the system then did not after all permit such encroachment. So 
they changed the system; all that the change tells us, then, is what it was, 
not any justification for it. For that you must look more widely.

  And you can't get
> angry and start killing settlers over it, or you will be tried for murder.
> By the laws that I live under right this minute, I would not have the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> with no fault of their own is pure revisionist B.S.

But the question of whether THEY had justification remains open, under the 
whole set of facts and governing institutions. Sure, it is not established in 
their favour - but SETTLER laws against their acts are subject to the same 
circularity described above, and so do not in themselves show any fault in 
the retaliation. Again, for that you must look more widely than the law which 
helped suppress them. PML.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

> No it isn't.  Neither God's laws

sure that you consider some of those old books to have special magical
powers while others are the work of the devils.  You are also probably
very selective in which 'laws' that you obey: do you grow a beard ? 
Do you eat bacon ?

One of the contemptable issues that I find is the attitude that "'God'
told me to do this" or "'God' gave me this" or even "'God' is on my
side so I can do what I like".  These seem to be the basis of your
'morality'.

Of course it is also the basis of the morality of 9/11.  'God' told
them to do that too.

> or manmade laws make such a claim or assumption.

Yes they do.  

> Stealing bread because you are starving is *not* the
> same as stealing a person's pension fund because you are greedy.

Yes it is, in _law_.  People have been hanged or transported for
stealing a loaf of bread, starving or not.

You may feel that morally people should not starve for want of a loaf
of bread, rightly so, but you are _wrong_ about the law.  Stealing
_is_ stealing.  I defy you to find some law that says it's OK to
shoplift as long as you eat it.

> In the case of pioneers setteling in what is for all intents and purposes
> wild country with maybe one person per 100 square miles or less is
> *not* the same as invading an established country with military.  It
> is not the same morally or practically.

cannot do anything wrong can you.

into 'wild country with only a few villages of palestinians' and
kicking them out is 'OK' because "'God' told them they could do it",
but blowing up restaurants and busses is completely wrong, immoral and
they should be punished (never mind that is what jews did in 1948).
 
> Right now in these United Stated, this very day, you can own a piece
> or property, and if somebody moves onto your duly titled land, if you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> angry and start killing settlers over it, or you will be tried for murder.

Right now in the US (or at least in parts of it) if someone enters
your property for the purpose of stealing from you then you can shoot
them dead legally.

Your viewpoint is at odds with legal precedent that says you can
protect your property with force.

You seem to be very selective with the laws that you wish to keep for
yourself and those that you wish others to be able to use.

In fact the laws that you describe as making settlement legal was
passed by _settlers_ and was not part of the laws that Indians had or
agreed to, in fact they weren't even part of the law making process
having no vote even.

Your 'legal precendent' is just pieces of paper that can be waved to
'prove' you have the 'right'.

> But the idea that the American Indians were only victims
> with no fault of their own is pure revisionist B.S.

Only because it makes you (and many Americans) the culprits.  You
refuse to accept that you should feel any guilt because "'God' is on
_your_ side" and thus whatever is done is 'right'.

Did the Indians invade Europe ?
```

```
course, the mere fact that they didn't know it mattered is a measure of US 
indifference.

infrastructure) done by US intervention in Grenada and done by UK 
intervention in Antigua. PML.
```

```
If my children were visiting your home and you permitted outlaws to

about it myself!

not protect American citizens on British soil?  The first and primary
responsibility of any government is to protect the lives and property of
the citizens of that government.  Not to protect a negligent government's
nose from getting bent out of shape when they fail to take responsibility.

This mindless and irrational anti-American bias around here has become
intolerable.  You wonder why any American could care less when our
actions are criticized like this?  I don't, not anymore.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

> The 'conquest' of what is now the U.S. was a very different process than
> what happened with Russia.  

Only from 'white european' perspective, not from that of the native
population, but then I guess that can be completely ignored anyway.

> In the case of Russia, there were defined
> neighboring countries that Russia decided to make war on to conquer for
> the purposes of annexation.  

Nonsense. Russia started kicking out the Mongol-Tartars who had
invaded led by Gengis Khan.  They certainly weren't 'defined
countries' until quite recently.

> In the case of the U.S., it was (generally)
> the case of people gradually moving into areas not defined as countries,

The Indian tribes had territories that they considered to be theirs.
But, of course, you can completely ignore this as they are only pagan
natives and your 'god' gave you dominion over theese sort of lesser
creatures.

In fact the whole of europe and asia has had only moving borders
between tribal areas for centuries.

> and when conflict arose, fighting to keep the land the people already
> occupied.  It was not all that clear cut, of course.  In the cases where the
> U.S. went to war to obtain territory, it was (except for the Indians, who
> fit under the above) the conquest of a 'possession', not an established
> sovereign nation.

You are attempting to make a distinction that has little foundation in
fact.  The Indians did not have a founding document or constitution,
but neither did the Mongol empire which was defeated by the Russian
empire when they 'conquered and annexed'.

> The one *really big* difference is that no state in the U.S. would choose
> to be independent (except the Indian Territories which aren't technically
> part of the 'states').  

You don't seem to understand the point.  'Battle of Little Big Horn'
was because they _didn't_want_to_be_conquered_and_annexed_.

> Even in the American Civil War, it wasn't really a
> case of the southern states generally wanting to be independent, it was a
> virtual single issue dispute that motivated the southern states to secede.

In what way is 'seceding' not leaving the union ?  In what way did the
13 states not want to be an independent confederation ?  In what way
did the North not 'conquer and (re)annex' the confederate states ?

> Not so of Russia.  There has never been a time when all of the parts of
> Russia wanted to be Russian, 

There have been (and probably still are) Indian territories that did
not want to be conquered and annexed to be part of the US.

> and none of the east European countries
> annexed by Russia after WWII wanted to be. 

__PARTS__ of some of the East European countries did not want to be
while other parts did want to be.  Most of the east european countries
(and western) were formed as random grouping of different peoples,
many of which were territorially distributed across several countries.

> Chechnya being a recent
> example of the former, and the rest of east Europe being recent examples
> (actually, the whole set) of the latter.

The only reason that you don't get similar problems in the US is that
you managed to almost entirely eliminate the indiginous peoples so
they no longer are a threat.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> with rush hour traffic.

The first jet hit the first tower at 8:45/8:46 and the 2nd at 9:03. 
There was rush hour traffic; but most people, by far, commuted to lower 
Manhattan underground (the NYC subway & the PATH). The towers were 
relatively empty (nowhere near completely). An attack at 11 AM or 2 PM 
would have killed far more people.


 From CNN for 09/12/2001:

http://www.cnn.com/2001/US/09/11/new.york.terror/

The assault on the towers began at 8:45 a.m. when a hijacked commercial 
airplane crashed into the north tower of the World Trade Center.

As smoke and flames consumed the building's upper levels and passersby 
1,000 feet below gathered to watch, a second hijacked airliner slammed 
into the south tower just 18 minutes later. It, too, burst into flames, 
shattering windows and raining debris.


From: http://www.thewtcmemorial.com/

In honor of all lost, there is a 6 month memorial that has been planned 
for the victims. At precisely 8:46 and 9:03 there will be two moments of 
silence. The nationally televised Battery Park tribute will also feature 
poetry, and words from Mayor Bloomberg, and two of the many gentlemen 
that led us though this difficult time, Rudy Guiliani, and Govenor 
George Pataki. We here at TheWTCMemorial encourage you to take part in 
the moments of silence, as well as watch the service.
```

```
Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote in message news:<0bTc9.31343$NV6.1016410@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>...

> >>The Pentagon almost looks like a mistake, hitting it in the outermost
> >>ring, thus minimizing the damage.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> devastation, and the pilot came in too low?

That's what I think too.  According to one witness, Noel Sepulveda,
"if the airliner had not hit the light poles, it would have slammed
into the Pentagon's 9th and 10th corridor "A" ring, and the loss of
life would have been greater."

http://www.af.mil/news/Apr2002/n20020415_0585.shtml

So it seems the plane lost altitude too quickly and couldn't clear the
Pentagon wall to hit in the courtyard area.  Directly across from
where the plane hit on the other side of the courtyard was Rumsfeld's
offices.  Could the plane have meant to strike there from above?

Last week I heard former Defense Secretary William Cohen talk about
how he has received warnings of assassination attempts of his life
while he was at the Pentagon.  Coincidence?

--isopodia
```

```
In article <3D712377.D40671AA@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
writes
>
>Give us a little more background on this one. I checked my kiddy
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>guy who finished up building roads isn't he <G>).

Encyclopaedia Britannica:

Probably descendants of pre-Celtic aborigines, the Picts were first
noticed in AD 297, when a Roman writer spoke of the "Picts and Irish
[Scots] attacking" Hadrian's Wall. Their warfare with the Romans during
the occupation was almost continual. Then or soon after, they seem to
have developed two kingdoms north of the Firth of Forth, a southern and
a northern; but by the 7th century there was a united "Pict-land," which
already had been penetrated by Christianity. In 843, Kenneth I MacAlpin,
king of the Scots (centred in Argyll and Bute), became also king of the
Picts, uniting their two lands in a new kingdom of Alba, which evolved
into Scotland. 


>
>You seem to imply the Picts were not of Celtic stock - although we know the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Isle of Man fit in this group ?).
No, Vikings.

>
>See Bobbino - we are back to those Celts again <G>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> In article <S71b9.30413$Hd4.6568516@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>, Judson
> McClendon <jud@sunvaley0.com> writes
> >We would have never been in Viet Nam if
> >the French had not acted so arragantly and stupidly there after WWII.

Neither arrogant nor stupid. In fact, it was an analogue of Dutch behaviour 
in the East Indies - and each was undercut by outside factors. Since US 
diplomatic behaviour was part of that, you can't sidestep responsibility that 
way.

> Actually it was initially the fault of the British. When Japan
> surrendered, a single British platoon was sent to Vietnam to take
> control from the Japanese.

Er... no, not quite. There was actually a joint holding operation, involving 
both the British and the Kuo Min Tang Chinese.

 The officer in charge realised that with
> thousands of armed Japanese roaming the country he had no effective
> control and allied himself to Ho Chi Minh and his guerilla forces. He
> led HCM to believe that the pay-off was an independent post-war Vietnam.

Bear in mind the prior behaviour of OSS liaison types; they did exactly the 
same thing during the war.

> Unfortunately, the British Imperial powers decided that the former
> French territory should be returned to it's former masters. The French
> merely continued after the war in much the same manner as they had
> before the war.

Well... not quite. They did go in as a military exercise, then switched to a 
stepped up version of the usual pacification, only to find they were in 
trouble after the northern frontier started supplying the Viet Minh.

> 
> >It wasn't the U.S. who divided up the Middle East with such amazing
> >insensitivity to local feelings and ethnicity.
> It was us Brits again. Sorry.

Er... that was actually a SUCCESS, right up until there were too many eggs 
for the British to juggle. The (ex)mandates were progressing nicely towards 
regular self rule, apart from one notorious exception (which was NOT a 
British initiative). Actually, that progress was undercut by the effects of 
the US undercutting Suez - western influence via the Bagdad Pact collapsed, 
and local tyrannies emerged. Just what Eden had said he was trying to prevent 
by nipping Egyptian moves in the bud (though that was undoubtedly a mere 
excuse, he was quite capable of telling a useful truth as well as a useful 
lie). PML.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT TOP POSTERS

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "David Robinson" <david@nighttime.demon.invalid>
- **Date:** 2002-09-01T13:36:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Joc9.6121$Sm.36438229@news-text.cableinet.net>`
- **References:** `<3d70811b.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <Gf4c9.4943$KI3.29966524@news-text.cableinet.net> <Po4c9.380198$q53.12570246@twister.austin.rr.com> <1Q4c9.4983$OP3.30205128@news-text.cableinet.net> <5G5c9.380327$q53.12583956@twister.austin.rr.com> <MQac9.327225$2p2.13879758@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <akrei9$eu7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:akrei9$eu7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
> BUT "top posting" is much easier for many of us  who have NG thread
readers.
> (Both when posting *AND* when reading)
>

Threading is pretty common now in newsreaders and top posting would only
work if someone had downloaded the entire discussion thread at once.
However, with Usenet, postings may arrive out of sequence, not get
propagated at all, someone may join a thread halfway through or be reading
replies the next day and need a little context for the original discussion.
That means you have to scroll through the post to find out which point(s)
is/are being replied to.

I'm not insisting that all top posters must reform their habits, rather I
point out the valid reasons for interleaving replies and bottom posting.
It's up to them whether they think it's a more sensible approach to
newsgroup discussion.

There is one habit more annoying, people who delete all of the text of the
post to which they are replying so all you have is their reply. Very, very
irritating if you'd read the post being replied to the day previous.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT TOP POSTERS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-09-01T18:56:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D726490.8E989EE2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3d70811b.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <Gf4c9.4943$KI3.29966524@news-text.cableinet.net> <Po4c9.380198$q53.12570246@twister.austin.rr.com> <1Q4c9.4983$OP3.30205128@news-text.cableinet.net> <3D711387.527E53BD@shaw.ca> <jU8c9.3457$2V3.680307@news20.bellglobal.com> <Ohgc9.45463$Ke2.3206767@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Cvkc9.1115$NV6.5460@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```


Liam Devlin wrote:

> >     Do you break your boiled eggs on the big end or the
> > little end?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> slice of toast, broken up, and stir well. This might be an English custom.

What a bloody mess !  You are referring to 'soft boiled' as opposed to 'hard
boiled', the latter where you would slice for a salad etc. The former, put it
into an egg cup and chop its head off. For the kids, slice the bread or toast so
that they can dip 'soldiers' into the egg.

But your breaking it reminds me of a red haired grumpy Irish Mick in the RAF.
Had a hangover from the night before and arrived in the mess for breakfast. Too
late, the cook apologized profusely telling him they weren't allowed to serve
breakfast after ........a.m. "Gimmie a bloody egg", says Pat. Cracks it into a
glass and swallows it in one go. Yuk ! (A variation on that one - I recall in
Germany, consommï¿½ type soups were served with a raw egg, as is, or  mixed in).

Jimmy
```

```

"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote in message
news:Rnkc9.1028$NV6.5460@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net...
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>>You think that Baseball is more boring than Curling?  or don't you
think
> >>>that Curling is a sport.
> >>>
> >>>I must say it is CLOSE, but I still give the nod to Curling over
baseball
> >>>(with or without a strike) <G>
> >>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Well I'm glad you found curling entertaining. I didn't. Blame us
Canucks - I
> > think we 'invented' it to show we could come up with something more dull
than
> > baseball. The only 'exciting' thing I find about curling is the frenzy
the
> > 'assistants' get into sweeping the bloody ice !
> >
> > I am no sports fan, and loathe being a couch potato watching endless
hours of
> > sport on TV. When the Calgary Herald arrives, the first section I chuck
on the
> > floor is the Sports Section.
> >
> > Now baseball, as we played it in UK with PTA groups - 'Rounders' - chuck
down
> > a few coats to mark the 'bases', a tennis ball and a bit of stick to hit
the
> > damn thing with - impromptu, that can be fun. Now baseball as played in
the
> > States, (and they have the audacity to refer to the 'World Series'), all
I see
> > are great pauses in the game, while people incessantly chew gum or spit
out
> > 'baccie, and the pitcher performs like an orang-utan before he throws
the damn
> > ball !
> >
> > Now as you know only too well Alistair, cricket, not as played at Lords
or the
> > international games, but on the village green, you can watch a bunch of
> > players prancing around in their whites while you are sipping a
delightful
> > glass of beer sitting in a deck chair. To me that's a nice civilized
approach
> > <G>.
> >
> > Golf - well time wise that would conflict with my gardening. But I am
> > fascinated by the beautiful scenery that I see Tiger Woods and others
playing
> > in. (No I don't watch it - my son switches it on when he comes over for
a free
> > meal each Sunday !).
> >
> > Let's see if we can come up with a list - add and change as you see fit
folks
> > :-
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > American/Canadian Football ( that's Rugby played wearing Ice Hockey
equipment
> > - what a bunch of pansies <G>)
> > Rugby
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    I have come to the (reluctant) conclusion that any activity with
judges (referees and umpires are more or less okay) is not a sport.

    The examples are numerous.  Boxing in Korean Olympics.  Ice dancing
recently.

    A true sport has rules and goals (ie things that you must do to win)
that the average
fan can understand.  When a sport becomes entirely decided by minute trivia,
corrupting
it becomes easy.  No one really trusts the outcome of ice dancing and
synchronized swimming
but the few people expert enough to be a judge.

    At least winner of a curling match can be decided with a measuring
device, not a judge.
```

- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <%aib9.18917$n73.1060431@news20.bellglobal.com> <R_nb9.162308$Yd.7151472@twister.austin.rr.com> <bTrb9.31573$kp.342673@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <RR3thKA1X2b9EwWj@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3D6F938A.9524A70A@shaw.ca> <UqscfAAaQ9b9EwFl@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> In article <3D6F938A.9524A70A@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >His contention -  gave 'em all something they could talk about in common.

It WAS once influential in Grenada. It has now been overwhelmed there by the 
cultural side effects of something else, and so baseball (urgh) has displaced 
it - not through any merit of its own.

> >
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

That does not happen to be the case. In the village competitions, as well as 
a plethora of English villages there are about three Scottish villages. A few 
years ago one of them, Freuchie, won the championship. PML.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better?

_(thread depth: 8)_

```
In article <3D813FEB.4C4C@netlink.com.au>, Peter Lawrence
<peterl@netlink.com.au> writes
>> Scotland only play county level cricket.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am not a cricket aficionado but I think you misunderstood; Scotland
(international team) has a standard of play deemed to be equivalent to
English County level. The man who told me that (during a discussion on
why England were incapable of winning at cricket) appraised me of the
fact that Scotland played cricket. 

For the record, I have never seen Scotland play cricket nor have I ever
heard of any results from cricket played in Scotland or by any team
based in Scotland.

Also for the record, I have only ever been present at one cricket match
(when aged 7 years) and it took five minutes before terminal boredom set
in.
```

```
well as
> >>a plethora of English villages there are about three Scottish villages.
A few
> >>years ago one of them, Freuchie, won the championship. PML.
> >>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > in.

Cricket does have a tradition on Scotland.  The Scottish team have played a
number of matches against (for example) touring Australian teams.

The only reason I know this is that one of them resulted in a traditional
cricket comment.  The Aussies got 326 in their alloted 60 overs of a one day
game (early 70's, obviously).  The Scottish captain, in an attempt to rev
the troops up, made the comment "Well, at least we know how many we have to
get" - and got the reply from one of his players "Aye - too bloody many".

Source is Dickie Birds' autobiography.
```

```
Played properly, it is far from boring.  Even at club level, you will find
bowlers hurling the ball down at somewhere around 75-80 miles an hour, on

to be bored after the first time you see someone take a bouncer to the
head.....
```

```
"PR" == "Paul Raulerson" writes:

PR> 
PR> P.S. - I should have mentioned that what I need to do is open a socket,
PR> drag down a web page, and parse some information out of it. Trivial
PR> indeed to do from say, Perl under AIX. Example included below for
PR> example, grabs a stock quote from the NYSE and displays it in an HTML
PR> table. 

    I checked out the REBOL language web site:

    http://www.rebol.com

    and they, unfortunately, don't have REBOL/Core for the IBM mainframes.
    It looks like a version for an OS/400 is planned, though.

    For those of you out there that have similar needs, but are operating on
    other platforms, you might want to consider using REBOL/Core.  It handles
    internet requirements elegantly and simply, and REBOL/Core is a free
    download at:

    http://www.rebol.com/platforms.html

    There are a bunch of scripts on the REBOL site, too.  I use REBOL on my
    Amiga here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT HTML posting

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T13:00:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MhJc9.388787$q53.12919011@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<As4c9.380199$q53.12570481@twister.austin.rr.com> <wX4c9.4999$NR3.30259437@news-text.cableinet.net> <kK5c9.380350$q53.12585159@twister.austin.rr.com> <306c9.5083$K_3.30572589@news-text.cableinet.net> <Yg6c9.380403$q53.12591205@twister.austin.rr.com> <Vxkc9.1140$NV6.5460@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <Kzpc9.384278$q53.12761564@twister.austin.rr.com> <mJCc9.19403$NV6.559313@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
The source for mozilla is in C, so C would be the best choice, but you can use any language that uses
C calling conventions (even COBOL) and link it in to the executable. (Or you can use a DLL or
shared library, but why do things the hard way?)  To code the EXIT (add the function call) in the mozilla code you have to use C of
course.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT HTML posting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "David Robinson" <david@nighttime.demon.invalid>
- **Date:** 2002-09-01T13:01:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Scoc9.6086$%r.36590750@news-text.cableinet.net>`
- **References:** `<As4c9.380199$q53.12570481@twister.austin.rr.com> <wX4c9.4999$NR3.30259437@news-text.cableinet.net> <kK5c9.380350$q53.12585159@twister.austin.rr.com> <306c9.5083$K_3.30572589@news-text.cableinet.net> <Yg6c9.380403$q53.12591205@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
news:Yg6c9.380403$q53.12591205@twister.austin.rr.com...
>
> "David Robinson" <david@nighttime.demon.invalid> wrote in message
news:306c9.5083$K_3.30572589@news-text.cableinet.net...
> > HTML is bad because nasties can be hidden in it. There's that infinite
loop
> > piece of JavaScript for a start, hit a few newsgroups I frequent
recently.
> >
>
> There are always bad guys. That is not enough reason (IMNSHO) to give up
something that
> adds tremendous value to a communications medium.You are welcome to
disagree of course,
> but I think you might find some flaws in your reasoning if you look a
little closer.
>

I'm sorry, but the flaws are in your reasoning. You are assuming that
everyone is using a newsreader that it capable of handling HTML or stripping
the HTML tags from posts. The only assumption you can make without fear of
contradiction is that any RFC-compliant newsreader is capable of displaying
7-bit clean ASCII text without any form of mark-up language included. Note
that this also holds true for e-mail clients.

The other aspect of HTML posts about introducing unnecessary bloat not only
affects bandwidth but also the storage of posts on ISPs' news servers. The
current cost of IDE hard drives is irrelevant to the costs paid by ISPs,
they tend to use SCSI hard drives in RAID configurations. They're a bit
funny like that, liking redundancy and reliability in their systems. :) So
we have more storage space required, more bytes being transferred and
therefore more load on the servers. Hmmm, this all has to be paid for
somehow.

However, so long as it's convenient to you, hang the inconvenience to
others, eh? :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT HTML posting

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-01T14:55:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dTpc9.384454$q53.12763807@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<As4c9.380199$q53.12570481@twister.austin.rr.com> <wX4c9.4999$NR3.30259437@news-text.cableinet.net> <kK5c9.380350$q53.12585159@twister.austin.rr.com> <306c9.5083$K_3.30572589@news-text.cableinet.net> <Yg6c9.380403$q53.12591205@twister.austin.rr.com> <Scoc9.6086$%r.36590750@news-text.cableinet.net>`

```
Well, perhaps - MR/ICE on OS/2 has to call a browser to deal with HTML encoded messages, if you don't tell it to strip out the HTML. 

But again, I contend that my reasoning is correct; the very small percentage of readers that *may* not be able to strip out HTML are either capable of being recompiled to do so, or else have front end systems (i.e. filters) that can do so. I can read posting using ISPF, and to that it even requires that the text be translated
to EBCDIC, as well as strip out HTML.  UNIX text only systems can do the same very easily with multiple tools, including emacs, vi(vim), rn, slrn, trn, and others. 

And as for bloat - I believe your reasoning is flawed there; you content that HTML adds 'bloat" while I contend it adds information content.  As I mentioned earlier, there are very good reasons that different typefaces and justification schemes were developed and HTML allows even the most trivial message to be formatted in such a way to increase the information density and the readability. 

For example, without enhanced encoding it would quite difficult indeed to insert a theta into this discussion:
θ   - since it is a non English character and defined in Unicode. However, by sending this message in HTML format, the theta above is correctly displayed. Unless of course, you strip it out and then you have go through a lot of effort. 



Yes, in the COBOL group, this is perhaps not that important. But even here, try putting a simple equation in that we all use:   I could code it  as 



FV = PV ( 1 + i ) ^n, 



but it sure looks better and it will be interpreted far more quickly if I code it as:

 

PV = FV(1 + i)n



Last point, if you meant "bloat" as "increase in network traffic", for legitimate uses that is not even a statistical blip. For use by spammers (i.e. Sex this and Gamble that and You Won This Free scam) you are 101% correct. The masses of garbage they spew out is nothing but stolen bandwidth from everyone else. But that has little or nothing to do with the you or I using or being able to use it. 

-Paul
```

## Thread: Call Windows API Support? — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [2002-08](2002-08.md#t-7ac0984e3f)._

#### ↳ Re: Call Windows API Support?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-09-01T08:43:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0209010743.6d1f8625@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<_J3c9.6075$yt3.2656586@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Michael - comments below...

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:<_J3c9.6075$yt3.2656586@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>...
> A little over a year ago (April '01) , there was a discussion here and with
> several individuals re the [in]ability to make calls to the Windows API when
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This was just a simple misunderstanding on the point of the poster -
the vendor name lost was Fujitsu.  Fujitsu allows calls to all these
DLL's using dynamic calls - such that an export library is not
required.  I assisted the original poster to get the Registry
functions working using the Fujitsu compiler.

> > You said the magic word.  Procedure pointers are not yet supported in
> > Fujitsu COBOL - and that was the point...
> 

This one is more problematic - but solveable as well using some
"tricks" of the API itself.  At any rate, this too can be handled with
Fujitsu.

> Is there still a need for something which would make calling the Windows API
> possible from language products which do not "natively" support this? Or
> have all the vendors now enhanced their products to the point where this is
> no longer a real problem?
>

What I've taken to doing is wrapping specific API calls in an ActiveX
control.  I have a couple of reasons for doing this - when running in
a Thin Client environment using Flexus tools, I need to use API calls
to do remote file I/O and some other functions.  These need to run on
the client PC - so I use an ActiveX control I wrote to make this
happen.  Additionally - most vendors document their OLE interface ---
however, few document the API in general.  I have found that the real
problem for COBOL programmers is understanding the API and how to
interface with it -- the docs for the API are not COBOL friendly.
 
> What I had suggested at the time was creating a Windows DLL which one
> *could* call from COBOL with something aking to the syntax....
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is similar to my ActiveX solution --- except that I code specific
functions and it's not as general as your solution.

> Either that or  it was something like setting it up to use the RUNDLL.EXE
> program. The main thing was you could use all 'regular' COBOL data items as
> parameters, and would not have to screw around with null-terminated strings,
> callback addresses, etc, etc. etc..
>

I think it will get quite complex.
 
> If there is still any interest in this, post here or drop me a note; if
> enough interest, I'll take a fresh look at it.

Good luck - it may be a dead/dying horse at this point.  I dunno!
```

<a id="t-84a1050d73"></a>
## Thread: Xpediting in Batch Mode !! — 2 msgs

### Xpediting in Batch Mode !!

- **From:** "Jags" <jnori@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T04:13:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S_qc9.7030$CD2.953523@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
Hiya,

I am trying to set up Xpediter to step thru a  host of sub-programs from the
main program but when I go to the sub-program which i want to xpedite it is
exiting the routine normally after the call 'sub-program' stmt. Could anyone
give an idea why ?? And if I can get into the sub-pgm I want to xpedite how
and where should I be setting  up the files required for this pgm ??

Any ideas, advises, suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thankx
Jags
```

#### ↳ Re: Xpediting in Batch Mode !!

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-01T20:31:55+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<remtka.ei3.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<S_qc9.7030$CD2.953523@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
"Jags" <jnori@bigfoot.com> schrieb in
news:S_qc9.7030$CD2.953523@news02.tsnz.net: 

> I am trying to set up Xpediter to step thru a  host of sub-programs from
> the main program but when I go to the sub-program which i want to
> xpedite it is exiting the routine normally after the call 'sub-program'
> stmt. 

Hi Jags,  (I would never say that in a plane  SCNR;)

I think you should try the INTERCEPT Command from within the main program.
You will find syntax and 'what-it-does' via RTFM, so here just a few words:

Type "INT <sub-program-name>" when yor Xpediter session starts or put the
command in your script file. If everything is okay (source and DDIO of
main program and sub-programs were found, you will see the 'source jump'
after the sub-program call.

Hint: Also put 'stop-before' mark on the main program line that calls
the sub-program(s)

By using INT two stop tags are set into the intercepted sub-program 
automatically. One at the PROCEDURE DIVISION USING... statement, the
other at GOBACK or RETURN.
```

```
Windows is not gear for realtime networking and multi-tasking application,
it only run well in single user mode.

Paul Raulerson <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
news:NFyb9.139400$eK6.4145663@twister.austin.rr.com...
> Sure, but there really isn't enough information there to make a judgment
call.
> Take for instance, the platform he is talking about. A 30gb filesystem
running on a
> Widows 2000 implementation? That is just plain silly- W2K would not be
able to
> reliably handle an indexed file that size. So if you want to run it on
W2K, break the file
> sizes down to split that 30G file into 10 3 Gig files or something like
that.
>
 If you are on a Mainframe(IBM S 4300, 3890 ES 9000, S/390, Z series),
AS/400(Correction Minicomputer not Mainframe), or in some case, a RS/6000
machine or the equivalent,a 30gb indexed file is doable, though backup will
sure be a pain in the a**.  Still, it will be doable, and run very fast,
depending upon the number of indices you need to operate, the transaction
rate, how it is being access (i.e. terminal, JDBC, etc., etc.,etc.)
>
> 30gb for DB/2 on almost any platform, outside of OS/2 is quite doable, and
the response from a well designed and tuned system will
> be fast and reliable. But, you will probably wind up doing some data
> redesign. If all you want to do is stick a 30gb indexed file into a DBMS,
then don't both. Just use it as
> an indexed file.
>
> Now you see why the answers are not flowing as thick as you might wish? I
didn't even touch the capabilities of things like
> segregating the data in DB/2 so that single table has the data for each
geographic location in separately defined areas, or the
> capability to eliminate an outside transaction processing system, or
federated databases, or cross platform,or how you use explains
> to get subsecond response on *every* query or database pooling for Web
Access from something like WebSphere or.....

more targeted answers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T02:51:22-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0209020151.72df9af0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com> <3D6EA31E.B4EE4868@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3D6EA31E.B4EE4868@shaw.ca>...
> Come on guys, Phillip posted a similar message at M/F Answer Exchange. I
> suggested he would probably get better coverage here, beyond what Bill and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Jimmy,

I haven't had time to look at CLC for the past few weeks but if you
feel there haven't been enough responses why not suggest the
correspondent search Google archives? The subject has been thrashed to
death over years...

For the file sizes he is talking about, Database is the way to go, but
there is no easy solution.

There have been some very good posts in this thread noting some of the
pitfalls, either way.

I smiled at your suggestion for him to contact some large Electricity
Companies, especially as he is based in Worcester.

I am currently Managing the IT for a section of just such a Utility
that is based in Birmingham, about 20 minutes drive from Worcester.
(It is one of the largest in the UK and a household name...)

You may be surprised to find that things have changed a bit in the
last 30 years <G>. There is NO COBOL used on the site. And NO indexed
files (COBOL based or otherwise).

The company's multi-million user base resides on ORACLE and SQL Server
DBs and we are in the process of moving to Siebel CRM as the "New
World" solution.(This will utilise ORACLE 11 for the database).

Our existing "Legacy" systems are based around Client/Server and
utilise VB, XML, ASP, DTS, JavaScript, Components (DCOM, ActiveX, OLE,
and Web Services [XML/SOAP]), and Visual Studio as the IDE. Legacy
applications are all Web Based and would be considered "cutting edge"
by many organisations; here they are already obsolete...(although the
New World Solution will also be Web Based)

Fortress COBOL is dead here and so is Fortress PL/1. There is one old
mainframe that churns out bills for our residential customers using a
system written in PL/1 many years ago. It is due for decommissioning
in about 3 months.

We are replacing the Waterfall with RAD and it is very successful (but
then, I would say that <G>)

I have been staggered by the skill of the staff here and my own
education has progressed in leaps and bounds. The concept of
processing files in COBOL is about as relevant as spitting when you
come to crossroads, or turning widdershins when the moon is full...
Part of a bygone era.

Mr Hickling should utilise Database technology.

End of story <G>.

Pete.






> Phil Hickling wrote:
> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T03:37:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0209040237.41303c2c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com> <6a371be2.0208300223.6646123d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208301338.799b27c5@posting.google.com> <a2cb1565.0208311000.71e36a18@posting.google.com> <Dgbc9.383203$q53.12650498@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Thank you again everyone for your comments - it is all very
interesting and helpful.

Paul, and others, have asked for more information about the
environment:
The system runs on a single NT4 server, with dual processors and loads
of memory.  Approximately 80 workstations running NT4 Workstation
connect during the online day - these get reasonable performance. 
Also a further 20 thin clients in remote offices connect via Citrix to
one of two Citrix Winframe servers, and thus into the main system -
these get barely acceptable performance.

The data is basically 350,000 names and addresses in one indexed file:
 200 Mb, compressed, 1 main key (our reference), and notes or history
of each case in another file:  1 Gb compressed, with several different
redefinitions of records for different data contents, 1 main key (our
reference), and 1 alternate key (a composite key, used for accessing
records sequentially in a different order than the main key).  We
needed to be able to search the main name and address file on 10
different fields:  name, 1 of 2 addresses, client, client's reference,
etc.:  this data doesn't change very often, so rather than have the
main data file with 10 alternate keys I created 10 separate indexed
files for the searches, approximately 10 Mb each.  There are a further
15 files with ancilary information, accessed only occassionally.

All files have fixed lenght records.  The main file is over 1110 bytes
long, the larger history file is 131 bytes.

The system is accessed by the 100 users throughout the working day,
viewing cases and their history, responding to phone calls, adding
history, etc.  There are several 'end of day' routines that take about
4 hours in total to run, reading right the way through the database,
moving cases on to the next stage, producing letters, invoices, etc.

The system is developed entirely with MicroFocus NetExpress.  All
files are NetExpress indexed files, most with compression, and with
single keys, or only 1 alternate.

File system is NTFS.  Hard disks are Raided, moderately fast SCSI
disks.

All files are opened for I-O when a user first logs in, and remain
open until they logoff, usually.  Locking is manual, and only done
when a user wants to update a record - in which case the record is
read or re-read with lock, changed and then immediately re-written.

We don't use transaction processing or rollbacks.  We would like to,
but as I said, when we tried Fileshare we had big problems!

We cope with the current performance, but would like it improved - who
wouldn't!  But we are faced with a possible 10 fold growth - and
recognise the current setup probably wouldn't cope.

I am an independant contractor - I designed and built the original
system single handedly, and have been maintaining it ever since.  The
client now has another programmer to help.  The client has raised the
question:  'Would we be better with a database?', but I have never
used one - hence the original question.

From the feedback so far, I suspect more beneficial changes would be
Fileshare and Unix, and also suspect web-enabling the application to
force a thin-client/fat server and reduce network traffic would help? 
I am sure I should also be analysing Lan volumes, disk accesses,
delays, clashes etc - but I have no experience or knowledge of how.

Question:  can anyone confirm that record sizes as a multiple of 128
bytes are no longer an advantage - I have often wondered about this?

Thank you,
Phil Hickling.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T12:17:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Rmd9.198798$Yd.8581287@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com> <6a371be2.0208300223.6646123d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208301338.799b27c5@posting.google.com> <a2cb1565.0208311000.71e36a18@posting.google.com> <Dgbc9.383203$q53.12650498@twister.austin.rr.com> <6a371be2.0209040237.41303c2c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hey Phil - now the situation is a whole lot clearer. I'll tell you what I would do in this case,
which will probably be a bit different from what other people would do. <grin>

The main sticking point to your performance issue is a two fold issue; one is client access by Windows machines trying to drag that
file all over the network; two is simply that the Windows server is spending a lot of cycles running Windows.

Ultimately, you will want to go to a database, probably something like DB/2, but the reason won't be performance, it will be so that
you can deal with ad-hoc queries. You don't currently have that need, probably because you designed the system well in the first
place. The idea of using separate files and
just rebuilding them as necessary is, IMNSHO, a perfect fit for this kind of processing.

Now, some concrete recommendations:

Move the system over to a small UNIX system, I suggest an IBM pSeries B50 system, in a small rack. The base system with 18gigs of
disk is going to cost just over $3000, and will easily support the number of users  and the data size you predict. There is a single
assumption here, and that is the assumption that you do all the edits and such in a text mode, and do not utilize the GUI stuff.
(i.e. dialog or screen section stuff.)
For your users, get a copy of PuTTY (free terminal emulation) and work on the system till you have the emulation working exactly the
way you want it. You will probably need some UNIX help for this, but the task isn't hard. Takes a couple or three hours.

At this point, you run PuTTY from both your central NT fileserver and your Citrix clients. Since you have the thinnest of thin
clients here, everything will run fast. (We run about a 100 users from Citrix and they are fast fast fast... faster in fact, than
the non-Citrix Windows machines.)

You should not even have to recompile the source code for this, and the data files should FTP over from the NT machine to the UNIX
machine unchanged as well. All you will need is a MF runtime for the AIX machine.

Now that you have it moved and have accounted for the projected growth (and by the way, now have a server under 24X7 OnSite warranty
for at least the first year...) you can start working on moving it towards a database if you wish, say DB/2.

Now if you have a GUI interface, this won't work, and the best choice is to go ahead and move it to a database now. It will be
slower than the file access, but not because of the database. It will be the
ODBC  (or whatever way you integrate SQL) and the network that slow you down. Even in this case though, you should move the database
to a dedicated server, and preferably, a UNIX based server. The Linux version of DB/2 would serve in this instance.

I can probably make a AIX machine available for limited testing for you over the internet if you decide to explore that path.

Good luck.
-Paul




"Phil Hickling" <philhickling@compuserve.com> wrote in message news:6a371be2.0209040237.41303c2c@posting.google.com...
> Thank you again everyone for your comments - it is all very
> interesting and helpful.
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> Phil Hickling.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T12:47:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209041147.674c724c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com> <6a371be2.0208300223.6646123d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208301338.799b27c5@posting.google.com> <a2cb1565.0208311000.71e36a18@posting.google.com> <Dgbc9.383203$q53.12650498@twister.austin.rr.com> <6a371be2.0209040237.41303c2c@posting.google.com>`

```
philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling) wrote 

> needed to be able to search the main name and address file on 10
> different fields:  name, 1 of 2 addresses, client, client's reference,
> etc.:  this data doesn't change very often, so rather than have the
> main data file with 10 alternate keys I created 10 separate indexed
> files for the searches, approximately 10 Mb each. 

I would probably have done a single seaparte search file (of 100Mb)
with each of the type+reference+main key as primary key and main key
as alternate.
 
> There are several 'end of day' routines that take about
> 4 hours in total to run, reading right the way through the database,
> moving cases on to the next stage, producing letters, invoices, etc.

If the end of day involves checking status etc which is changed during
the day and only finds a small proportion (5%-10%) to do things with
then I would probably build a small 'action' file during the day with
one record per main record and a series of 'process type' flags
(delete record when all processing done). This should be faster than
reading through the whole file.
 
> We cope with the current performance, but would like it improved - who
> wouldn't!  But we are faced with a possible 10 fold growth - and
> recognise the current setup probably wouldn't cope.

Taking for example the daily processing of 4 hours: multiply by 10 and
???
 
> The client has raised the
> question:  'Would we be better with a database?', but I have never
> used one - hence the original question.

In general the answer would be 'No', it would probably not be faster
on the existing hardware.  However if a grunty RS6000 was running,
say, Oracle using raw partitions then there would be potential for
performance improvement.

For example by just opening files across the network the traffic
between the client machine and the server includes all the necessary
index blocks to do each index look up and update.  Using fileshare or
DB should reduce the traffic to just the actual data records.

Given that you say the Citrix clients are not acceptable it may be
that the NT server is severely overloaded and is using 100% CPU and
disk access with most going to managing the disk cache leaving little
CPU for the programs running there.  If you had two machines with one
(the existing) running the citrix terminals and the new just running
the file system then you would probably get improvements to both - as
long as it didn't overload the LAN - use a separate 100Mb segment.
 
> From the feedback so far, I suspect more beneficial changes would be
> Fileshare and Unix, and also suspect web-enabling the application to
> force a thin-client/fat server and reduce network traffic would help? 

Citrix/TSE is a real resource hog.  If it is only being used to run a
'dumb terminal' emulation then get rid of it.  Web serving is only OK
as long as the loading and file opening of CGI doesn't kill the
system.  Each interaction loads a fresh copy of the program unless you
use ISAPI or NAPI or something or use a CGI feed program piping
requests into an application server type program (eg like CICS). 
Could be a job for SP/2 thin client.

> I am sure I should also be analysing Lan volumes, disk accesses,
> delays, clashes etc - but I have no experience or knowledge of how.

You need to quantify the actual problems.  A LAN analyser should be
able to tell you LAN utilisation very quickly. Can you get disk
statistics from the server ?
 
> Question:  can anyone confirm that record sizes as a multiple of 128
> bytes are no longer an advantage - I have often wondered about this?

The advantage of x128 was entirely that it reduced the average number
of block reads because the records did not cross block boundries. 
With C2 indexed files there is a file header and record headers so
there is no mechanism to ensure alignment, and in any case you are
using compression so you could never get this.

I have no idea whether aligning with blocks would make any difference
with modern caching systems anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T21:26:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D767C5C.30C7EF6F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com> <6a371be2.0208300223.6646123d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208301338.799b27c5@posting.google.com> <a2cb1565.0208311000.71e36a18@posting.google.com> <Dgbc9.383203$q53.12650498@twister.austin.rr.com> <6a371be2.0209040237.41303c2c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0209041147.674c724c@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:

> > There are several 'end of day' routines that take about
> > 4 hours in total to run, reading right the way through the database,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm 'small league', but I concur with that design approach Richard. Even if
he moved to a DB, it would seem to be that it would be effective there too.

In COBOL terms that would be an ISAM file containing a record with only the
PrimeKey. (As you suggest the PrimeKey could be what we would normally term
the Alternate Key).  I currently do the same thing, but not on a daily
basis  - read the ISAM sequentially (1) No records - you are finished, or
(2) records found, do updating (calculations) on main file; close and
re-open the 'flag' file as input then close. Even switching to SQL,  I
*might* retain the ISAM flag file concept.

Jimmy
```

```
On 2 Sep 2002 01:28:42 -0700, fredrik.bertilsson@wmdata.se (Fredrik
Bertilsson) wrote:

>Hello,
>does anyone know which Cobol compilers are supported by Oracle
>Pro*Cobol? In particular, is RM/Cobol supported? If that is the case,
>how do I do to relink rtsora for use with RM/Cobol?
PRO*Cobol is not support by RM (yet, but I am working on it).

But, if you are interested, then maybe using the Oracle OCI will
suffice for you.
This is supported (with some limitations/work arounds) on some
specific operating systems so far (Windows NT-4/2000 (probably 95/98
and XP), Sco openserver and Unixware 7, OpenUnix 8 (and probably Linux
also)(port can be made available for some).

Regards

Frederico Fonseca
```

## Thread: Cobol Runtime Library — 1 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [2002-08](2002-08.md#t-40c8d5cad8)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Runtime Library

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T11:03:24+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akvd78$rki$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<89dd71d1.0208290527.1e5e4f98@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208291146.4c08346e@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0208291146.4c08346e@posting.google.com...
> d.g.a@gmx.net (Diego Gozzi Aranda) wrote
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

This message is because you are attempting to run a 16-bit version of Micro
Focus COBOL, or application created by it, on Windows NT/2000/XP and the
Os2LibPath environment variable does not contain the directory containing
the run-time DLLs for your COBOL system (eg COBOL\EXEDLL). You can, as
Richard pointed out, use FORCEDOS to run the DOS part of the executable, but
setting up the environment variable eliminates the need to do so as it runs
the 16-bit OS/2 part of the executable instead.

I hope this helps.
```
