## Thread: How to use the "AddressOf" of "SetWindowlong" Api call — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to use the "AddressOf" of "SetWindowlong" Api call

- **From:** gmspano <member@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T18:25:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1765940.1030991156@dbforums.com>`

```

Hello to all

Does anyone know how to obtain the "AddressOf" of a procedure?
I will explain better..
I'm "translating" a Vb source project to manage the scrollbar in a form
(i'm using Fujitsu Netcobol for Windows 6.1) and searching in the
Windows api list, i found the "ShowScrollbar" api call.
I'm able to see the scrollbar (horz and vert), but i can't capture their
events.
Here is a little portion of the vb source code:

m_oldWinProc = SetWindowLong (m_hWnd, GWL_WNDPROC, AddressOf
HandleScrollMsg)

The HANDLESCROLLMSG is a routine that checks the scroll type (horz
or vert)...

Could someone help me?

Thanks in advance..

Gianni
```

#### ↳ Re: How to use the "AddressOf" of "SetWindowlong" Api call

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T14:14:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al0dj6$e42$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1765940.1030991156@dbforums.com>`

```
What compiler (Vendor and release) are you using.

Some (certainly NOT all) Windows compilers have a

  05 Procedure-Ptr  Usage Procedure-Pointer.
       ....
 Set Procedure-Ptr to Address of  "entry-point-name"

feature.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to use the "AddressOf" of "SetWindowlong" Api call

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-02T20:01:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<osPc9.6355$yt3.2923253@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1765940.1030991156@dbforums.com> <al0dj6$e42$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Even if the compiler supports PROCEDURE POINTER, you will also need to setup
a COBOL ENTRY (or subprogram) for the subclass procedure.

BUT....
If "all"  the subclass procedure is doing  is checking if it's a horizontal
or vertical scrollbar ("The HANDLESCROLLMSG is a routine that checks the
scroll type (horz or vert)...),  it is *not* necessary to set up a subclass
procedure to do that. You will know within the COBOL program which control
is sending the message, and as the designer, you will "know" if that
particular control is a horizontal or vertical bar. You could also figure it
out from GetWindowRect, if you are willing to live with the assumption a
scrollbar which is wider than it is high is 'horizontal.' (Although this is
not a requirement. You can make really short, really fat scrollbars if you
want).

Can you post the VB subclass procedure so we can check? It may be doing
something else. It does not make sense that someone would go to the trouble
of setting up a subclass procedure just to check which control is sending
the message.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to use the "AddressOf" of "SetWindowlong" Api call

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<1765940.1030991156@dbforums.com>`

```
Gianni
```

```
from
```

```
> if dos will work with XP.  Someone can correct me if I'm wrong.
>
> hope this helps
> jim

DOS no longer exists, true. But almost all DOS programs execute properly
under W9x, W2K, NT, and, of course, XP. For W2K, NT, and XP, the OS emulates
DOS, but the result is the same. We still have over 200 customers running
our old DOS inventory system on a variety of platforms (W95, W98, W2K, NT,
and, of course, XP) with little trouble. What problems we do have stem
almost exclusively from WinModems and WinPrinters.

From time to time we have to update one of these old programs: the compiler,
Realia 4, runs swell under XP also.
```

```
I can't quite figure out whether your Cobol compiled application will not
run or the compiler itself.

If it is a Cobol App......then make sure of a few basic things.......

make sure  Command.com   is copied to the root or the \windows directory
lots of old DOS programs use Command.com to invoke file deletes, renames,
etc....

make sure the working directory specified in the Icon points to the correct
directory, which will likely be the directory where your app. runs

make sure your runtime file handler (if there is one) is also in the correct
directory

if I should somehow hit a nail, cool.......if not spell out exactly what you
are using and how you have setup the icon properties for it then we'll see


Al.........
```

<a id="t-ded50179be"></a>
## Thread: Server Express & Oracle 9i — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Server Express & Oracle 9i

- **From:** "Michael Woods" <mikedamnwoods@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-03T04:46:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<L8Xc9.79953$gb.27651@news2.central.cox.net>`

```
Has anyone used Micro Focus Server Express and Oracle 9I together? I'm
trying to compile a .gnt and .exe with dynamically linked libraries on HP-UX
11.11 (on an hp 9000) but have had some problems. The Srever Express
documentation doesn't mention Oracle 9i when explaining the cobsql compiler
directive. The cobsql directive calls the Oracle procob command to
preprocess my sql code.  The problem is, all my sql code is in my gnt and my
exe should be linked with Oracle libraries. It seems that some of the Oracle
libraries have functions that are undefined (if I do a  "nm libsql32.sl" I
can see functions that are undefined). Is this normal or do these libraries
need to be rebuilt?
```

#### ↳ Re: Server Express & Oracle 9i

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T10:28:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al4jun$dsd$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<L8Xc9.79953$gb.27651@news2.central.cox.net>`

```
If you look in $ORACLE_HOME/precomp/lib you will find make files
ins_precomp.mk and env_precomp.mk

You will also find a file called cobsqlintf.o which contains the entry
points SQLADR and SQLBEX

For running gnt code or debugging etc you need to rebuild the Server Express
run time using the supplied make files to make an executable called 'rtsora'

For running programs compiled to executables, you need to link in
cobsqlintf.o

Hope this helps.

"Michael Woods" <mikedamnwoods@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:L8Xc9.79953$gb.27651@news2.central.cox.net...
> Has anyone used Micro Focus Server Express and Oracle 9I together? I'm
> trying to compile a .gnt and .exe with dynamically linked libraries on
HP-UX
> 11.11 (on an hp 9000) but have had some problems. The Srever Express
> documentation doesn't mention Oracle 9i when explaining the cobsql
compiler
> directive. The cobsql directive calls the Oracle procob command to
> preprocess my sql code.  The problem is, all my sql code is in my gnt and
my
> exe should be linked with Oracle libraries. It seems that some of the
Oracle
> libraries have functions that are undefined (if I do a  "nm libsql32.sl" I
> can see functions that are undefined). Is this normal or do these
libraries
> need to be rebuilt?
>
>
```

```
Is there any way to do this using
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-03T06:42:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al278f$kio$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com> <ajh07t$7tc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <al1v1i$1ml2mu$1@ID-145448.news.dfncis.de>`

```
There are "similar" ways to do this (not using COBOL facilities) with
earlier COBOL compilers.  This can include using ISPF callable services,
Assembler sub-routines, TSO services, etc.

However, the only documented and supported "native-COBOL" way to do this
does not work before IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2.

NOTE: You may want to check into the already announced DROP of support dates
for all prior releases of COBOL - including IBM COBOL for MVS & VS V1
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** "gorky" <gork2y@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-20T15:20:28+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:ajh07t$7tc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> After you stop SHOUTING at us, (even COBOL doesn't require capitals any
more
> <G>)
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3) These programs need PGMNAME(LONGMIXED) and NODYNAM


Hi !

Down is the example I compiled folowing your instructions
but I got this linker error:

IEW2606S 4B39 MODULE INCORPORATES PROGRAM MANAGEMENT 3 FEATURES
                               AND CANNOT BE SAVED IN LOAD MODULE FORMAT.

What is that I'm doing wrong ?
Thanks !
Best regards ...

CODE:

CBL pgmname(longmixed) nodynam
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. "testf1".
      *------------------------------------------------------*
      * Test dynamic dataset alocation
      *------------------------------------------------------*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
      *------------------------------------------------------*
      * Input dataset
      *------------------------------------------------------*
           SELECT INPF     ASSIGN TO S-SWIOUT
                           FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTE.
      *------------------------------------------------------*
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  INPF
           RECORD CONTAINS 300 CHARACTERS
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01  INP-REC.
           02 LIN-TXT                PIC X(80).
           02 LIN-TXT-R              PIC X(220).
      *
      *------------------------------------------------------*
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *------------------------------------------------------*
      *
       01  P POINTER.
       01  PUTENV-ARG1 PIC X(80) VALUE
                        Z"SWIOUT=DSN(KBRAZ23.M950.TEMP) SHR".
       01  PUTENV-ARG2 PIC X(80) VALUE
                        Z"SWIOUT=DSN(KBRAZ23.M103.TEMP) SHR".
       01  RC PIC 9(9) BINARY.
       77  STATUS-BYTE         PIC 99.
           88 USPESNO        VALUE 00.
      *
      * ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++ *
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      * ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++ *
      * Set environment variable
           SET P TO ADDRESS OF PUTENV-ARG1.
           CALL "putenv" USING BY VALUE P RETURNING RC.
           IF RC NOT = 0 THEN
             DISPLAY "PUTENV 1 FAILED"
           STOP RUN
           END-IF.
           PERFORM 2000-READ-LINE.
           CLOSE INPF.
      * Set environment variable
           SET P TO ADDRESS OF PUTENV-ARG2.
           CALL "putenv" USING BY VALUE P RETURNING RC.
           IF RC NOT = 0 THEN
             DISPLAY "PUTENV 2 FAILED"
           STOP RUN
           END-IF.
           PERFORM 2000-READ-LINE.
           GO TO ENDP.
      * ---------------------------------------------------- *
       2000-READ-LINE.
      * ---------------------------------------------------- *
           OPEN INPUT INPF.
           IF STATUS-BYTE NOT = 0
              DISPLAY 'FILE OPEN FAILED     ' STATUS-BYTE
              MOVE 12 TO RETURN-CODE
              STOP RUN.
      *
           READ INPF AT END
              GO TO ENDP.
           IF STATUS-BYTE > 9
              DISPLAY 'FILE OPEN FAILED     ' STATUS-BYTE
              MOVE 12 TO RETURN-CODE
              GO TO ENDP.
           DISPLAY LIN-TXT.
      * ---------------------------------------------------- *
       ENDP.
           CLOSE INPF.
           STOP RUN.
```

## Thread: EAN-13 barcode font — 4 msgs

### EAN-13 barcode font

- **From:** "Fernando Kvistgaard" <fek@lr.dk>
- **Date:** 2002-09-03T12:47:53+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vr0d9.29$n45.758@news.get2net.dk>`

```
Is there any one who have had success with print EAN-13 barcods with COBOL
and with which font ?

Regards

Fernando Kvistgaard
```

#### ↳ Re: EAN-13 barcode font

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-03T16:22:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209031522.67953048@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vr0d9.29$n45.758@news.get2net.dk>`

```
"Fernando Kvistgaard" <fek@lr.dk> wrote in message news:<vr0d9.29$n45.758@news.get2net.dk>...
> Is there any one who have had success with print EAN-13 barcods with COBOL
> and with which font ?

I have printed various barcodes using Cobol.  Some I drew using raw
matrix printer graphics, some are output as a series of rectangles in
PCL (HP Laser) codes, others use specific printer's barcode interface.

OTOH I have also used various form printing systems creating the merge
data in Cobol and having the form define how the barcode is made.  For
example see PowerForm from Fujitsu and FormPrint from Flexus.

As you haven't specified which Cobol and which OS it is difficult to
give specific help.
```

#### ↳ Re: EAN-13 barcode font

- **From:** qIroS <qIroS@tlhIngan.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T00:10:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2i4dnuo92fe4umdn9t38uesbl4a723a6pk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vr0d9.29$n45.758@news.get2net.dk>`

```
ghItlh "Fernando Kvistgaard" <fek@lr.dk> 

>Is there any one who have had success with print EAN-13 barcods with COBOL
>and with which font ?
>

I remember doing EAN-13 in cobol in DOS years ago, ISTR that I did it by
outputting epson ESC codes for the relevant graphics into the file that
I was producing on disk. The file was later sent to the printer with
'print'. Was a bugger to keep all the text lined up with the graphic
part.

There must be a better way than that nowadays though!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Submitting JOB from COBOL program ?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T02:10:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020903221017.17058.00000001@mb-fd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<al29rm$1mv234$1@ID-145448.news.dfncis.de>`

```
>From: "gorky" gork2y@hotmail.com 
>Date: 9/3/02 8:27 AM Eastern Daylight Time
>

>Unfortunately my COBOL knowledge is a step far from
>"Hello World!" (JCL knowledge is stil there) so I would be eternally
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Basically you're writing an 80 character record to an output file.  The output
file is assigned to the internal reader via the JCL in the job that is running
NOT the job that needs to be executed.

//MYOUTPUT  DD  SYSOUT=whatever your internal reader is at your shop.


SELECT MY-OUTPUT-FILE ASSIGN MYOUTPUT.


FD  MY-OUTPUT-FILE
  etc etc
01  OUTPUT-REC    PIC X(80).

WORKING-STORAGE.

01  JCL-TABLE.
    05  FILLER            PIC X(80) VALUE
        '//MYJOB  JOB  etc etc.....

WRITE OUTPUT REC FROM JCL-CARD (SUB)  etc etc

nothin' to it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Submitting JOB from COBOL program ?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T02:31:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020910223126.06896.00000858@mb-mv.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<al25fr$1kbi44$1@ID-145448.news.dfncis.de>`

```
In article <al25fr$1kbi44$1@ID-145448.news.dfncis.de>, "gorky"
<gork2y@hotmail.com> writes:

>Is it possible to submit jcl file for execution, from COBOL
>program ?

Did you ever get your answer?
As far as I recall and was able to find, you just write out your JCL syntax
to  a file and assign it to the internal reader to be automatically fired off
when you close the file.

//SYSUT2   DD SYSOUT=(*,INTRDR)

Check on some of the IEBGENER samples for more ways of submitting JCL.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<amec86$re1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<al25fr$1kbi44$1@ID-145448.news.dfncis.de> <20020910223126.06896.00000858@mb-mv.aol.com> <aln15v$1r1ho1$1@ID-152194.news.dfncis.de> <6oPf9.639$e44.12282@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D88CE95.9DFEAC50@yahoo.com> <3D8AA2B2.67150016@worldnet.att.net>`

```
The online manual section (including examples) for IEBGENER is at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dgt2u110/6.0

For anyone just starting in a z/OS (or OS/390 or MVS) environment, an
INCREDIBLY high-level overview of the DFS "Data Management Utilities" - see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dgt2di10/4.1.5?

The full set of manuals for DFS online is at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/Shelves/DGT2BK11

User-Beware, if you need to read that 2nd reference, please do NOT try
everything in the 3rd reference!!!
```

## Thread: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room? — 25 this month / 29 total

### OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-03T13:41:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just a personal observation/comment.

It seems to me that recently CLC (comp.lang.cobol) has turned into a "chat
room" where the MAJORITY of posts have little if anything to do with COBOL
or data processing.  As someone who is "house-bound" and does NOT have a lot
of interaction with people on a "social level" - this seems (or seemed) fine
to me.

ON THE OTHER HAND, imagine that you are someone "new" to CLC (and/or new to
COBOL) and you look at our newsgroup for the first time.  Does it seem like
a "serious" place for COBOL issues?  How easy is it to "plod" thru all the
posts to see what information is available in this NG.

I am WELL aware of the desire to respond "just one more time" when someone
seems to have missed your point - or misrepresented your views.  However, I
would like to suggest that everyone think about how the NG "as a whole"
looks to those interested in COBOL - whenever posting a reply to a
(lengthy?) OT thread.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T00:34:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d754581_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
After being "away" for a while I find that I look at the threads with a new
vision.

I have literally been too busy to even look at CLC for some weeks now and
when I can look in it has to be quick. That means I look for topics that may
be of interest and names that I know post good sense. There are some people
whose posts I always read.

I find less and less that I want to contribute. The arguments are hackneyed
and we've beaten them to death over years. The entrenched attitudes of the
fortress COBOL people (for the most part... not all) means that there is
little point in exploring the issues because no-one's mind is going to be
changed. The Client/Server people are mainly working on small or personal
systems and work the way they do because that's what they know and love and
they have always worked that way. The majority of the contributors here are
just not able to see the way the industry is going or why.

And it was all predictable years ago. The Market Place is speaking. As I
have remarked many times here, 200,000,000 people are not about to become
computer programmers. The tools they demand and need are being delivered,
and there is no longer any real need to have "Programming skills" in order
to develop commercial systems. The smug complacency of computer programmers
is no longer relevant. I see people every day doing it for themselves and
the concept of using a procedural non-object-oriented language is as foreign
to them as trying to persuade a European Tour Guide to learn Swahili as an
important or useful job skill.

Fewer and fewer of the young are taking up the Language so there is less
need for advice. Seven years ago I was tutoring around 17 students on the
Internet at any given moment. Today there are none. I get mail asking about
.NET COBOL, (which, ironically, I don't use myself <G>), Object Oriented
COBOL, and Project Management, Program Management, and general Management
issues, but nothing about the nuts and bolts of COBOL.

The COBOL topics are diminishing because the use of COBOL is diminishing.

There are still some of us who love the language and continue to use it, but
it is getting harder and harder to do this on a commercial basis.

For instance, I am unable to make any kind of case for introducing COBOL
into my current workplace. Five years ago I could have; today I can't.

Even my beloved OO COBOL is verbose and unwieldy when measured against Java
and VB. And these tools are arcane when measured against the many "Quick
Build" solutions that are becoming available. Most of these implement a
point and click development interface. Need a database? Three mouse clicks
sets it up. Another six or seven create your tables from a data dictionary.
Load it with test data from live flatfiles? A half-dozen more mouse clicks,
and some help from SQL Server and DTS. Or an ADO component... no code
required. Want to interact with it via the Web or the Company IntraNET?
Some more mouse clicks...XML, HTML generators, and pre-built templates
transport the data to the presentation tier for formatting and interaction.
Want to be able to access it from anywhere on Earth WITHOUT holding another
copy of it on a different server? Wrap it in SOAP. No code...no programming,
just tools and components that are becoming more and more effective and
easier to use.

The only real use of COBOL today is for building components.

Application development  in COBOL is just a "quaint" alternative for the
Cyber impoverished. (A bit like making lace at home instead of doing it at
the factory...you do it because you need some curtains, can't afford a lace
machine that would do it in a tenth of the time, and you won't buy the
manufactured lace on principle...<G>)

It is ironic that less than five years ago I could write 10 good reasons to
use COBOL for development. Today all of these reasons have been overtaken by
events.

Given this scenario it is inevitable that CLC will become a chat room.
(Nothing wrong with that, but I believe Bill's concern over the impression
people will receive is misplaced. There are SO many places where people can
get technical support on the Internet these days that it really doesn't
matter if they have to wade through some junk to get what they want in CLC.
Most are students who are just trying to get their homework short-cutted
anyway. They have no innate desire to learn COBOL, it is just part of the
paper chase. As significant as learning Classical Latin or Greek is today.)

Yes, there will be installations that are still running mainframe COBOL in
10 years time but they will be hard to find. The old Management are retiring
or dying. The next generation will have no compunction in removing
yesterday's technology. The trickle is becoming a torrent. I wouldn't mind
betting that everyone reading this knows of at least one installation where
they are dropping COBOL for future development, or has a friend or colleague
who works in such a place. I'll further wager that not one of you knows a
greenfield site where they are deciding that all future development will be
in COBOL, for any Company with a turnover in excess of $US15,000,000.

 Even the last bastions of fortress COBOL (Utilities, Banks, Insurance
Companies, and Airlines) are being slowly undermined. (As I mentioned
elsewhere this week, the Utility I am currently working with, a household
name in the UK, has NO COBOL in any of their divisions...I am told that
British Airways are moving to RAD DSDM and away from mainframe development,
the Halifax (a major Bank/Building Society) is also doing the same for new
development (ORACLE)... the landslide is starting; the pebbles won't get to
vote...)

I have previously advised people here to learn JAVA. That is still good
advice (mainly because it teaches OO concepts, and new commercial software
in the future will be object oriented). However, I would add to this that
the skills that will be required in the future are more and more SQL and
packages like ORACLE and SQL Server. Relational Database rules, and the
dream of the DB Evangelists in 1983 is today becoming reality.

Furthermore, if you get a chance at training on SAP, Siebel, or Chordiant
(JD Edwards), grab  it with both hands!

Someone asked me the other day why COBOL is dying (some believe it is
already dead...). I answered with the following:

1. No credibility. No proper standard and a farce of an organization
controlling it.
2. Indexed Sequential files. (More properly, a closed file system that means
data developed for COBOL systems is virtually inaccessible to standard
packages and tools. This was OK in an era where centralized data was the
norm. In the modern world data must be acccessible from anywhere. Therefore
it must be open. It is no longer acceptable to have to write a COBOL program
every time a new Query, Report, or downstream feed is required). )
3. A lack of understanding by COBOL people of the needs of the Market Place
and a lack of tools to get robust solutions delivered quickly.
4. The failure of the user base to pick up and embrace OO.

I'm sure many of you will disagree, but, as always, I call 'em like I see
'em...<G>

I won't say I told you so in 1994, but I did...<G>

Comments on Bill's post embedded below...

Pete.

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> Just a personal observation/comment.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, I tried to keep the above to the subject of COBOL, Bill <G>

> As someone who is "house-bound" and does NOT have a lot
> of interaction with people on a "social level" - this seems (or seemed)
fine
> to me.
>
Sorry to hear that your health is deteriorating. You have been through a
lot. Whether or not the COBOL community recognises it, a lot is owed to you.
Hope you are better soon.

>
>
> ON THE OTHER HAND, imagine that you are someone "new" to CLC (and/or new
to
> COBOL) and you look at our newsgroup for the first time.  Does it seem
like
> a "serious" place for COBOL issues?  How easy is it to "plod" thru all the

See post above regarding diminishing mail of the nature you describe.
"Serious" just doesn't cut it here any more...personally, I think that's OK.

> I am WELL aware of the desire to respond "just one more time" when someone
> seems to have missed your point - or misrepresented your views.  However,
I
> would like to suggest that everyone think about how the NG "as a whole"

Who cares? At the end of the day people will vote with their feet. If they
don't like it here, they won't come. I miss the interactions we had here,
but I intend to withdraw more from CLC. It is of less interest to me and I
simply don't have the time for it that I did previously. I don't normally
frequent chat rooms and you are right that this is becoming like one.
Nevertheless, I still plan to keep an eye on it and contribute when I see
something of interest.

Take care of yourself,

Regards,

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T13:46:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3d754581_1@Usenet.com>`

```

On  3-Sep-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> 1. No credibility. No proper standard and a farce of an organization
> controlling it.

Does "C" have a proper standard and control?   Do Webmasters follow HTML
standards?

> 2. Indexed Sequential files. (More properly, a closed file system that means
> data developed for COBOL systems is virtually inaccessible to standard
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> every time a new Query, Report, or downstream feed is required). )

So we can write CoBOL that accesses ISAM data.   We can write programs in most
languages that access these files.   I don't see any particular relationship
between ISAM and CoBOL.    We have one KSDS file left in our environment that
will go away as soon as we replace the Collections subsystem.   We are a
database shop.

> 3. A lack of understanding by COBOL people of the needs of the Market Place
> and a lack of tools to get robust solutions delivered quickly.

What language should I use in my environment (IBM MAINFRAME), that would improve
on my ability to get robust solutions delivered quickly?

> 4. The failure of the user base to pick up and embrace OO.

So there are users who don't embrace OO.   Users who find the costs of
interdependency to be too high for its benefits.    OK.   Let's say you passed a
law and said that they can't think for themselves and had to go OO.   Would that
increase the use of CoBOL on the mainframe?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T14:56:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al56t2$ei9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3d754581_1@Usenet.com> <al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On  4-Sep-2002, "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

> > 4. The failure of the user base to pick up and embrace OO.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> increase the use of CoBOL on the mainframe?

I just read an article in Computer World of September 2.  Nicholas Petreley's
article "Singularly Vulnerable" discusses why Microsoft is vulnerable:

"The mistake made by Microsoft critics is that they focus on the buffer
overflows and specific security holes.  Microsoft's design flaws go much deeper
than that.  The problem with Microsoft is that it integrates where others
isolate."

Integration allows you to do lots of neat things without reinventing the wheel
each time.   Isolation provides much better security and reliability.

Picking one over the other for a particular environment is a value judgment.
```

```
> overflows and specific security holes.  Microsoft's design flaws go much
deeper
> than that.  The problem with Microsoft is that it integrates where others
> isolate."

"In other words -- and this is the rock-solid principle
on which the whole of the Corporation's Galaxywide
success is founded -- their fundemental design flaws
are completely hidden by their superficial design flaws."

Douglas Adams, as quoted in Steve McConnell,
"Code Complete," Microsoft Press, 1993, page 143
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

- **From:** "Gabriele Boccone" <gabriele.osi@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T15:14:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<prpd9.148725$lu5.3658324@twister1.libero.it>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3d754581_1@Usenet.com> <al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Mentre attraversavo il messaggio news:al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu,
un giorno senza sole,
vidi Howard Brazee pianger d'amore:

> On  3-Sep-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> HTML standards?

ANSI C and C++, you know? If you are talking about standard libraries, they
are "standard enough". If I stop using Borland C++ today, tomorrow I can
probably have my code running under Micro$oft C++, assuming I have all the
libraries.

C is standard as a *language*, since it has so few reserved words and
everything else is done by external modules and libraries. The same thing
can't be said about COBOL.

Extensions to the standard "C" language are usually compiler directives, or
"hints" (fastcall, stdcall, inline...) that can be ignored by most compilers
which do not support them.

>> 2. Indexed Sequential files. (More properly, a closed file system
>> that means data developed for COBOL systems is virtually
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Collections subsystem.   We are a database shop.

I'd never trust libraries which write ISAM data without knowing *everything*
about the format. We've been *fighting* with ISAM data produced by RM/COBOL,
since there is no specification. What many COBOL programs do on these files
is: "Write in it, read from it, don't bother about *how* it's done. You'll
never need to open those files with anything else than COBOL programs. Not
even COBOL programs compiled with another COBOL compiler."

>> 3. A lack of understanding by COBOL people of the needs of the
>> Market Place and a lack of tools to get robust solutions delivered
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> would improve on my ability to get robust solutions delivered quickly?

I can't answer, but I think some C compiler is available for you too. :-)

COBOL can be a good solution in this environment. Of course, if you have
more than one tool, you can choose the best one for every single job.

>> 4. The failure of the user base to pick up and embrace OO.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> had to go OO.   Would that increase the use of CoBOL on the mainframe?

OO is better for developers who maintain *big* projects, not always for
users. Anyway OO projects can grow with a smaller difference in cost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ ISAM and COBOL was Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T08:17:27-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D773D47.160F3F14@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3d754581_1@Usenet.com> <al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <prpd9.148725$lu5.3658324@twister1.libero.it>`

```
The problem you discuss with ISAM is because the PC and Unix
environments did not emulate the IBM, Univac, Burroughs, etc. mainframes
and implement a key sequenced (ISAM) file as a native data type.  On the
IBM mainframes that I am familiar with the keyed files are described in
the appropriate manuals in excruciating detail and accessible by most
languages du jour, let alone COBOL and PL/1.
, 

Gabriele Boccone wrote:
> 
> Mentre attraversavo il messaggio news:al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu,
> un giorno senza sole,
> vidi Howard Brazee pianger d'amore:
> 

> snip 
> 
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> users. Anyway OO projects can grow with a smaller difference in cost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ISAM and COBOL was Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T14:08:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al7oe1$3qs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3d754581_1@Usenet.com> <al52oj$c4o$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <prpd9.148725$lu5.3658324@twister1.libero.it> <3D773D47.160F3F14@istar.ca>`

```

On  5-Sep-2002, "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

> The problem you discuss with ISAM is because the PC and Unix
> environments did not emulate the IBM, Univac, Burroughs, etc. mainframes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> languages du jour, let alone COBOL and PL/1.

Because CoBOL is required to be able to access ISAM, and PCs don't have ISAM,
requiring CoBOL to add this ability natively is not a limitation of CoBOL.

But still, any language does some things better than other languages do.   IMHO,
the way mainframes will consider OO CoBOL is not for the commonly described
benefits of OO (isolation is valued more than interdependency in all mainframe
environments that I have worked in), but they will consider it for the ease in
picking the appropriate language for a particular task.

For instance, we could have CoBOL doing the business logic, and have a 4th
generation Java tool doing the screens.   Objects are used as ways to
encapsulate the communication, similar to TCP/IP.   This can be beneficial even
without reuse.
```

```
Fujitsu Software Makes Landmark COBOL Sale To Indian Computer Institute
Association

$15 million deal includes training for more than 800,000 students who will
learn COBOL using the Fujitsu COBOL compiler and tools.




San Jose, Calif., October 10, 2000 - Fujitsu Software Corporation, a leading
supplier of COBOL compilers and tools, today announced that it has been
awarded a $15 million contract to be the exclusive provider of COBOL
software and associated video training for the Indian Computer Institute
Association (ICIA). The package will be made available to the ICIA's 2000
member colleges and universities beginning Q1 2001.

"Our goal is to help India develop a strong and progressive computer
industry," said Prof. G. Amarnath, Head of Technical Development, ICIA.
"COBOL clearly has to be part of the equation because it forms the backbone
of such a large proportion of business applications. In selecting Fujitsu
COBOL, we are giving our students a quality COBOL implementation that will
be easy to learn and use, delivers outstanding performance, and that is
clearly evolving with the latest technologies such as graphical user
interfaces, Web support and object integration."

Fujitsu Software Corporation will be supplying ICIA with a special academic
edition of the Fujitsu COBOL product along with more than 25 hours of
video-based training. The video training provides an introduction to the
COBOL language, guidance in using the Fujitsu COBOL development environment,
thorough training in the design and execution of graphical user interfaces
using PowerCOBOL, and many worked exercises demonstrating the use of Fujitsu
COBOL and PowerCOBOL. According to Ron Langer, Director COBOL Sales and
Marketing for Fujitsu Software Corporation, the software portion of the
contract is in excess of $10 million dollars, which is believed to be the
largest COBOL sale in recent history.

"This is a very significant announcement for both Fujitsu and the COBOL
community, " said Langer. "Not only did Fujitsu win business in the face of
some tough competition, which is strong testimony to the performance and
superiority of our product, but it clearly supports our conviction that
COBOL has made a smooth transition to the 21st century."

For detailed information about Fujitsu COBOL refer to our website at:
http://www.adtools.com or send an email to cobol@adtools.com.

About Indian Computer Institute Association
The Indian Computer Institute Association (ICIA) is a non-profit making body
set up in 1982 by Professors Bannerjee and Balasunderam. Its main objective
is to strengthen Information Technology throughout the academic and
commercial communities in India. By 1992 it had become one of the largest
non-profit making bodies in India, with a membership of approximately 2,000
Institutions, Universities, Polytechnics, Colleges, and Research &
Development establishments throughout the country. It runs seminars to
promote its objects, and co-ordinates the supply of hardware, software and
computer training packages to Member Institutions.


About Fujitsu Limited
Fujitsu Limited (TSE: 6702) is a leading provider of Internet- based
information technology solutions for the global marketplace. Comprising over
500 group companies and affiliates worldwide -- including ICL, Amdahl and
DMR Consulting -- it had consolidated revenues of 5.26 trillion yen ($49.6
billion) in the fiscal year ended March 31, 2000. Fujitsu's pace-setting
technologies, world-class computing and telecommunications platforms, and
global corps of over 60,000 systems and services experts make it uniquely
positioned to unleash the infinite possibilities of the Internet to help its
customers succeed. Altogether, the Fujitsu Group has 188,000 employees and
operations in over 100 countries. Internet: http://www.fujitsu.com/

About Fujitsu Software Corporation
Fujitsu Software Corporation ("FS"), a wholly-owned subsidiary of Fujitsu
Limited ("Fujitsu"), was established in July 1991. FS is engaged in software
research and development, software product and technology procurement, and
software product sales. FS markets and sells the following products: Program
Products, such as Fujitsu's network management application package,
SystemWalker; Enterprise application server software products, INTERSTAGE;
Work Flow products, i-Flow; COBOL language products; and Fujitsu's
Japanese-language and translation software products, including Atlas and
TransLinGo!.

-####-
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - CLC (primarily???) a chat room?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T23:23:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d768577_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<al2vpe$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <7%dd9.26508$0u4.550230@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <%4id9.31$WU3.47676@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
 Jim,

this is an example of the covert United States plot to keep leading edge
technology out of the hands of the Third World...<G>.

Tie them up with COBOL and maybe they won't notice what the rest of us are
doing...

Given the HUGE pickup of COBOL by India, I reckon we should outsource the
production of the new Standard to them as well.

I can see it now...

"Ranji, there hasn't been a COBOL standard for 17 years. Those nice people
in J4 sent me the minutes of the last meeting (the one in Florida, where it
was agreed to have another meeting in Las Vegas in 3 months), and I have
tried to get it moved to Bombay but they are suddenly all very busy."

"Ah, Ravi, these Western people have so much affluence they must work very
hard to protect it. What are you going to do about publishing the Standard?"

"They said just to publish what is there so far, and make sure it is all in
Legalese English and very thick so that no-one is likely to read it or use
it... I sent it to my cousin in Calcutta who is a very senior Civil Servant
and he pronounced it "excellent!" but not thick enough. (It won't quite keep
the back door on the bungalow closed when there is a monsoon). To remedy
this defect he inserted several thousand verses from the Bhagavad Gita into
it at random points. This has given it a much more sensual and readable
property. Listen to this...

MOVE -- As the arrow flies from Krishna's bow to the target, driven by the
mind of God, so flies the first operand to the operand(s) after the "TO",
driven by the mind of Microsoft/IBM (sorry, that IS God...). Even as
Krishna's quiver remains full, verily, shall the first operand remain in
place...

PERFORM -- Even as Arjuna, the charioteer of Krishna, sometimes strays from
the broad path, in order to explore the byways, so does this verb allow the
temporary diversion of control from the main execution sequence in order to
explore more remote code, before returning to the Path of Righteousness...

OPEN -- Just as the great Book is prepared for reading by the Brahmin, so
does this verb prepare the data files to be read.

STOP RUN -- Even as the magic substance, Lomotil, can cause the cessation of
the curried eggs leaving the body in liquid form, so does this verb bring
about cessation of the Run Unit..."

"Oh, My Goodness me, Ravi, that is so beautiful, it brings tears to my
eyes... I am very sure everyone will want to read it and COBOL will be the
first Spiritual Programming Language. So, let us take this beautiful
standard and make chapattis and burn incense to honour it. Truly it must be
a wondrous work that has occupied such brilliant minds for such a long
time..."

"Yes, indeed, my friend, indeed...

We have also added a clause that says all vendors must have a compile option
that will produce diagnostics in Sanskrit, Punjabi, Urdu, Bengalese, or any
of the 463 other local dialects that exist in our great country. We want
COBOL to be the computer programming language of the people. Did you know
that in October, 2000, over 800,000 people were given the opportunity to
learn COBOL at University?"

"That was two years ago, Ravi. How many are still in their COBOL course?"

"Just a moment, I have the figures here...er, let me see...I know they were
supposed to be here... Aha! here it is... There are four..."

"Four hundred thousand?"

"No, four...  Gupta, who is on Adult Education... Pandit, who is on the
mahout retraining program... Saraya, who has a grant for education which she
uses to support her Mother... and Suki, who ducked into the University so as
to avoid suttee when her husband died..."

"So, Ravi, were there really 800,000?"

"No Ranji, that was a computer error... Just as soon as we get the four
above trained, we'll fix the program..."

Seriously, I have seen some very good work coming out of India. And not just
in COBOL. (There has been some rubbish too, but that simply means that the
sub-continent is much like us...good in parts.)

In fact, I shelved  a component-based artificial intelligence project two
years ago (Dulcinea) because it looked as though India would get there
first. The concept has since been overtaken by events and is now almost
redundant.

Coming to your questions, Jim...

jim <eeoyore@slic.com> wrote in message
news:%4id9.31$WU3.47676@newsfeed.slurp.net...
> >the landslide is starting; the pebbles won't get to
> >vote...)
> Here are 800,000 pebbles.  Do they get to vote???       <SEE BELOW>
>
No. They are just glad to get educated. And we should all acknowledge that
India is doing a fantastic job in this department.

> I dont get it.  Are the indians writing new code in cobol or is there that
> many jobs in India for already
> developed code?  I guess I might need to find an indian to get a different
> answer.
>
Much of the COBOL being written there is out-sourced code from the West.  It
is MUCH Cheaper to source COBOL maintenance or even development code from
India than it is to train and retain COBOL programmers in the West.
(Assuming you can find some...)

> sticks and stones might break Mr. Dashwoods bones but names will never
hurt
> him.
> He doesnt strike me as the pebble type.
>
Well..er...thanks! I think... I am definitely not the pebble type: even
though "Peter" means "rock". Despite my parent's endowing me with this solid
appellation, I am far from rock-like. Quite sensitive, actually <G>.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-41494233be"></a>
## Thread: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface — 15 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

- **From:** aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen)
- **Date:** 2002-09-03T18:13:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I was wondering if anyone has had success bringing a large amount of
cobol legacy code to a web interface.

I have the task of trying to figure out the best way to do this and I
have been looking into the following options: -

 - Using Micro Focus Net Express to create server side programs with
cgi to let the web interface talk to the existing but modified cobol
programs.
 - Using Micro Focus Net Express to create either COM Objects or Java
Beans for EJB from the existing business logic code that exists in our
cobol code. THen use ASP or JSP for the dynamic interface.
 - Using IBM's VisualAge for Java to convert the cobol to Java Beans
and running something like websphere to run the full system on EJB.

Can anyone give me some advantages/disadvantages or advice on what
you've found is the best way..

So far I think: - 
 - CGI will end up messy and a maintenance effort once it takes off
and the system grows.
 - COM is nice and easy with ASP, could base the whole thing on .NET
 - Personally I prefer java, but then we would need to buy websphere,
and possibly VisualAge.
 - Complete rewrite would be nice but probably not viable.

The cobol code is pretty old and some of it is very messy and poorly
written - in a lot of ways I would like to completely ditch it and
re-write something from scratch. But at the same time, there's no
point doing that if its going to take 3 times as long as re-using
what's there in the form of modern objects.

Just on Net Express - I have been using it for the past couple of days
and have found it to be very buggy, even after the installation of
Service Pack 1. I seem to be onto Micro Focus a coulpe of times
already regarding bugs and fixes.

Thanks in advance

Aaron.
```

#### ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T01:08:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209040008.99c73ac@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com>`

```
aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen) wrote 

> I was wondering if anyone has had success bringing a large amount of
> cobol legacy code to a web interface.

I have some Cobol CGI programs running active web sites.  Sometimes I
have used shared code between the interactive terminal application and
the web server.  Such things as pricing, discount and tax
calculations.  Mostly the web application is quite different from the
local interactive application and code isn't recycled.
 
> Can anyone give me some advantages/disadvantages or advice on what
> you've found is the best way..
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and the system grows.

I use templating for the HTML, but I did it myself because I don't
like how others have done it.  The CGI program builds a table of data
items and then the template merger builds the HTML output, often in
segments.  This way completely separates the appearance and layout
from the cobol code.

The advantage of this (for me) is that my clients can fiddle with the
templates as much as they like (within reason) to get the appearance
as they want it without any impact of the code.

>  - COM is nice and easy with ASP, could base the whole thing on .NET

I find ASP to be inverted and just as bad as having the HTML buried in
program code.

>  - Personally I prefer java, but then we would need to buy websphere,
> and possibly VisualAge.

Look at Tomcat.

>  - Complete rewrite would be nice but probably not viable.

Unless you are targetting the same users (the office workers) you
probably need to have a completely different interface.

With my systems the local interactive system is for employees and the
web site is for customers and remote reps (see www.tesa.co.nz).
 
> Just on Net Express - I have been using it for the past couple of days
> and have found it to be very buggy, 

Are you _sure_ that it is MF bugs ?
```

```
> > Just on Net Express - I have been using it for the past couple of days
> > and have found it to be very buggy,

> Are you _sure_ that it is MF bugs ?

I was a little surprised at that comment too. I've never found M/F to be
buggy since DOS days. But perhaps I don't push the envelope enough, sticking
to the bread and butter stuff. Although *comparatively* new compared to
Procedural, I haven't hit any zingers in OO. Each time I reference an
invalid object, even where I can't figure it out, I *know* it's me, not the
compiler.

Suggest you step through the Animator, Aaron - then like me, you will
probably say to yourself, "Bloody idiot !". <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface - OracleNote70245.1.txt (0/1)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** James Johnson <jamesj55@spam-not.telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T20:13:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mqkvnu8t5ma3arrefq9i9ctjqqklbdfdc5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0209040008.99c73ac@posting.google.com> <3D76809A.4A40DA01@shaw.ca> <qilinukfc0a9ckijnbt2ghg3am2js2nnuk@4ax.com> <zcde9.411481$q53.14129819@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
There have been several errors.  One is the code 114 alignment execution error
on the Sun Solaris described in the attached Oracle note.  Unfortunately not all
occurrences have been corrected using the -C compiler option and adding sync to
the working storage declaration.  We also could  reorder working storage
elements to make sure that S(9)'s fall on 2 byte boundaries but that would be
very burdensome (800 applications, 3 million lines of code) but we may have to
do that for some of the programs.

The other problem was with a report that listed transactions that had unprinted
documents associated.  In the materials management side there are transactions
that would create a transfer order, issue order, stowage order, etc and these
documents would be saved on the database.  A print daemon would print the
document on the proper printer ( a user in Georgia could have an order print out
to a warehouse clerk in California) base on the transaction data in the data
base record.  The daily exception report would contain orders that didn't print
due to printer being down and so on.  

The document  contained page breaks and line feeds and defined as PIC X(2000)
and initialized to spaces, and some transactions would create a transaction
record with only one document and some would create two.  
.

The logic was something like this (names changed to protect the innocent)

IF WS-ORDER-DOC-2 > SPACES
	MOVE WS-ORDER-DOC-2 TO  REPORT-BODY-1
	ADD 1 TO WS-REPORT-CTR-2
ELSE
	MOVE WS-NO-DOC-2-FOR-TRANS-MESSAGE  REPORT-BODY-1
	ADD 1 TO WS-NO-DOC-2-CTR
END-IF.

When animating or executing the .int using NetExpress,   if  WS-ORDER-DOC-2
contained a document it did move WS-ORDER-DOC-2 to the report as you would
expect.  

However, on the Sun server animating with ServerExpress, even though
WS-ORDER-DOC-2 was NOT filled with spaces and contained a valid document
begriming with a page break, the else statement was executed and the no second
document message was moved to the report for every occurance.

I  changed the logic to the following which worked correctly on both platforms

IF WS-ORDER-DOC-2 EQUAL SPACES
	MOVE WS-NO-DOC-2-FOR-TRANS-MESSAGE  REPORT-BODY-1
	ADD 1 TO WS-NO-DOC-2-CTR
ELSE
	MOVE WS-ORDER-DOC-2 TO  REPORT-BODY-1
	ADD 1 TO WS-REPORT-CTR-2
END-IF.

Hope this helps.  YMMV

James Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T21:36:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d766dd5_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com>`

```
COM and ASP is the best option. However, I would use Fujitsu in preference
to Net Express, not because there is anything wrong with Net Express, but
because the Fujitsu support for COM, OLE, and ActiveX objects is superb and
takes much of the drudgery out of building these objects.

You asked for experience; I have been using Fujitsu PowerCOBOL to build COM
objects for the past 4 years.. it really is fun and easy, and it hides many
of the layers from you.

COBOL is ideally suited to the building of business objects. (More so than
it is to file processing...and it is pretty good at that <G>)

Wrap your objects in SOAP and they can be used as Web Services.
Alternatively, buy Fujitsu NETCobol and you don't even need SOAP! (I do not
personally use NETCobol <yet>.)

Pete.

Aaron Clausen <aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au> wrote in message
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

- **From:** aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen)
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T19:32:25-07:00

```
Thanks for the information everyone..

In regards to the bugs and someone said am I sure? yeah unfortunately
I wish it was my fault but it isn't. I have had two problems so far..
One was the problem with it crashing the netexpress.exe when it
detects IE6 (it didn't like it, it expected IE4 or 5) - But the
servicepack1 fixed this problem as well as a lot of other potential
problems.

But then I was running through one of the tutorial examples from the
manual itself, and using its own supplied example .cbl file
(untouched) to go through a simlpe tutorial - when you get to a
certain point and click "Finish" the netexpress.exe crashed too. I
emailed them for support and the nice girl is looking into it with
their programmers and has confirmed the same problem at her end too.

As for Tomcat - yeah thanks for reminding - yeah it doesn't have to be
websphere, i've heard a lot of good reports about tomcat too.

I'm still in a bit of a muddle, there's so many options to try,
several products out there doing the same thing. I'm going to play
further with some examples that the Micro Focus support girl sent me
and if I can get one of those working nicely then that's ok, but as
someone said, yeah I would much prefer to stuff the COBOL code into
COM and use ASP - that would be nice.

Aaron.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

- **From:** aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen)
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T19:44:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<662fce7b.0209041844.35f795a9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com> <3d766dd5_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Hey Peter,

Just a question.

If all of this old COBOL code currently resides on a hp-ux server and
I wanted to chuck the business logic into COM objects.
Well I think that the COBOL code throws data at some message queues or
MOA servers or something. What are the chances of my COM objects being
able to do all of this stuff just the same ? The COM objects would
then need to reside on the web server wouldn't they (unless i used
DCOM) - but can you use DCOM in such a way with the COM objects on a
unix box ?

Thanks again.

Aaron.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-09-07T03:13:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0209070213.164da06@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com> <3d766dd5_6@Usenet.com> <662fce7b.0209041844.35f795a9@posting.google.com>`

```
Aaron,

comments below,

Pete.


aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen) wrote in message news:<662fce7b.0209041844.35f795a9@posting.google.com>...
> Hey Peter,
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I wanted to chuck the business logic into COM objects.

This, to me, is eminently sensible...<G>

> Well I think that the COBOL code throws data at some message queues or
> MOA servers or something. What are the chances of my COM objects being
> able to do all of this stuff just the same ? 

Provided the queues are simply files (not in memory) you should have
no problem.

I am not familiar with the details of the UNIX environment so I am not
an expert in this area. I HAVE used COBOL legacy code (developed
originally for IBM mainframe) and recompiled it with Fujitsu, wrapped
it as a COM object, and deployed it successfully in Web Pages and on
the Windows desk top, so I would think if you can do that, it should
be a doddle to do it with UNIX based COBOL. (This compenent did write
and read flat files. I have others that use ODBC to connect to
databases, but the staff I currently manage are persuading me that ADO
is the best option and we have had some fierce debates about
efficiency of DB access (some of which I lost and was thus persuaded
to change my mind <G>) I still like the "universality" and platform
independence of ODBC, but I accept that direct linkage to ADO objects
can be more efficient and is viable if you are SURE you will not be
transporting the code to platforms other than MicroSoft.).

There is no dobut in my mind that for serious development, components
are the way to go. In a multi-tiered application it really doesn't
matter whether you use ASP or HTML for the presentation layer or use
XML to transport the data from the component back to the presentation
layer.

We had a requirement recently to look at all the compay's legacy
systems to obtain Customer data (and update it), from a single front
end, running under Siebel CRM. (In other words, customers who have
beenn migrated to our "New World" solution (Siebel) would be obtained
from the ORACLE databases supporting Siebel, but customers who are in
Legacy systems that have not yet been migrated were a problem...

The initial solution was to do a quick "Cut down" migration, migrating
customer "stubs" to Siebel then running extracts from Legacy every
night to update them. This would ensure that Customer data could be
accessed under Siebel. It would also require customer data in two
places for the same customer, and all the horrors that batch
synchronization entails...

I vetoed it.

Instead we produced a Legacy Portal Component (it took half a day,
given that we had most of the requisite code existing in Legacy and
simply had to wrap it as a component) and will plug this component
into Siebel next week.

This will enable all our Legacy systems to continue running while we
properly address the full migration issues and, at the same time ALL
of the Customer base can be accessed transparently from a Siebel
terminal (which is what the Business wanted.) No Batch sync runs, no
"half-arsed" migration, no impact on existing Business Processes, and
no impact on either Siebel or Legacy. The customer data is simply
obtained from wherever it resides (Siebel or Legacy)in real-time.

This to me, is the best pay-off I have yet seen from using Component
technology. It saved 6 man-months of work...


> The COM objects would
> then need to reside on the web server wouldn't they (unless i used
> DCOM) - but can you use DCOM in such a way with the COM objects on a
> unix box ?
> 

The COM Objects should reside on the server and their methods can be
invoked remotely from the clients using DCOM, or directly on the
server using ASP or whatever you are scripting with.

As I said, I am not an expert in the UNIX environment. Traditionally,
UNIX has tended towards using CORBA and Java, but I understand the
latest version of CORBA includes support for the MS world of ActiveX,
COM, DCOM, and OLE. If you develop with SOAP the objects become Web
Services and can then be deployed in any environment where .NET is
present.

If it is going to continue to run on a UNIX box you might consider
wrapping the COBOL code with Java and building Java Beans which can
then be deployed with CORBA in a similar way to COM in the MS
environment.

If you get stuck mail me privately and I'll get one of my resident
Unix gurus to have a look at it.

Pete.

PS see also the following:
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/Archives/V3/V30201.asp
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen)
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T16:37:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<662fce7b.0209091537.51cdc286@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com> <3d766dd5_6@Usenet.com> <662fce7b.0209041844.35f795a9@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0209070213.164da06@posting.google.com>`

```
Peter,

Thanks very much for your extremely helpful information and especially
that article that you posted the url to. I read the article, and its
exactly what my head is thinking.

Do you think it would be better to create COBOL COM objects and let
them reside on a windows server or leave the legacy code running on
the unix machine and somehow call it possibly via a client/server
binding type tool (Micro Focus writes something to do this, however I
feel its a bit of a hack/workaround and temporary solution to moving
the cobol into objects).

Personally, It makes 100% sense to me to ditch the legacy programs in
their 'program' form and slam the logic into objects. The legacy
programs contain dodgy screen layout code and also message queue code
- which to me is ridiculous.

I even think that doing a cheap hack like talking to the existing
cobol in its same form on the unix server might actualyl take the same
or longer to implement as doing it the smart object way..

Regards

Aaron.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Giving legacy cobol code a web interface

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-09-10T09:37:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0209100837.65285fd4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<662fce7b.0209031713.55336d3d@posting.google.com> <3d766dd5_6@Usenet.com> <662fce7b.0209041844.35f795a9@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0209070213.164da06@posting.google.com> <662fce7b.0209091537.51cdc286@posting.google.com>`

```
aaron.clausen@newcastlepermanent.com.au (Aaron Clausen) wrote in message news:<662fce7b.0209091537.51cdc286@posting.google.com>...

> 
> Thanks very much for your extremely helpful information and especially
> that article that you posted the url to. I read the article, and its
> exactly what my head is thinking.
>
Glad you liked it. Sometimes I lose sight of the fact that this NG CAN
actually help people...<G>

> Do you think it would be better to create COBOL COM objects and let
> them reside on a windows server or leave the legacy code running on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the cobol into objects).

I agree. I would go for the components every time because encapsulated
re-usable functionality has to be better than continuing to point at
something that is well past its "use by" date. I'm sure you will be
able to lever much of the code and simply discard the inbuilt
"presentation layer" and replace it with a proper transport to HTML or
ASP.

> 
> Personally, It makes 100% sense to me to ditch the legacy programs in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

We agree on this.
 
> I even think that doing a cheap hack like talking to the existing
> cobol in its same form on the unix server might actualyl take the same
> or longer to implement as doing it the smart object way..
> 

Absolutely! Go Objects! (There is a spinoff in that other software can
also talk to your Objects, so you could import existing functionality
to a WORD document or Spreadsheet with no additional effort on your
part...All because of the magic of the COM (ActiveX) interface. Now,
with SOAP and XML your code can become a Web Service and then you are
really smoking <G>!

Wrap that Source code and let it go. The days of King Source are
nearly over; long live King Object!.

Cheers,

Pete.
```

```
Hi Aaron,

You might want to take a look at the C-Cubed MVS Direct API product - it
allows you to call MVS load modules directly, including Cobol, Assembler,
PL/I, C, etc.   They have several examples on their web-site of how they
interface with various MVS products:  CA-7, CA-View, Information Management,
Endevor, IOF, etc..  The price is very reasonable as well.

They can be found at www.c-cubed.net
```

## Thread: Text Generation and Error Handling — 1 msgs

### Text Generation and Error Handling

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T02:15:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c253b8$a3e24c80$f595f243@chottel>`

```
A while back there was a thread on generating text and a program named
RACTOR or RACTER? In the "Practice of Programming" by Brian Kernigan and
Rob Pike, chapter 3 discusses a "markov chain algorithm". Sounds
intimidating but the program reads text and generates new text based on the
frequency of appearance of phrases of a fixed length. They give the program
in C, C++, AWK, and PERL.

In the same book page 111 they discuss error handling: "Detect errors at a
low level, handle them at a high level. ... In most cases the caller should
determine how to handle an error, not he callee.  ...
```

```
private email to:

recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe) — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe)

- **From:** baskar <member@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T17:29:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1774189.1031160566@dbforums.com>`

```

Please refer the following program

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.   DYNAMIC1.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
SELECT INPUT-FILE ASSIGN TO INPUTF.
SELECT OUTPUT-FILE ASSIGN TO OUTPUTF.
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD  INPUT-FILE
RECORDING MODE IS F.
01  INPUT-FILE-REC            PIC X(80).

FD  OUTPUT-FILE
RECORDING MODE IS F.
01  OUTPUT-FILE-REC           PIC X(80).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
77  ADDRESS-POINTER        POINTER.
77  RC                                PIC 9(9) BINARY.
77  p pointer.
77  INPUTF-var   PIC X(07) VALUE Z"INPUTF".
77  VAR-PTR                POINTER.
77  VAR-len                pic 9(4) binary.
77  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARA             PIC X(43)
VALUE Z"INPUTF=DSN('COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAIN)') SHR".
77  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARB             PIC X(43)
VALUE Z"OUTPUTF=DSN(COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAOUT)) SHR".
linkage section.
01  var    pic x(50).
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
DISPLAY '-' FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARA '-'
DISPLAY '-' FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARB '-'
SET ADDRESS-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF
FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARA.
call 'putenv' using by value address-pointer
returning rc.

     IF RC NOT = 0 THEN
     DISPLAY 'CALL FAILED '
     STOP RUN.

     SET p              TO  ADDRESS OF
     INPUTF-VAR.
     call 'getenv' using by value P
     returning VAR-PTR.
     if var-ptr =  null
     display 'inputf not set'
     else
     Set address of var to var-ptr
     Move 0 to var-len
     Inspect var tallying var-len
     for characters before initial X"00"
     Display "PATH = " var(1:var-len)
     End-if

     OPEN INPUT INPUT-FILE.

    set address-pointer to address of
    file-environment-varb.
    call 'putenv' using by value address-pointer
    returning rc.

    IF RC NOT = 0 THEN
    DISPLAY 'CALL FAILED '
    STOP RUN.

    OPEN OUTPUT OUTPUT-FILE

    READ INPUT-FILE.
    MOVE INPUT-FILE-REC TO OUTPUT-FILE-REC.
    WRITE OUTPUT-FILE-REC.
    CLOSE INPUT-FILE OUTPUT-FILE.
    STOP RUN.

The above sets the Environment variable correctly and I was able to get
the value using "getenv". I read in the manual that the LE will look for
the DDNAME in the JCL first and if it not present it will look for the
Environment variable with the same name.

While running my program fails with the following message
-INPUTF=DSN('COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAIN)') SHR -
-OUTPUTF=DSN(COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAOUT)) SHR -
PATH = DSN('COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAIN)') SHR
IGZ0035S There was an unsuccessful OPEN or CLOSE of file INPUTF in
program DYNAMIC1 at relative location X'060E'. Neither FILE STATUS nor
an ERROR declarative were specified. The status  code was 35. From
compile unit DYNAMIC1 at entry point DYNAMIC1 at compile unit offset
+0000060E at address 10600B66.
LEAID ENTERED (LEVEL 08/21/2000 AT 14.41)
LEAID ABENDAID DD ALLOCATED BY #XAAMKDD DYNALLOC RC =00000


I have tried the above program with
a) Filename with quotes
b) Filename without quotes
c) I also tried removing high level qualifer which is my userid.

Nothing seems to work. Can someone help ? Is there any runtime option
which needs to be set for the above to work ?

==============================================
More info
==============================================

Compiler : IBM COBOL FOR OS/390 & VM  2.2.1

cA: OS/390 V2R10 Language Environment

Options in effect
ABPERC(NONE)
ABTERMENC(ABEND)
NOAIXBLD
ALL31(OFF)
ANYHEAP(16384,8192,ANYWHERE,FREE)
NOAUTOTASK
BELOWHEAP(8192,4096,FREE)
CBLOPTS(ON)
CBLPSHPOP(ON)
CBLQDA(ON)
CHECK(OFF)
COUNTRY(US)
NODEBUG
DEPTHCONDLMT(10)
ENVAR("")
ERRCOUNT(0)
ERRUNIT(6)
FILEHIST
NOFLOW
HEAP(524288,524288,ANYWHERE,KEEP,40960,4096

  HEAPCHK(OFF,1,0)
  HEAPPOOLS(OFF,8,10,32,10,128,10,256,10,1024

  INFOMSGFILTER(OFF,,,,)
  INQPCOPN
  INTERRUPT(OFF)
  LIBRARY(SYSCEE)
  LIBSTACK(8192,4096,FREE)
  MSGFILE(SYSOUT,FBA,121,0)
  MSGQ(15)
  NATLANG(ENU)
  OCSTATUS
  NOPC
  PLITASKCOUNT(20)
  POSIX(OFF)
  PROFILE(OFF,"")
  PRTUNIT(6)
  PUNUNIT(7)
  RDRUNIT(5)
  RECPAD(OFF)
  RPTOPTS(OFF)

  NORTEREUS

  NOSIMVRD
  STACK(524288,524288,BELOW,KEEP)
  STORAGE(NONE,NONE,NONE,8192)
  TERMTHDACT(UADUMP)
  NOTEST(ALL,"*","PROMPT","INSPPREF")
  THREADHEAP(4096,4096,ANYWHERE,KEEP)
  TRACE(OFF,4096,DUMP,LE=0)
  TRAP(ON,SPIE)
  UPSI(00000000)
  NOUSRHDLR()
  VCTRSAVE(OFF)
  VERSION()
  XUFLOW(AUTO)
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T13:39:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al5k97$jlf$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1774189.1031160566@dbforums.com>`

```
You have shown us the run-time (LE) options, but have not shown us your
compile time options.  There are (at least) 2 required compile-time options
(that you may NOT be using).  They are:

  "NODYNAM and PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)"

See:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.3

I also seem to remeber that there MIGHT be problems with using spaces
instead of commas to separate "parameters" - even though it is documented as
valid to do so.  See:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr00/4.2.3.1

You might want to try changing,

> 77  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARA             PIC X(43)
> VALUE Z"INPUTF=DSN('COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAIN)') SHR".
> 77  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARB             PIC X(43)
> VALUE Z"OUTPUTF=DSN(COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAOUT)) SHR".

    to

 77  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARA             PIC X(43)
 VALUE Z"INPUTF=DSN('COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAIN)'), SHR".
 77  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARB             PIC X(43)
 VALUE Z"OUTPUTF=DSN(COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAOUT)), SHR".

P.S.  Is your OUTPUT file really "DISP=SHR" ?  (i.e. does it exist already?)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe)

- **From:** waynemorrone@yahoo.com (Wayne Morrone)
- **Date:** 2002-09-20T14:01:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0f7e531.0209201301.1a95305a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1774189.1031160566@dbforums.com> <al5k97$jlf$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Try changing your working storage as described below.  The null field
will delimit your environment variable.  Note you need to change the
data description from a 77 level to something else.  I used an 01
level.

01  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARB.
    05 FILLER                PIC X(43)
        VALUE Z"OUTPUTF=DSN(COGBD.WORK.COBOL(DYNAOUT)) SHR".
    05 FILLER                PIC X(01) VALUE LOW-VALUES.

For your reading enjoyment the bookmanger book Enterprise COBOL for
z/OS and OS/390 V3R1 Programming Guide, is pretty good.  But beware,
the examples for dynamic allocation do not reflect how the book
describes setting environment variables.  This case in point.

Good luck


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<al5k97$jlf$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>...
> You have shown us the run-time (LE) options, but have not shown us your
> compile time options.  There are (at least) 2 required compile-time options
…[209 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-04T15:25:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al5q94$7lt$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1774189.1031160566@dbforums.com> <1774790.1031167695@dbforums.com>`

```
Sorry, I forgot to look at your source code.

When you use PGMNAME(LONGMIXED),  you need to put your Program-Id in quotes,
e.e.

Program-Id.  "MyPGM".

See:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/2.4.39.3


Also,
  If your "load module" resides in a PDS rather than PDSE, then you need to
use the pre-linker.  See:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/4.3.4
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe)

- **From:** baskar <member@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T14:27:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1778493.1031236043@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<1774189.1031160566@dbforums.com>`

```

As you said, I compiled, pre-linked and link edited and still it is
not working.

Then I looked at the Run time options, The POSIX run time option was set
OFF. I changed it to POSIX(ON) and still it is not working.

Do I need to have Z/OS for it to work. Currently I have OS/390 V2 R10
Language Environment.

Please help me
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic File allocation using putenv (In Mainframe)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T10:28:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al7t82$uk3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1774189.1031160566@dbforums.com> <1778493.1031236043@dbforums.com>`

```
No, you do not z/OS (LE).  It should work with the LE in OS/390 V2 R10
*should* work.

Can you either email me (remove the NOSPAM if you try and reply to this
note) or POST the exact compile listing output?  I *might* see something
there that would ring a bell.

Alternatively (and possibly better) you should have your COBOL systems
programmer contact IBM support to find out what is missing.

Either way, make certain that you tell whether your load modules are in a
PDS, PDSE, or HFS file.   Also, I assume you are trying to run this in
"batch" - not under IMS or CICS or USS.

Finally, one more time,  your file status 35 indicates that the file does
NOT exist already.  As you are using DISP=SHR, you are POSITIVE that the
files do exist (INPUT and OUTPUT) and that they are already cataloged -
before you try and allocate them?
```

```
why should I have to familiarize myself with two O/S  ?".

Jimmy
```
