```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote

> - You need a compiler which acknowledges Linux, or can you make things 'fit' ?

There are several.  I've been using MicroFocus for a couple of years
on Linux.  Same code runs on DOS, Windows, SCO Unix and Linux.

I am working through Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux, it is not the final
release yet though.

There are also Tiny-Cobol, OpenCobol and Kobol (from the Kompany).
These are free or cheap but are not yet complete (to my needs).

CobolScript is almost like Cobol and useful for some things on Windows
or Linux.

> From memory the Micro Focus solution to that would cost me about $3,000 +
> Canadian.

Yes, probably, plus run time licencing.  Bummer.  At least Fujitsu is
free run-times.  It seems quite solid but no Screen-Section or X/Open.
 CGI works well, communications, SQL, file sharing all work out of
box.

> -  How do you sell your customers on switching from Windows to Linux - their
> immediate response, "I'm using so much already that is written for Windows,
> why should I have to familiarize myself with two O/S  ?".

Fortunately I do systems for non-Windows users: warehousing,
distribution, workshops.  Some clients have a requirement that it
_not_ be Windows because the end-user will only be running the
application and not spending half the working hours changing the
screen saver to the latest animated one EMailed to them or playing
minesweeper.

The requirement is increasingly for bar code readers and RF terminals
(8 lines by 20ch).  Also using CGI to allow customer access to the
live data.

Of course the systems can be run from Windows machines.  Telnet to the
Linux server and run the application - over local LAN or internet.

> About a year ago I saw a small article about a software house here working in
> Linux. (no Jerry, they weren't Christians, Bhuddits, Maoists or any other sort
> of cult <G>). But I've not seen a word since about Linux in the oil patch -
> doesn't mean it isn't being used - but certainly no publicity.

Depends where you look I guess.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Linux (was Re: test)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T12:12:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209051112.35d6d1f4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D76599B.2000109@sympatico.ca> <3d766dcc_6@Usenet.com> <3D767706.6020400@sympatico.ca> <y1wd9.53309$0u4.891023@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D76A6CE.9000508@btopenworld.com> <3D76A855.81ED8F22@shaw.ca> <LIId9.6401$zv.1204679@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote 

> What I am hearing Jimmy, is that the new versions of Linux can run windows
> as an application. Since windows is essentially a single user system, I
> really do not see why this cannot be done, and if so, then it would make the
> job fairly straightforward.

There have been several ways of running Windows or Windows programs on
Linux for some years.  For example Lin4Win will run an actual copy of
Windows 95/98/ME in a window.  This derives from the old SCO Merge
that started a decade ago running DOS and Win3.x.  It works well, uses
Linux file system and so files can be accessed easily by both and it
can be faster than Windows on the same machine because the Linux file
system is better than FAT32.  It won't run NT/2000/XP.

VMWare will boot several operating systems at the same time (including
Linux and Windows NT/2000).  These are partitioned - basically the
hardware is time-sliced.

WINE/WINE-X/Twin etc run Windows applications without Windows being
loaded by providing the Windows APIs as dynamic libraries.  Some work,
some don't.  Mostly they seem to be trying to get all of MS Office
running which is a complete waste.

One approach is to continue running existing Windows machines (ie no
more updates) while replacing Office with StarOffice/OpenOffice on
Windows and using Opera/Netscape/Mozilla plus EMail and putting in
Linux for Database/File/print/Web servers/routers/firewalls etc. So
that it then becomes moot whether the OS is Windows or Linux because
the applications run on both.
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Wiggy wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't mean it isn't being used - but certainly no publicity.

There's publicity here in the US. The 05 Sept edition of the NY Times 
carried the following article. I'm beside myself laughing at the notion 
of Microsoft crying foul & asking for a level playing surface:

   -------------------------------------------------------------

September 5, 2002
An Alternative to Microsoft Gains Support in High Places
By STEVE LOHR

Governments around the world, afraid that Microsoft has become too 
powerful in critical software markets, have begun working to ensure an 
alternative.

More than two dozen countries in Asia, Europe and Latin America, 
including China and Germany, are now encouraging their government 
agencies to use "open source" software ï¿½ developed by communities of 
programmers who distribute the code without charge and donate their 
labor to cooperatively debug, modify and otherwise improve the software.

The best known of these projects is Linux, a computer operating system 
that Microsoft now regards as the leading competitive threat to its 
lucrative Windows franchise in the market for software that runs 
computer servers. The foremost corporate champion of Linux is I.B.M., 
which is working with many governments on Linux projects.

Against this opposition, Microsoft has found itself in the uncommon 
position of campaigning for the even-handed competition of "a level 
playing field." And I.B.M., once the feared monopolist of the era of 
mainframe computers, is casting itself as a force of liberation from 
Microsoft, the monopolist of today.

Microsoft worries that some governments may all but require the use of 
Linux for their powerful servers, which provide data to large networks 
of computer users. For the most part, the battle does not involve the 
kind of software that runs on the typical computer user's desk.

To curb such moves, Microsoft is backing an industry group called the 
Initiative for Software Choice. The group lists 20 members ï¿½ besides the 
chip maker Intel, a close ally, most of them small foreign companies or 
organizations. (Illegally stifling choice, of course, was precisely what 
the federal courts in the long-running antitrust case ruled that 
Microsoft did in the market for personal computer software.)

The motivations and actions by foreign governments vary somewhat, but 
mostly they seem to be trying to ensure competition. That was the stance 
taken by a delegation of Chinese officials involved in developing their 
software industry, who visited the United States last month.

In an interview, Jiang Guangzhi, director of a software development 
center in Shanghai, discussed the progress made in China on various 
Linux projects and emphasized that the government did not want one 
company "to manipulate or dominate the Chinese market." With its entry 
into the World Trade Organization, China is facing increased pressure to 
crack down on software piracy, adding to the appeal of free software 
like Linux, Mr. Jiang said.

His delegation had attended the LinuxWorld conference in San Francisco, 
and met with I.B.M. executives and its Linux experts at the company's 
headquarters in Armonk, N.Y.

Yet Mr. Jiang also spoke glowingly of Microsoft's involvement in China. 
The company set up a research laboratory in Beijing and recently made a 
commitment to invest $700 million in China over the next three years in 
education, training and research, and in investments in local companies.

"We appreciate Microsoft's contributions," Mr. Jiang said.

To Chinese Communist officials, it seems, Linux is a useful tool of 
pragmatic capitalism to pump-prime market competition to China's advantage.

The support of open source software by governments around the world is 
rising. There are currently 66 government proposals, statements and 
studies promoting open source software in 25 countries, according to the 
Initiative for Software Choice. The policy statements and legislative 
proposals mainly encourage the use of open source software in government 
procurement, and nearly all of them have cropped up in the last 18 months.

"It's growing, unfortunately, from our perspective," said Mike Wendy, a 
spokesman for the software initiative, which was founded in May.

The impetus for the international activity was in Europe. A technology 
advisory group to the European Commission issued a report two years ago 
that termed open source software "a great opportunity" for the region 
that could perhaps "change the rules in the information technology 
industry," wresting the lead in software from the United States and 
reducing Europe's reliance on imports.

As open source software, especially Linux, has spread, countries in 
other regions have also come to regard it as both a model of software 
development and perhaps an engine of economic growth. The government 
proposals and projects are efforts to position their nations to exploit 
a promising trend in technology.

Source code is software rendered in a programming language that human 
programmers can read and understand, before it is compiled down to the 
digital 1's and 0's that the machine processes. Software companies, like 
Microsoft, typically guard their source code as a trade secret, and 
certainly do not allow outsiders to modify or redistribute it.

In the open source model, the source code is freely published for all to 
see. Then, interested programmers ï¿½ often all over the world, 
communicating over the Internet ï¿½ work on a project to fix, modify and 
add improvements. These self-selected communities work out their own 
governing arrangements to determine when changes in the code are 
approved or rejected.

The leading open source projects are Apache, the software most used for 
distributing Web pages to desktop computers, and the Linux operating 
system. The kernel, or basic engine, of Linux was initially developed by 
Linus Torvalds, a Finnish programmer who now works in the United States 
ï¿½ though the operating system itself is a result of work from many 
contributors, including Richard M. Stallman, who leads a free software 
project called GNU.

Just how far the open source model can go is uncertain. The projects 
rely on voluntary contributions from programmers who work at 
universities, government laboratories and companies. Money is made in 
the open source environment by supplying technical support, services and 
writing applications that run on top of the open source software.

Linux has certainly gone a long way already. Though there are versions 
of Linux that run on desktop PC's, the real success of Linux has been as 
an operating system on larger data-serving machines, which power 
computer networks in corporations and governments and the Internet.

The big market for computer server software is also crucial to 
Microsoft's future. Although the company controls a huge portion of the 
personal computer operating systems market, to keep growing it must push 
increasingly into the lucrative market for software that runs corporate 
and government data centers. It is there that Microsoft encounters what 
its senior executives have cited as the two most significant competitive 
threats: I.B.M. and free software, notably Linux.

That combination, in Microsoft's view, could be particularly powerful, 
especially if open source software emerges as the most politically 
acceptable technological path.

In Germany, for example, the lower house of Parliament adopted a 
resolution last November declaring that the government should use open 
source software "whenever doing so will reduce costs." The resolution 
also cited as advantages "stability" and "security." Microsoft's Windows 
operating system is often criticized for crashing too often and for 
being susceptible to computer viruses and security breaches.

Then in June, the German government and I.B.M. announced a "far-reaching 
cooperation agreement" to use open source software in national and 
municipal government agencies. "The fact that Linux provides a true 
alternative to the Windows operating system," said Otto Schily, the 
German interior minister, "increases our independence and improves our 
position as a big customer for software."

The German case, I.B.M. says, is part of an emerging pattern. "There's 
not a large government in the world we're not talking to," said Steven 
Solazzo, general manager of I.B.M.'s Linux business.

The Initiative for Software Choice, the Microsoft-supported group, said 
it has nothing against open source software as such, but that a declared 
policy favoring one development model is a bias ï¿½ a competition based on 
prejudice instead of the merits of the products.

"All we're looking for is a level playing field competitively," said 
Peter Houston, a senior strategy executive in Microsoft's Windows group.

As open source software moves out of its incubator of a comparatively 
small community of devoted software developers and into the commercial 
mainstream, customers ï¿½ in governments and corporations ï¿½ will 
increasingly see its limitations, Mr. Houston said. Windows, he said, 
has a wide range of tools and technical abilities that Linux does not 
have in a "comprehensive, integrated, easy-to-use" package.

By contrast, Mr. Houston said, I.B.M. is mainly trying to convert its 
weakness in the operating-system market to its advantage by making money 
supplying the software ï¿½ the ingredients that an operating system like 
Linux lacks ï¿½ and collecting services revenue for putting it all together.

"I.B.M. is just trying to move the value up the chain from the operating 
system," Mr. Houston said.

In the end, market competition should determine whether Microsoft or 
Linux gains the upper hand.

Copyright The New York Times Company | Permissions | Privacy Policy

http://www.nytimes.com/2002/09/05/technology/05CODE.html?todaysheadlines=&pagewanted=print&position=top

The NYTimes site requires registration, which is free.
```

- **References:** `<3D76599B.2000109@sympatico.ca> <3d766dcc_6@Usenet.com> <3D767706.6020400@sympatico.ca> <y1wd9.53309$0u4.891023@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D76A6CE.9000508@btopenworld.com> <3D76A855.81ED8F22@shaw.ca> <cSYd9.72$rU2.11842@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```


Liam Devlin wrote:

>
> There's publicity here in the US. The 05 Sept edition of the NY Times
> carried the following article. I'm beside myself laughing at the notion
> of Microsoft crying foul & asking for a level playing surface:
>

<snip>

> Copyright The New York Times Company | Permissions | Privacy Policy
>
> http://www.nytimes.com/2002/09/05/technology/05CODE.html?todaysheadlines=&pagewanted=print&position=top
>
> The NYTimes site requires registration, which is free.

A good extract Liam, very informative. I picked up on a paraphrased version in the local paper about San
Francisco, with strong support from IBM, HP/Compaq and Sun, (I believe) supporting the Linux movement.
Microsoft even had a booth tucked away in the background.

In principle, "Open Source" or which other fancy name you choose to use, just has to be right. Computing at
the moment is rather like had Henry Ford produced his first car with a lock on the hood so that you
couldn't get a look at the 'innards'. I know some of the 'grey hairs' here started out with machine code
then moved on to Assembler - but I'd reckon that for in excess of 80% here, the 'guts' of programming are
still a mystery, content to let a COBOL compiler figure out how to address the machine.

The article did pick up on one of the flaws of Open Source - and not unkindly - as we see in COBOL, a
somewhat leisurely pace to produce the final goods. To date our own COBOL enthusiasts, using C as the
primary tool (?), seem to be working out COBOL 85 plus additional but not critical, additions from the
draft COBOL 200X. So far I don't recall seeing that any have addressed the preprocessor needs of DBs and
SQL, GUI interface and certainly not OO - which is where all the other languages have headed. Haven't
checked - but I wouldn't be surprised, if in paying lip service to the draft Standard, they have also taken
into account those universally used modules - Report Writer and Screen Section !

In their wildest dreams I don't see any of our COBOL Open Source being used on mainframes. Which leads to
the question, to whom are they trying to market, with compilers that really only satisfy a DOS text market.

The other point is they are 'enthusiastic amateurs', not meant in a derogatory sense. There are many who
are probably just as, if not more, proficient than their counterparts working say for Fujitsu or Miicro
Focus. However, the latter are the coming together of technicians into one group, with a plan and the end
objective being corporate profit. Contrast that with the 'democratic' process of Open Source, where there
may be a 'leader', but with a team of very 'individual' people, and as yet, I don't see any serious target
dates being set for completion - they could well be ready when J4 release the next, say COBOL 2010 - by
which time they will have to start all over again ! (I'm assuming COBOL 200X happens before COBOL 2010 -
but who knows ?).

Let me touch on something else. (Donald, in essence this is what I was going to query with you). Our COBOL
Open Source projects seem to be very much dependent upon C or C++ to do the 'virtual machine' thinggy. I
read criticisms of Java 'slowness' due to VM interpretation, similarly the same applies to something like
Smalltalk SQUEAK which is a freebie. (You can download a demo and see what it does in a Smalltalk GUI
environment). It is 'slow' when you look at the demo, and other than being an interesting model. I don't
think it is used by anybody So far as I am aware the 'major' Smalltalk is a commercial version, with its
own site - comp.lang.dolphin. Bill you'll love this - they never appear to prefix anything with "OT" - pure
Smalltalk issues.

Bill indicated sometime back that Net Express is some 80-90% written in COBOL - and a fair bet it uses OO
techniques which M/F have had since about '93 when OO was first mooted. Which leads me to ask - what is the
other 10-20% written in - and prompts the question what syntax is missing from COBOL to achieve that.
(Surely the objective of all our COBOL syntax is to give us in 'plain English' what can be translated into
machine instructions).

What is the critical problem with creating a COBOL VM that translates COBOL syntax into machine
instructions - and in turn generates ints or gnts and EXEs ? Anything which removes the mystique from
computing has to have an world wide economic value. You are no longer constrained to vendors' packages,
(and runtimes of course <G>), and the Open Source concept allows innovators to introduce new techniques
much more quickly.

Just the ramblings of somebody who is much more interested in design rather than programming. OK, so Henry
Ford didn't introduce his car with a lock on the hood. But this particular rocket scientist only opens that
hood to reluctantly feed the beast an additional gulp of wind washer fluid as necessary <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Linux (was Re: test)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T11:58:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aln81l$dqh$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3D76599B.2000109@sympatico.ca> <3d766dcc_6@Usenet.com> <3D767706.6020400@sympatico.ca> <y1wd9.53309$0u4.891023@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D76A6CE.9000508@btopenworld.com> <3D76A855.81ED8F22@shaw.ca> <cSYd9.72$rU2.11842@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D78F42C.271F0729@shaw.ca>`

```
Hi Jimmy,

Just to answer your point:

<snip>
Bill indicated sometime back that Net Express is some 80-90% written in COBOL - and a fair bet it uses OO techniques which M/F have had since about '93 when OO was first mooted. Which leads me to ask - what is the other 10-20% written in - and prompts the question what syntax is missing from COBOL to achieve that. (Surely the objective of all our COBOL syntax is to give us in 'plain English' what can be translated into machine instructions). 
</snip>

COBOL, as you know, is the best language for business rules processing and because of it's flexibility can do a who lot more besides. The 10% to 20% of the rest is functionality that is best served by some other language like C or C++ which have nothing at all to do with business rules but realy low-level stuff. MF use the best tools for the job. In most cases, yes, that is COBOL.

Nothing is "missing" from COBOL. As COmmon Business Oriented Language - it's the best. Micro Focus produce the best of the best.

If you want to write and operating system, or interact with it at a very low level, use something that was designed for the task. As you are originally from Blighty, you will have heared the phrase "Horses for Courses"

Regards,

Paul
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3D78F42C.271F0729@shaw.ca...
    
  Liam Devlin wrote: 

     
    There's publicity here in the US. The 05 Sept edition of the NY Times 
    carried the following article. I'm beside myself laughing at the notion 
    of Microsoft crying foul & asking for a level playing surface: 
     
  <snip> 
    Copyright The New York Times Company | Permissions | Privacy Policy 
    http://www.nytimes.com/2002/09/05/technology/05CODE.html?todaysheadlines=&pagewanted=print&position=top 

    The NYTimes site requires registration, which is free.

  A good extract Liam, very informative. I picked up on a paraphrased version in the local paper about San Francisco, with strong support from IBM, HP/Compaq and Sun, (I believe) supporting the Linux movement. Microsoft even had a booth tucked away in the background. 
  In principle, "Open Source" or which other fancy name you choose to use, just has to be right. Computing at the moment is rather like had Henry Ford produced his first car with a lock on the hood so that you couldn't get a look at the 'innards'. I know some of the 'grey hairs' here started out with machine code then moved on to Assembler - but I'd reckon that for in excess of 80% here, the 'guts' of programming are still a mystery, content to let a COBOL compiler figure out how to address the machine. 

  The article did pick up on one of the flaws of Open Source - and not unkindly - as we see in COBOL, a somewhat leisurely pace to produce the final goods. To date our own COBOL enthusiasts, using C as the primary tool (?), seem to be working out COBOL 85 plus additional but not critical, additions from the draft COBOL 200X. So far I don't recall seeing that any have addressed the preprocessor needs of DBs and SQL, GUI interface and certainly not OO - which is where all the other languages have headed. Haven't checked - but I wouldn't be surprised, if in paying lip service to the draft Standard, they have also taken into account those universally used modules - Report Writer and Screen Section ! 

  In their wildest dreams I don't see any of our COBOL Open Source being used on mainframes. Which leads to the question, to whom are they trying to market, with compilers that really only satisfy a DOS text market. 

  The other point is they are 'enthusiastic amateurs', not meant in a derogatory sense. There are many who are probably just as, if not more, proficient than their counterparts working say for Fujitsu or Miicro Focus. However, the latter are the coming together of technicians into one group, with a plan and the end objective being corporate profit. Contrast that with the 'democratic' process of Open Source, where there may be a 'leader', but with a team of very 'individual' people, and as yet, I don't see any serious target dates being set for completion - they could well be ready when J4 release the next, say COBOL 2010 - by which time they will have to start all over again ! (I'm assuming COBOL 200X happens before COBOL 2010 - but who knows ?). 

  Let me touch on something else. (Donald, in essence this is what I was going to query with you). Our COBOL Open Source projects seem to be very much dependent upon C or C++ to do the 'virtual machine' thinggy. I read criticisms of Java 'slowness' due to VM interpretation, similarly the same applies to something like Smalltalk SQUEAK which is a freebie. (You can download a demo and see what it does in a Smalltalk GUI environment). It is 'slow' when you look at the demo, and other than being an interesting model. I don't think it is used by anybody So far as I am aware the 'major' Smalltalk is a commercial version, with its own site - comp.lang.dolphin. Bill you'll love this - they never appear to prefix anything with "OT" - pure Smalltalk issues. 

  Bill indicated sometime back that Net Express is some 80-90% written in COBOL - and a fair bet it uses OO techniques which M/F have had since about '93 when OO was first mooted. Which leads me to ask - what is the other 10-20% written in - and prompts the question what syntax is missing from COBOL to achieve that. (Surely the objective of all our COBOL syntax is to give us in 'plain English' what can be translated into machine instructions). 

  What is the critical problem with creating a COBOL VM that translates COBOL syntax into machine instructions - and in turn generates ints or gnts and EXEs ? Anything which removes the mystique from computing has to have an world wide economic value. You are no longer constrained to vendors' packages, (and runtimes of course <G>), and the Open Source concept allows innovators to introduce new techniques much more quickly. 

  Just the ramblings of somebody who is much more interested in design rather than programming. OK, so Henry Ford didn't introduce his car with a lock on the hood. But this particular rocket scientist only opens that hood to reluctantly feed the beast an additional gulp of wind washer fluid as necessary <G> 

  Jimmy
```

<a id="t-2915aec14c"></a>
## Thread: Handling RETURN-CODE from C — 2 msgs

### Handling RETURN-CODE from C

- **From:** "Serge Lacombe" <serge.lacombe@umontreal.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T15:12:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MvKd9.2019$016.249289@charlie.risq.qc.ca>`

```
Hi,

I'm using MicroFocus Cobol under HP-UX 11.

I'm calling a cobol subprogram (using cobcall) from a C program. How can i
verify from the C program the RETURN-CODE register set in the cobol
subprogram?

Thanks.

serge.lacombe@umontreal.ca
```

#### ↳ Re: Handling RETURN-CODE from C

- **From:** "Bill Yarnall" <Bill.Yarnall@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-05T10:02:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al82ub$p7u$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<MvKd9.2019$016.249289@charlie.risq.qc.ca>`

```
You do not specify which Micro Focus COBOL product you are using,  Server
Express or Object COBOL Developers Suite

In the docs for Micro FOcus Object COBOL Developers suite the cobcall()
function is documented as follows:

long cobcall(name, argc, argv)

indicating that cobcall() returns a long integer

The Server Express docs use Micro Focus data prototypes to document the same
function

cobrtncode_t cobcall (const cobchar_t *name, int argc,  cobchar_t **argv);

The definiution for cobrtncode_t can be found in the
$COBDIR/include/cobcall.h


"Serge Lacombe" <serge.lacombe@umontreal.ca> wrote in message
news:MvKd9.2019$016.249289@charlie.risq.qc.ca...

>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: additional cost for 1099 contracting

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T14:39:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<po2f9.528150$m91.21149448@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20020905180631.00994.00001874@mb-ms.aol.com>`

```

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020905180631.00994.00001874@mb-ms.aol.com...
> What is the additional cost for "1099" contracting?
> There is an additional cost for the employer's portion of FICA (?)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are there any differences in liability exposure?

As an employee, if you screw up, they can fire you.

As a contractor, if you screw up, they own your children.

For $100 you can incorporate in Delaware and no 1099.

Private health insurance is astronomical compared to a group plan. Find a
group (Rotarians, Big Game Hunters, Private Pilots, Democrats,
People-Who-Live-In-Ticky-Tacky Houses, etc.).

The company for whom you contract saves from 15-50% over comparable salaries
(as an employee, if you get $10K, it costs the company upwards of $13K to
keep you on the payroll). Don't be afraid to ask for more than a comparable
salary.
```

```
And I can't believe that you wrote that <G> - you trying to tell me the POWERCOBOL you use
is a freebie ????

Frankly on this one I jumped in before somebody said, once again,  use V3 (?) accompanying
Thane's book. It is deceitful. Eventually to do serious work he has to upgrade AT A PRICE.
That's assuming he doesn't want to take a five minute look at COBOL and wants to program
seriously in it.

BTW - how's this for 'neat' marketing. Let's assume approx prices $CDN. Original compiler
$3,600 and then you go for maintenance $900 pa. The latter gives you Fix packs, which cure
boo-boos and adds new features, like Java interoperability or ESQL Assistant. After four
years - you've bought a new compiler ! Not bad marketing ! How does F/J stack up against
that ?.

BTW2 - as you know I'm using ESQL Assistant which is a refined version of your DECLGEN.
Works like a dream and got rid of my NULL problems <G>.

BTW3 - Fortress COBOL ain't doing too bad with F/J trying to seduce some 8,000 (?) Indians
(East). Now if M/F could get the Peoples' Republic interested ......?

Jimmy
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

```
"ricky" <rveach@comcast.net> wrote

> based programs?

go to 'student' and register for for download.  PowerCobol 3.0 is
listed along with Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 on that page, but I don't know if
the free version includes PowerCobol.

they work.
```

```
"ricky" <rveach@comcast.net> wrote in message news:<iw7e9.480721$2p2.19029157@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> Are there any good, free Cobol compilers that has a GUI for making window
> based programs?

Yes! (Despite the response from Jimmy, who apparently slipped a
cog...<G>)

You can get Fujitsu powerCOBOL from http://www.adtools.com

I started COBOL development for Windows using MF DCOBOL and DIALOG but
switched to PowerCOBOL and have used it ever since.

It is an IDEAL COBOL tool for GUI deveopment.

You build your GUIs using drag and drop, specify your event processing
in COBOL, your main processing in COBOL (if any) and then compile to
either .EXE or .DLL. It is simple, quick, and fun. You do not need a
lot of knowledge of the Windows environment but if you have such
knowledge you CAN use it, with API calls etc. all part of the standard
compilation (no need to address separate libraries (you simply drop
them on your project)and grapple with the compiler environment.)

Highly recommended.

Pete.
```

```
"ricky" <rveach@comcast.net> wrote 

> Yeah, I used Fujitsu powerCOBOL and liked it alot but I can't send the
> programs I made outside my comp :(. Thats why I'm not using it.

It may seem strange to you, but Fujitsu is based on this business
model where customers send them money.  They find that this helps them
pay some of their bills, such as employee's paychecks.

I suggest that you try a company that uses a different model, such as
warez.  This is where you get a copy of MS Visual Studio and use it as
you wish for free.  Just be sure to send your name and address to the
BSA to confirm that you are doing this.
```

<a id="t-84f0dcea8d"></a>
## Thread: How to interrupt/terminate(cntl-c) an executing program? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to interrupt/terminate(cntl-c) an executing program?

- **From:** "Garry D." <garry_dalgarno@telus.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-06T23:23:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GNae9.274$_J3.40331@news0.telusplanet.net>`

```
Fujitsu 6.1
Windows2000

Periodically, we want to stop one of our programs, mid execution! (Executed
from a CMD window)

It's a fairly simple program that won't respond, when we want to CNTL-C it
and terminate/interrupt/stop it's execution.

This program doesn't require the user to key any data into the systems, so
no regular input is required.

Unfortunately, it would appear the program isn't listening for any input
whatsoever, even a user request to stop executing.  We must CNTL-ALT-DEL and
end the task... ouch.

I've scanned the Fujitsu PDF's (and online reference) for interrupt, break,
and CNTL-C... can't spot anything that would help?

Has anybody ran into this problem?
```

#### ↳ Re: How to interrupt/terminate(cntl-c) an executing program?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-07T05:59:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qBge9.15425$jG2.1129320@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<GNae9.274$_J3.40331@news0.telusplanet.net>`

```

"Garry D." <garry_dalgarno@telus.net> wrote in message
news:GNae9.274$_J3.40331@news0.telusplanet.net...
> Fujitsu 6.1
> Windows2000
>
> Periodically, we want to stop one of our programs, mid execution!
(Executed
> from a CMD window)
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> whatsoever, even a user request to stop executing.  We must CNTL-ALT-DEL
and
> end the task... ouch.
>
> I've scanned the Fujitsu PDF's (and online reference) for interrupt,
break,
> and CNTL-C... can't spot anything that would help?
>
> Has anybody ran into this problem?
>

    With Microfocus, I found that this can happen if the program does not
make
any system calls.  Try putting something in.  For example, check the
keyboard
status, accept time, something of that sort.

    Even disk access can be enough.  Be sure to limit the frequency of
whatever you do,
since even checking the system date or time can be slower than you might
think.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to interrupt/terminate(cntl-c) an executing program?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-09-08T08:18:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D7AF9BD.C8846731@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<GNae9.274$_J3.40331@news0.telusplanet.net> <qBge9.15425$jG2.1129320@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> 
>     With Microfocus, I found that this can happen if the program does not
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> think.

That, of course, ensures that the program tries to use and waste the
maximum amount of processor time doing something that would usually not
use any.  It was not an acceptable mechanism when single-tasking DOS was
used and certainly isn't with multi-tasking systems.  Windows sometimes
struggles when programs are being nice.

There are 'dispatch' calls, even under DOS, that release the current
time slice.  So even if the program is just cycling waiting for a
keystroke it can do so minimising the CPU time used by dispatching.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to interrupt/terminate(cntl-c) an executing program?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-07T12:42:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gvme9.230279$On.9058867@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<GNae9.274$_J3.40331@news0.telusplanet.net>`

```

"Garry D." <garry_dalgarno@telus.net> wrote in message
news:GNae9.274$_J3.40331@news0.telusplanet.net...
> Fujitsu 6.1
> Windows2000
>
> Periodically, we want to stop one of our programs, mid execution!
(Executed
> from a CMD window)
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> whatsoever, even a user request to stop executing.  We must CNTL-ALT-DEL
and
> end the task... ouch.
>
> I've scanned the Fujitsu PDF's (and online reference) for interrupt,
break,
> and CNTL-C... can't spot anything that would help?
>
> Has anybody ran into this problem?

Here's how we interrupt a running program in PowerCOBOL:

1. Add a "Pause" button to the form. It's click event has one instruction:
    MOVE "Y" TO PAUSE-JOB

2. In the processing code, we have the following:

    (every 100 records)
   INVOKE POW-SELF 'THRUEVENTS'
   IF PAUSE-JOB = "Y"
      ask user if he wants to abandon the job
      etc

The trick is the THRUEVENTS method (q.v.). This processes the pending
Windows message queue.

In a strict COBOL-only program, you'll probably have to read the keyboard
yourself at regular intervals (see CBL_GET_KBD_STATUS and
CBL_READ_KBD_CHAR).
```

#### ↳ Re: unstring

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-07T19:49:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BLse9.7239$yt3.3572780@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<a2ddbcbb.0209070834.6fdaa5f3@posting.google.com>`

```
"e-milia" <emilia_koleva@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:a2ddbcbb.0209070834.6fdaa5f3@posting.google.com...
> I am using the UNSRTING operator to transform *.xls data to COBOL data &
the problem
> occurs by compiling with Acu85:unstring source may not be reference
> modified!I don't know what that means & what schould I do.

I do not use that compiler, but it sounds like you are trying  to

  UNSTRING  dataname(n:m)
       delimited by comma
       into ....


.. and the compiler is telling you that "dataname (n:m)" is not allowed, and
you must use something like..

   move dataname(n:m) to ws-dataname
   unstring ws-dataname     <<< no reference modification
     delimited by comma
     into ...


MCM
```

## Thread: ODBC and Cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ODBC and Cobol

- **From:** "Adi" <w_kusch@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2002-09-08T00:00:58+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aldt0f$il7$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hello!
I am a newbie ;). I try to coonect with a MS-Access-DB via COBOL (NetCobol,
ConsoleApplication), but I get a error massage:
 *** Error: 'Fujitsu.COBOL.COBOLRuntimeException' in
fujitsu.cobol.runtime.programcontrol.netmodule.
        JMP0371I-U [PID:00000da0 TID:00000f24]  ENVIRONMENT INFORMATION FILE
ERROR TO PERFORM SQL. '@ODBC_Inf'.         PGM=CobolTestApp.Program1***

My code:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
.....
EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
01 SQLSTATE PIC X(5).
EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
EXEC SQL CONNECT TO 'WEBGINE' END-EXEC.
EXEC SQL DISCONNECT ALL END-EXEC
....


In file "cobol85.cbr":

[CobolTestApp.Program1]
@ODBC_Inf=C:\.............\Cobol\CobolTestApp\OdbcInfo.inf





In file "OdbcInfo.inf":

[SERVER LIST]
WEBGINE=WEBGINE
[WEBGINE]
@SQL_DATASRC=OfficeControl
@SQL_USERID=
@SQL_PASSWORD=
@SQL_ACCESS_MODE=READ_ONLY
@SQL_COMMIT_MODE=MANUAL
@SQL_CONCURRENCY=READ_ONLY
@SQL_ODBC_CURSORS=USE_DRIVER
@SQL_QUERY_TIMEOUT=0


How I can create a connection without an error?
Many thanks .
Regards,
Adi
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC and Cobol

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T13:38:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209091238.4892ffd2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<aldt0f$il7$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
"Adi" <w_kusch@t-online.de> wrote 

> In file "cobol85.cbr":
> 
> [CobolTestApp.Program1]
> @ODBC_Inf=C:\.............\Cobol\CobolTestApp\OdbcInfo.inf

I found that it needed this to be in the environment rather than in
the run time settings file:

SET @ODBC_INF=C:\...\ODBCInfo.inf
run program

You may need to add this to autoexec.bat to reserve environment space.
 I usually have a space holder in autoexec.bat that does:

SET ZZZ1=ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZ
SET ZZZ2=ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZ ..

Then later do: 

SET ZZZ1=
SET ZZZ2=

which then leaves unused and reusable space for new settings.

Also, use program SQLODBCS to set the inf file, especially an encoded
password.
```

### OT - Re: Presidential eligibility.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-09-08T20:09:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D7BB062.108A466A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <anfa9.5422$2E6.2196968@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <akddqe$7ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <yQra9.4995$yt3.2105167@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <akdn8r$d6v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ake0es$kjg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ake33b$jvs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jVza9.5996$p%3.454322@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0208271140.1539fdf7@posting.google.com> <BvRa9.78343$%v4.3258660@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D6BEEA6.A0532820@shaw.ca> <HfTa9.79357$%v4.3289441@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <OLTa9.7253$p%3.557213@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <nUUa9.80426$%v4.3335466@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ZLwPXTArZMb9Ew2g@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Rb5b9.32524$Hd4.7428265@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <f2U+nSAFCib9Ew0c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <sftb9.93913$%v4.5162602@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Vwy85vAja3b9EwkX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3D7BB451.4259@netlink.com.au>`

```


Peter Lawrence wrote:

> > >You're correct. Unlike what has been stated in these threads, the U.S.
> > >does not like to go to war. But if someone even accidentally knocks
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> very definitely not "saving".

One of those imponderables when you try to re-cast how history might have turned
out.

In another message Jerry picked up on an article that suggested Chamberlain's
1938 appeasement gave Britain breathing space to build up the RAF. Out in a
political wilderness, Churchill was sounding dire warnings about "NAH-zism" back
in 1935 and was well acquainted with the lack of readiness of the RAF through
friends in the Air Ministry and Professor Lindemann. The UK government had
exactly the same information.

The collapse of France, Belgium, Netherlands etc. was a pushover in 1940. Mistake
#1 - halting the Panzers when the depleted BEF was encircled at Dunkirk and
allowing the British and allies to escape to Britain.

Mistake # 2 - the RAF was exhausted by the end of the Battle of Britain, both
lack of aircraft and perhaps more importantly, trained fighter pilots. On a whim,
Luftwaffe attacks were directed at bombing London, and others, rather than
knocking the RAF out of the game.

Mistake # 3 - Hitler had to be a poor student of history. By attacking the USSR
he allowed himself to get between the vice of the British in the West and the
Russians in the East - exactly what Napoleon did, with the same results.

Remember there never were any sea battles fought in either the English Channel or
the North Sea. Any naval actions like the Bismarck or Graf Spe were carried out
in the Atlantic. The Admiralty would never commit to a concentration of ships in
the English Channel, (without superior air coverage, and the U-boat threat), as
evidenced by the small naval support at Dieppe. There was only a concentration of
big ships, given VAST air superiority on D-Day June 4, 1944.

(*** Dieppe : Canada, prior to 9/11 anniversary just coming up, has just
remembered the 60th anniversary of Wednesday August 19, 1942 - in some six hours,
our greatest loss of troops in WW2).

Given that scenario, the Nazis COULD and probably WOULD have won, had they
invaded England (Operation Sea Lion), shortly after Dunkirk. The shortest route
to conquering Britain had to be roughly Calais across the Channel to English
South East coast. Once the threat was known, the RN would have dispatched
cruisers and battleships from Plymouth and Portsmouth - but too late to be
effective - and sitting ducks for both U-boat and aerial attacks. With Britain
conquered then the Nazis could have dwelt in some depth with what they wanted to
do about the Soviets. (That's accepting the fact that their war machine was
dependent upon Eastern European oil supplies).

The USA was providing lend-lease plus a major food supplier for UK. Had Britain
been defeated, and one can assume a grab for British colonies in the Far East by
Japan, and possibly looking enviously towards you folks <G>, would the US have
been provoked or even felt a need to jump in ?

I still contend the US didn't 'save any butts'. They were in the game for their
own interests - and don't knock them for that.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <anfa9.5422$2E6.2196968@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <akddqe$7ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <yQra9.4995$yt3.2105167@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <akdn8r$d6v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ake0es$kjg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ake33b$jvs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jVza9.5996$p%3.454322@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0208271140.1539fdf7@posting.google.com> <BvRa9.78343$%v4.3258660@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D6BEEA6.A0532820@shaw.ca> <HfTa9.79357$%v4.3289441@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <OLTa9.7253$p%3.557213@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <nUUa9.80426$%v4.3335466@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ZLwPXTArZMb9Ew2g@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Rb5b9.32524$Hd4.7428265@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <f2U+nSAFCib9Ew0c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <sftb9.93913$%v4.5162602@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Vwy85vAja3b9EwkX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3D7BB451.4259@netlink.com.au> <3D7BB062.108A466A@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Peter Lawrence wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> allowing the British and allies to escape to Britain.

Well, yes, with hindsight; only, at that point the Germans themselves were 
almost out of options (since they had actually made an all or nothing 
commitment, and it was no pushover when you bear that in mind). They didn't 
have very much to divert towards Dunkirk anyway.

> 
> Mistake # 2 - the RAF was exhausted by the end of the Battle of Britain, both
> lack of aircraft and perhaps more importantly, trained fighter pilots. On a whim,
> Luftwaffe attacks were directed at bombing London, and others, rather than
> knocking the RAF out of the game.

Not a whim - another understandable mistake, once you bear in mind one 
prevalent error in prewar thinking. The idea they switched to was knocking 
out the morale of the population, not attrition of the military structure 
(Trenchard think).

> 
> Mistake # 3 - Hitler had to be a poor student of history. By attacking the USSR
> he allowed himself to get between the vice of the British in the West and the
> Russians in the East - exactly what Napoleon did, with the same results.

Again, few options. It was already too late by then (striking at the USSR 
was like Prussia taking the initiative in the Seven Years War - it was 
fairly certain that he didn't so much start the eastern front as strike 
first).

> 
> Remember there never were any sea battles fought in either the English Channel or
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> dependent upon Eastern European oil supplies).

However, none of this touches on US contributions, any "saving". For that to 
be meaningful, they would have had to join in that early - not once Hitler 
was getting sucked in in the east.

> 
> The USA was providing lend-lease

Not for nothing, and not of adequate quality; they were giving things that 
were nearing the end of their shelf lives, and getting strategic resources 
like naval bases. All of which adds up to attrition of the former Britain, 
not saving it.

 plus a major food supplier for UK. Had Britain
> been defeated, and one can assume a grab for British colonies in the Far East by
> Japan,

More likely the Finlandisation approach they did in Siam and Vichy French 
Indochina, at least to begin with.

 and possibly looking enviously towards you folks <G>, would the US have
> been provoked or even felt a need to jump in ?
> 
> I still contend the US didn't 'save any butts'. They were in the game for their
> own interests - and don't knock them for that.

Not doing it. Only, we should recognise them for what they are, and for what 
they do. Long spoons, and that. PML.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Re: Presidential eligibility.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-08T23:42:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209082242.ef3ede8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <OLTa9.7253$p%3.557213@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <nUUa9.80426$%v4.3335466@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ZLwPXTArZMb9Ew2g@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Rb5b9.32524$Hd4.7428265@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <f2U+nSAFCib9Ew0c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <sftb9.93913$%v4.5162602@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Vwy85vAja3b9EwkX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3D7BB451.4259@netlink.com.au> <3D7BB062.108A466A@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> In another message Jerry picked up on an article that suggested Chamberlain's
> 1938 appeasement gave ...

These are not new ideas, wee A.J.P.Taylor 'Origins of 2ndWW'.  It has
been argued about for 40 years.

> The collapse of France, Belgium, Netherlands etc. was a pushover in 1940. Mistake
> #1 - halting the Panzers when the depleted BEF was encircled at Dunkirk and
> allowing the British and allies to escape to Britain.

They had no reason to suspect that the BEF could escape.  They
expected a strongly defended stand and did not have sufficient forces
to attack such a large force.  A few days were required for the
Panzers to assemble sufficient men and arms into the area - they were
committed elsewhere already, and it was expected that a seige would
get results.
 
> Mistake # 2 - the RAF was exhausted by the end of the Battle of Britain, both
> lack of aircraft and perhaps more importantly, trained fighter pilots. 

Not true. The RAF was less exhausted than the Germans.  In fact the
RAF had _more_ aircraft at the end of the BoB then they had at the
beginning.

> On a whim,
> Luftwaffe attacks were directed at bombing London, and others, rather than
> knocking the RAF out of the game.

It wasn't a 'whim', it was a reaction to the carefully contrived raids
on Berlin.  Of course Churchill would never admit to deliberately
manipulating the bombing of London, but it saved the country.

> Mistake # 3 - Hitler had to be a poor student of history. By attacking the USSR
> he allowed himself to get between the vice of the British in the West and the
> Russians in the East - exactly what Napoleon did, with the same results.

The whole point of the war was to recover the land that Germany had
'won' in the first war before being forced to give it back.

> Remember there never were any sea battles fought in either the English Channel or
> the North Sea. Any naval actions like the Bismarck or Graf Spe were carried out
> in the Atlantic. 

June 1940.  Glorious sunk, Scarnhhorst and Geisenau damaged.  In what
way was this not a 'Naval action' ?

> Given that scenario, the Nazis COULD and probably WOULD have won, had they
> invaded England (Operation Sea Lion), shortly after Dunkirk. 

Nonsense.  'Shortly after Dunkirk' they had no air superiority and had
not even established the bases they later used for BoB.  They also had
no transport that could invade at that time.  They never had such
things as landing craft (as used by allies) but managed to modify
ordinary river barges for use, but there were not sufficient of these
until months later (if in fact there ever were enough).

> The shortest route
> to conquering Britain had to be roughly Calais across the Channel to English
> South East coast. 

They couldn't just drive their panzers over the channel.

Any casual glance at the SE Coast opposite Calais shows it to be
cliffs (White Cliffs of Dover).  There are only a few easily defended
access routes in that area.  Without air superiority (which the BoB
proved they didn't ever have) any invasion attempt would have been
easily defeated ...

> Once the threat was known, the RN would have dispatched
> cruisers and battleships from Plymouth and Portsmouth - but too late to be
> effective - and sitting ducks for both U-boat and aerial attacks. 

  ... without need for any RN involvement beyond the existing channel
based forces.

There never was any opportunity for an invasion.  The Germans had no
capability of transporting and supplying an invasion force at any
time, by the time that they had built up a supply of river barges it
had been shown that there was no air superiority and the deteriorating
weather would have wrecked any attempt, river barges with tanks on
them needed extraordinary calm seas and a protected harbour to unload.

Even loading and reloading the barges would have been a significant
problem because the required harbours were easily attacked.
```

```
There is a school of thought which believes that the invasion of the
USSR was not related to a desire to destroy the barbarian communists but
simply to acquire dominion over those lands which accommodated the bulk
of the Jewish peoples with a view to implementing as fully as possible
the 'final solution' in Europe before the inevitable demise of the
Nazi's Third Reich.

hierarchy had concluded that the war was essentially already lost and
that the final solution had to be implemented before any Nazi surrender.
```

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

> In article <3D7BB062.108A466A@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
> writes
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alistair Maclean

You might find the following fascinating but horrifying :-

    http://library.byu.edu/~rdh/eurodocs/germ/wanneng.html

Jewish websites make note of the following - At the Wanasee Conference, 20 Jan 1942,
of the 13 participants, 9 had PhDs - so much for early to mid twentieth century
morality !

It's worth noting that amongst the 11 million Jews to be exterminated - the list
includes 330,000 in England and 4,000 in Ireland. Pure conjecture on my part - are
they referring to geographic entities, i.e. GB = England, Wales and Scotland,
whereas Ireland = Ulster and the Republic ?

Doesn't quite sound like the megalomania of a bunch about to give up, thinking they
had lost WW2. Why would they associate Brits with the Final Solution if they were
already in a defeatist frame of mind.

The other factor regarding the date 20 Jan 42 - consider the history timelines :-

- From mid 1940 onwards the European coastline was 'Nazi owned' from Norway, down
through Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Northern France and Vichy Southern France,
down to the borders of Spain. (The latter neutral but Fascist).

- 22 June 1941 they attacked USSR. On December 6 they were 25 miles from Moscow.
December 10 is the generally accepted date for the Soviet counter-offensive which
took the Germans by surprise. By the middle of Jan 1942 a deep wedge had been driven
into the German front, south of Moscow. While December '41 is the start of the
'Reversal',  surely Jan '42 was too early for a knee-jerk reaction by the Wannasee
participants that the game was over.

- 1941 - North Africa - an ebb and flow situation with Erwin Rommel the prime winner
with his Afrika Korps. The situation didn't change dramatically until Montgomery
triggered El Alamein on 22 Oct '42, with of course George Patton making inroads at
the Kesserine Pass, heading eastwards.

The theory is certainly interesting - but rather like Saddam Hussein spitefully
scorched the Kuwaiti oil fields - wouldn't the SS have looked for and coldly minuted
'faster' methods to achieve the Final Solution. I would suggest they would have
adopted a 'speedier' approach, out of pure vindictiveness, if they sensed they were
losing  ?

Jimmy
```

```
> Doesn't quite sound like the megalomania of a bunch about to give up,
thinking they
> had lost WW2. Why would they associate Brits with the Final Solution if
they were
> already in a defeatist frame of mind.
>
> The other factor regarding the date 20 Jan 42 - consider the history
timelines :-
>
> - From mid 1940 onwards the European coastline was 'Nazi owned' from
Norway, down
> through Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Northern France and Vichy Southern
France,
> down to the borders of Spain. (The latter neutral but Fascist).
>
> - 22 June 1941 they attacked USSR. On December 6 they were 25 miles from
Moscow.
> December 10 is the generally accepted date for the Soviet
counter-offensive which
> took the Germans by surprise. By the middle of Jan 1942 a deep wedge had
been driven
> into the German front, south of Moscow. While December '41 is the start of
the
> 'Reversal',  surely Jan '42 was too early for a knee-jerk reaction by the
Wannasee
> participants that the game was over.
>
> - 1941 - North Africa - an ebb and flow situation with Erwin Rommel the
prime winner
> with his Afrika Korps. The situation didn't change dramatically until
Montgomery
> triggered El Alamein on 22 Oct '42, with of course George Patton making
inroads at
> the Kesserine Pass, heading eastwards.

Just a slight correction, but you make these sound as if they occurred at
the same time, but the Kasserine Pass battle was February '43.   Also the
Allied invasion of Western North Africa didn't start untill November '42.

>
> The theory is certainly interesting - but rather like Saddam Hussein
spitefully
> scorched the Kuwaiti oil fields - wouldn't the SS have looked for and
coldly minuted
> 'faster' methods to achieve the Final Solution. I would suggest they would
have
> adopted a 'speedier' approach, out of pure vindictiveness, if they sensed
they were
> losing  ?
>
> Jimmy
>
```

### Installing Micro Focus Cobol

- **From:** "w.c.h.r. zinkhann" <w.zinkhann@hccnet.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T10:01:29+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<alhkj1$do4$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
Hi there,
When installing Micro Focus XM developer version 3.5.09 I get an error 0019
(Install Protection Device) when I try to compile a program. What can I do
about this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Installing Micro Focus Cobol

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T13:58:55+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ali69u$rus$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<alhkj1$do4$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
The "Protection Device" is a dongle which fits into the parallel port of
your PC. If you have a legitimate copy of Micro Focus COBOL then this should
be in your possession.

"w.c.h.r. zinkhann" <w.zinkhann@hccnet.nl> wrote in message
news:alhkj1$do4$1@news.hccnet.nl...

0019
> (Install Protection Device) when I try to compile a program. What can I do
> about this?
>
>
```

```
> Hi there,
> When installing Micro Focus XM developer version 3.5.09 I get an error 0019
> (Install Protection Device) when I try to compile a program. What can I do

licensed copy of the software they will help you. If you don't have a
licensed copy, too bad.
```

<a id="t-91bba197dd"></a>
## Thread: UNIX KORN shell/end of record seems to be carriage control and misreads file — 4 msgs

### UNIX KORN shell/end of record seems to be carriage control and misreads file

- **From:** morant@dnr.state.wi.us (Tom Morano)
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T06:08:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<55066e.0209090508.7a545ee8@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm having a problem reading records from a flat file with UNIX COBOL.
We have just converted to the KORN shell, if that matters.

Say I'm reading a record that is 80 characters long, looking at the
file it looks like a normal record, characters out to byte 80.  When I
read the first record it works fine, reads the 80 characters.  Then I
read the second record, but instead of starting in the first byte of
record 2, it starts with the 81st character of record 1, which seems
to do what a carriage return would do, though it's not visible when I
look at it in a couple of formats.  It also reads characters 1 thru 79
of record 2.   So it's offset by 1 character.
For the third read it reads characters 80 & 81 of record 2, and
characters 1 thru 78 of record 3.  It continues that pattern thru the
rest of the file.

My code worked fine over on VAX alpha server, but this is happening
when running this on UNIX cobol.   Yes, the record lengths match the
FD's and such in the program exactly.

What has worked in some instances is reading it into a SAS program as
80 cahracters, then writing out those 80 characters and an 'x' as a
placeholder in character 81.  Then we adjust the ensuing cobol program
thusly.  The last program we're working on though isn't fooled by
this, and still reads that carriage control character that seems to be
to the right of the last character in the record.

It's a normal read we're doing:
READ MASTER-RECORD INTO TRANSACTION-RECORD-LAYOUT
   AT END
      MOVE 'Y' TO END-OF-MASTER-FILE-SW.

Has anyone else run across this??
```

#### ↳ Re: UNIX KORN shell/end of record seems to be carriage control and misreads file

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T14:57:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ali9ni$sa3$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<55066e.0209090508.7a545ee8@posting.google.com>`

```
The character in position 81 should be a line-feed character. In Micro Focus
cobol you define the record length as 80 characters but with ORGANIZATION
LINE SEQUENTIAL and then the line-feed is interpreted as end of line.If, you
don't specify LINE-SEQUENTIAL, then chucks of 80 bytes are read from the
file, including the markers. Using the ORGANIZATION entry allows you to
specify which way you want to work. It's up to you how to decide which. I
would suggest LINE SEQUENTIAL from what you say.

Incidentally, on M$ environments, the end of line is shown by both
carriage-return and line-feed.

"Tom Morano" <morant@dnr.state.wi.us> wrote in message
news:55066e.0209090508.7a545ee8@posting.google.com...
> I'm having a problem reading records from a flat file with UNIX COBOL.
> We have just converted to the KORN shell, if that matters.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anyone else run across this??
```

#### ↳ Re: UNIX KORN shell/end of record seems to be carriage control and misreads f...

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T20:17:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020909161701.05562.00000684@mb-fc.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<55066e.0209090508.7a545ee8@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <55066e.0209090508.7a545ee8@posting.google.com>,
morant@dnr.state.wi.us (Tom Morano) writes:

>Say I'm reading a record that is 80 characters long, looking at the
>file it looks like a normal record, characters out to byte 80.  When I
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>rest of the file.

This looks suspiciously like a sequential file definition that is reading 
a 'line sequential' file.  One of the 'transitional' things that you have to 
deal with when moving from a Mainframe to a PC based environment.
If you are taking data directly from the original platform the program 
should function as defined, but if you are 'creating' test data on the new
platform in some text editor; you are getting the extra CR character added.
```

#### ↳ Re: UNIX KORN shell/end of record seems to be carriage control and misreads file

- **From:** Doug Robinson <fdr@mytemyke.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T17:43:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2002.09.11.17.43.55.244375.5368@mytemyke.com>`
- **References:** `<55066e.0209090508.7a545ee8@posting.google.com>`

```
On Mon, 09 Sep 2002 09:08:53 -0400, Tom Morano wrote:

> I'm having a problem reading records from a flat file with UNIX COBOL.
> We have just converted to the KORN shell, if that matters.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anyone else run across this??


I don't know what platform you are on, but try an 'apropos hex' and see
what the viewer is for hex.  On my machines of various types I have
hexdump.  From there take a look at the record and see if each record
ends with a c/r + linefeed.  That is a windoze construct and most unix
machines delimit records with just the linefeed.  You wouldn't be copying
files with Samba would you.....just checking.  If so do another apropos
and see the utility that converts wondoze formats to unix and vice versa.
Been there, done that, got the T shirt......fdr
```

<a id="t-223488df2d"></a>
## Thread: Announcement: New Course - Introduction to z/OS UNIX — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Announcement: New Course - Introduction to z/OS UNIX

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-09-09T15:35:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020909113556.00803.00002998@mb-fe.aol.com>`

```
Hi,

I am very pleased to announce a new course, the first of a new curriuculm on
UNIX on the mainframe.

YES! You can run UNIX on the mainframe (of course, you guys already know that).
Why would you want to do that?, some have said. The main reasons are efficiency
and cost savings. You can now run your UNIX apps on the same box that runs your
mission-critical day to day work - this includes your Web-based applications.

Now a single system can run your COBOL, CICS, DB2 applications and your email,
intranet, and Internet applications. One system. (Or multiple systems, for
large shops, but all the same: z/OS mainframes also running UNIX and Web.) Get
rid of the UNIX boxes and roll those apps onto your mainframe.

Mainframes aren't dead at all. In fact, they have been expanded enoromously
over the last 8 years.

This new course is intended for applications programmers. If we don't get
applications out there, visible, it might be another nail in the mainframe
coffin. So take a minute and spread the word in your installation: who in your
shop needs to learn these skills? This is a first step on the path for
extending the COBOL / PL/I / C / Assembler mainframe applications programmers
into the Web and UNIX worlds: trade on the business knowledge these people have
and help them apply that to the corporate Web presence - all on the mainframe.


Anyway, quick overview of the first course:

Introduction to z/OS UNIX - 3 days.

Audience: applications programmers who need to support or develop UNIX
applications on the mainframe; must have good TSO/ISPF skills to start.

What's cool: by the end of the course, each student will have created a small
web site on your mainframe, viewable from browsers that can access your
corporate intranet.

You don't need that expensive WebSphere software for this class. IBM provides a
free basic web server with OS/390 and z/OS (yes, this course can be run on
systems that are OS/390 V2R8 or later, also), and we use that for our labs.

For details, check out our course description at
http://www.trainersfriend.com/u510descr.htm

As usual, you can link from their to the course objectives and / or the
detailed topical outline.

---------------------

More UNIX on the mainframe courses coming. Other cool tricks we will teach
include: sending email from your mainframe; how to have your mainframe contact
a pager or cell phone (set up auto call for abends of production work, or
successful completion of that important job, for example). And more.

---------------------

Kind regards,

-Steve Comstock
The Trainer's Friend, Inc,
http://www.trainersfriend.com
800-993-8716
303-393-8716
```

<a id="t-07f731474c"></a>
## Thread: Reading SMF Type 30 records with COBOL — 1 msgs

### Reading SMF Type 30 records with COBOL

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-10T11:17:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D7E374D.D2A0828A@attglobal.net>`

```
Sathaporn asked me to post SMF Type 30 record descriptions in COBOL.
Here is the basic definition:
The COBOL program contains this statement:
     COPY SMFTYP30.
This COPY member contains the following:
*    THE FOLLOWING DATA ITEMS ARE REFERENCED IN THE COPY MEMBER,
*    BUT CANNOT BE CODED INSIDE IT:
 01  SMF30ACL-LONG.
  05                PIC X(01).
  05 SMF30ACL-LO    PIC X(01).
 01  SMF30ACL-N     REDEFINES
     SMF30ACL-LONG  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
*    THIS IS A DESCRIPTION OF SMF RECORD TYPE 30 WHICH IS
*    SUITABLE FOR USE IN A PROGRAM'S WORKING-STORAGE.
*    IT INCLUDES THE DATA THAT CAN BE ADDRESSED IN THE "FD" AND
*    THE RECORD HEADER THAT COBOL NORMALLY HIDES FROM THE
*    PROGRAM.
 01  SMF-TYPE-30.
*    THE "VARIABLE LENGTH RECORD HEADER" IS INCLUDED IN THIS
*    DESCRIPTION BECAUSE THERE ARE NUMEROUS OFFSETS IN THE
*    RECORD THAT ARE RELATIVE TO THE TRUE START OF THE RECORD,
*    AND IT WILL BE EASIER TO ADDRESS THE DIFFERENT SEGMENTS
*    IF THE OFFSET VALUES DON'T HAVE TO BE MANIPULATED.
  05 SMF-RECORD-HEADER BINARY.
  10 SMF30LEN                   PIC S9(4).
  10 SMF30SEG                   PIC S9(4).
  05 SMF-30-RECORD.
*    NESTED COPY STATEMENT:
     COPY SMF30.
(I have not copied columns 1 - 6.)
COPY member SMF30 has the "fixed" data, with other COPY members nested
within it.
*    HEADER / SELF-DEFINING SECTION:
 06  SMF30HDR.
 10  SMF30FLG                   PIC X(01).
 10  SMF30RTY                   PIC X(01).
  88 SMF-REC-TYPE-30                      VALUE X'1E'.
 10  SMF30TME                   PIC S9(8) BINARY.
 10  SMF30DTE                   PIC S9(7) PACKED-DECIMAL.
 10  SMF30SID                   PIC X(04).
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-S1                       VALUE 'S190'.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-S6                       VALUE 'S690'.
 10  SMF30WID                   PIC X(04).
 10  SMF30STP                   PIC S9(4) BINARY.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-JOB-START                VALUE +1.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-INTERVAL-ACT             VALUE +2.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-INTERVAL-ACT-L           VALUE +3.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-STEP-TOTAL               VALUE +4.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-JOB-TERM                 VALUE +5.
  88 SMF-TYPE-30-SYS-ADDR-SPACE           VALUE +6.
 10  SEGMENT-TRIPLES                      BINARY.
*    THE SEVERAL SEGMENTS WHICH MAY BE PRESENT AFTER THE FIXED
*    PART OF THE RECORD EACH HAVE A "TRIPLE" HERE.
*    THE FIRST FULLWORD IS AN OFFSET FROM THE START OF
*    THE RECORD (INCLUDING THE RECORD CONTROL WORD).
*    THE SECOND HALFWORD IS THE LENGTH OF THE SEGMENT.
*    THE THIRD HALFWORD IS THE NUMBER OF SUCH SEGMENTS.
*    MISSING SEGMENTS WILL HAVE ZERO IN THE "OFFSET", BUT MAY
*    HAVE NON-ZERO VALUES IN THE OTHER FIELDS.
 15  SMF30SOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30SLN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30SON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30IOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30ILN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30ION                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30UOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30ULN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30UON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30TOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30TLN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30TON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30COF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30CLN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30CON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30AOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30ALN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30AON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30ROF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30RLN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30RON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30POF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30PLN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30PON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30OOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30OLN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30OON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30EOF                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30ELN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30EON                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30EOR                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30RVD                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30EOS                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30DRO                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30DRL                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30DRN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30ARO                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30ARL                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30ARN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30OPO                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30OPL                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30OPN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30OPM                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30UDO                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30UDL                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30UDN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30UDS                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30RMO                   PIC S9(9).
 15  SMF30RML                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30RMN                   PIC S9(4).
 15  SMF30RMS                   PIC S9(9).
 06  VARIABLE-SEGMENTS.
 08  SUBSYSTEM-SECTION.
     COPY SMF30S.
 08  IDENTIFICATION-SECTION.
     COPY SMF30I.
 08  I-O-ACTIVITY-SECTION     OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30UON.
     COPY SMF30U.
 08  COMPLETION-SECTION       OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30TON.
     COPY SMF30T.
 08  PROCESSOR-SECTION        OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30CON.
  10 PIC X(76).
 08  ACCOUNTING-SECTION       OCCURS 0 TO 255  DEPENDING SMF30AON.
     COPY SMF30A.
 08  STORAGE-SECTION          OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30RON.
     COPY SMF30R.
 08  PERFORMANCE-SECTION      OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30PON.
     COPY SMF30P.
 08  OPERATOR-SECTION         OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30OON.
     COPY SMF30O.
 08  EXCP-AREA-IN-RECORD      OCCURS 0 TO 255  DEPENDING SMF30EON.
  10 PIC X(22).
 08  APPC-CUMULATIVE-SECTION  OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30ARN.
     COPY SMF30AR.
 08  APPC-SECTION             OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30DRN.
     COPY SMF30D.
 08  OPEN-MVS-SECTION         OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30OPN.
     COPY SMF30OP.
 08  USAGE-DATA-SECTION       OCCURS 0 TO 255  DEPENDING SMF30UDN.
     COPY SMF30UD.
 08  AUTO-RESTART-SECTION     OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30RMN.
     COPY SMF30RM.
Member SMF30A:
*    ACCOUNTING SECTION:
*    SMF30ACL FIELD CONTAINS LENGTH OF SMF30ACT IN 1 BYTE.
*    IT MUST BE MOVED TO "SMF30ACL-LO" BEFORE PROCESSING.
 10  SMF30ACL PIC X(01).
*    "SMF30ACL-N" REDEFINES "SMF30ACL-LONG" AS FOLLOWS:
*                 01  SMF30ACL-LONG.
*                  05                PIC X(01).
*                  05 SMF30ACL-LO    PIC X(01).
*                 01  SMF30ACL-N     REDEFINES
*                     SMF30ACL-LONG  PIC S9(4).
 10  SMF30ACT PIC X(01) OCCURS 1 TO 255 DEPENDING SMF30ACL-N.
Member SMF30AR:
*    APPC MVS CUMULATIVE SECTION:
 10  SMF30CN  PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30CNA PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30SEN PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30DAT           COMPUTATIONAL-2.
 10  SMF30REC PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30DAR           COMPUTATIONAL-2.
 10  SMF30TAC PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30ATR PIC S9(9) BINARY.
Member SMF30C:
*    PROCESSOR ACCOUNTING SECTION:
 10  SMF30PTY PIC S9(4) BINARY.
 10  SMF30TFL PIC X(02).
 10  SMF30CPT PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30CPS PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30ICU PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30ISB PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30JVU PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30IVU PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30JVA PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 10  SMF30IVA PIC S9(9) BINARY.

 10           PIC X(08).

 10  SMF30INP PIC S9(9) BINARY.

 10  SMF30RDT PIC X(01).
```
