 10  SMF30TRR PIC S9(9) BINARY.
SMF30UD:
*    USAGE DATA SECTION:
 10  SMF30UPO PIC X(16).
 10  SMF30UPN PIC X(16).
 10  SMF30UPO PIC X(08).
 10  SMF30UPQ PIC X(08).
 10  SMF30UPI PIC X(08).
 10  SMF30UCT PIC S9(4) BINARY.
 10  SMF30UCS PIC S9(4) BINARY.
 10  SMF30URD PIC X(08).
 10  SMF30UDF PIC X(01).
 10  SMF30UFG PIC X(01).
 10           PIC X(02).
I used an Assembler Language program to read the records, so that I
could get the variable length record "control word".  COBOL would hide
the record control word from the program.

This program was called with the level 01 data-name and a FILE STATUS
item.  It emulates the way COBOL updates file status items, setting
values 35 for an unsuccessful open, 10 for end of file, or 00 for
successful operations.
Here is the Assembler program:
GET      TITLE 'SMF READER ROUTINE CALLED BY "SMF98" COBOL PROGRAM'
SMF98GET CSECT
         PRINT NOGEN
SMF98GET AMODE 31
SMF98GET RMODE 24
GET      CSAVE R12,RETCODE
         L     R6,0(R1)        RECORD AREA ADDRESS
         L     R3,4(R1)        FILE STATUS ADDRESS
         USING FILESTAT,R3
         MVC   FILEST,=C'00'   ASSUME FILE WILL PROCESS WITHOUT ERRORS
         SPACE
         LA    R9,SMFINPUT     DCB ADDRESS
         USING IHADCB,R9
         TM    DCBOFLGS,DCBOFOPN FIRST TIME?
         BO    READREC
         OPEN  SMFINPUT
         TM    DCBOFLGS,DCBOFOPN OPEN SUCCESSFUL?
         BO    READREC
         MVC   FILEST,=C'35'   PASS BACK AN ERROR FILE STATUS
         LA    R15,12
         ST    R15,RETCODE     SET RETURN CODE = ERROR
         B     EXIT
         SPACE
READREC  BAL   R11,READRECS
         SR    R15,R15
         ST    R15,RETCODE     SET RETURN CODE = NORMAL
EXIT     EXIT  GET
         SPACE
READRECS GET   SMFINPUT        LOCATE THE NEXT LOGICAL RECORD
         LR    R8,R1
         LH    R9,0(R8)
         LR    R7,R9
         MVCL  R6,R8           RETURN RECORD TO CALLER'S AREA
         BR    R11
EOF      MVC   FILEST,=C'10'   RETURN END OF FILE STATUS,
         CLOSE SMFINPUT        CLOSE SMF FILE
         FREEPOOL SMFINPUT
         BR    R11
         DS    0D
RETCODE  DC    F'0'
         SPACE
         DS    0D
SMFINPUT DCB   DDNAME=SMFINPUT,DSORG=PS,MACRF=GL,RECFM=VB,EODAD=EOF
         DCBD  DSORG=PS,DEVD=DA
FILESTAT DSECT
FILEST   DS    CL2
         END
The record control word is not required in my program to process the
Type 30 records.  Therefore, the COBOL program could read the records
directly.  If this is done, FUNCTION LENGTH or the special register
LENGTH OF would be useful for determining the current record's actual
length, so that it could be moved from the FD to Working-Storage.  The
record would be moved to the Level 05 field named SMF-30-RECORD.

My program is used to obtain CPU time and EXCP counts, in order to
identify job steps that consume lots of resources.
Here is the relevant code for processing a type 30 record (subtype 4,
which is a "total" record):
 PROCESS-TYPE-30-RECS.
     IF       SMF30RTY                     =  X'1E'
       ADD    1                            TO TYPE30-COUNT
       IF     SMF30STP                     =  4
              ADD      1                   TO T30ST4-COUNT
              PERFORM  CREATE-CPU-TIME-RECORD
       END-IF
     END-IF
     PERFORM  READ-SMF
     .
 CREATE-CPU-TIME-RECORD.
     COMPUTE  CPU-SEGADDR                  =  SMF30COF + 1
     MOVE     SMF-TYPE-30(CPU-SEGADDR:SMF30CLN)
       TO     SEPARATED-PROCESSOR-SECTION
     IF       CPU-MINIMUM                  >= SMF30CPT
              ADD     1                    TO CPUMIN-COUNT
     ELSE
      MOVE    1                            TO SMF-TIME-COUNT
      COMPUTE SMF-EXCP-VALUE(1)            =  SMF30TEP(1)
      COMPUTE SMF-TIME-VALUE(1)            =  SMF30CPT
      MOVE    SMF-TIME-VALUE(1)            TO SMF-MIN-TIME
                                              SMF-MAX-TIME
      MOVE    SMF30SID                     TO SMF-SYSTEMID
      MOVE    SMF30JBN                     TO SMF-JOBNAME
      MOVE    SMF30STN                     TO SMF-STEP-NUMBER
      MOVE    SMF30PGM                     TO SMF-PROGNAME
      COMPUTE SMF-DATE                     =  SMF30DTE + 1900000
      MOVE    SMF30TME                     TO SMF-TIME
      IF      SMF30PSN                     =  SPACE
        MOVE  SPACE                        TO SMF-STEPNAME-IN-PROC
        MOVE  SMF30STM                     TO SMF-STEPNAME
      ELSE
        MOVE  SMF30STM                     TO SMF-STEPNAME-IN-PROC
        MOVE  SMF30PSN                     TO SMF-STEPNAME
      END-IF
      PERFORM WRITE-CPU-TIME-REC
     END-IF
     .
Note the use of Reference Modification to get the portion of the record
that contains the CPU data into a place where it can be addressed.  The
starting address is computed, and the length is taken from one of the
fields in the fixed part of a type 30 record.  The
SEPARATED-PROCESSOR-SECTION looks like this:
 01  SEPARATED-PROCESSOR-SECTION.
     COPY SMF30C.
The field names and their contents are explained in IBM manual "MVS
System Management Facilities", which is in the "MVS Unlicensed
Bookshelf" for OS/390 or z/OS.

Happy coding!
```

---

<a id="t-64a03c1305"></a>
## Thread: JFCBEXAD — 7 msgs

### JFCBEXAD

- **From:** jeffreymcfarland@yahoo.com (Jeff McFarland)
- **Date:** 2002-09-10T13:46:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com>`

```
I am trying to retreive more than 5 volsers from a COBOL program.  I
found an old thread that explained how to use an assembler program to
retreive DSN info, but it is limited to 5 volsers.  We need quite a
few more than that.  I haven't found any reference to using JFCBEXAD
which is supposed to retrieve the extended inofmation.

Thanks for the help.

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: JFCBEXAD

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-10T14:07:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D7E5F0F.977DC7F8@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com>`

```
You could use it as an Address Pointer, to point to the JFCB Extension.

SET ADDRESS OF JFCB-EXTENSION TO JFCBEXAD or something like that.


Jeff McFarland wrote:

> I am trying to retreive more than 5 volsers from a COBOL program.  I
> found an old thread that explained how to use an assembler program to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: JFCBEXAD

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-09-10T23:33:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c25922$269738c0$f593f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com>`

```
The code below shows control block access in general and specifically
access to the JFCB.  You could probaby modify it to do what you want.

ID DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.   COBPTR.
       AUTHOR.       XXXX.
       DATE-WRITTEN. 08/22/01.
       DATE-COMPILED.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    VS COBOL II POINTER FEATURE EXAMPLE                        *
      *    USING MVS CONTROL BLOCKS                                   *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT TEST-FILE ASSIGN TO DD1.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  TEST-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           DATA RECORD IS TEST-REC.
       01  TEST-REC PIC X(80).
      /
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

TEST***01  WS-TEST                            PIC S9(9) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  WS-WORK                             PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT                  PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LEN                              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LENGTH REDEFINES WS-LEN          PIC X(02).
       01  WS-FLAG                             PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-FLAG1 REDEFINES WS-FLAG          PIC X(02).

       01  WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINT.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT REDEFINES WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.

       01  WS-JFCB-POINT.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-JFCB-PTR REDEFINES WS-JFCB-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES  WS-JFCB-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-JFCB-LOW-3               PIC X(3).

       01  WS-POINT.
           05  WS-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-PTR REDEFINES WS-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-LOW-3                    PIC X(3).

       01  WS-SWA-POINT.
           05  WS-SWA-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-SWA-PTR REDEFINES WS-SWA-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-SWA-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-SWA-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-SWA-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-QMAT-POINT.
           05  WS-QMAT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-QMAT-PTR REDEFINES WS-QMAT-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
      /
       01  WS-UCB-POINT.
           05  WS-UCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-UCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-UCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-UCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DEB-POINT.
           05  WS-DEB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DEB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DEB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DEB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DCB-POINT.
           05  WS-DCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASSB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-JSAB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * PUT THE STATEMENTS BELOW IN AN ASSEMBLER PROGRAM TO LOCATE    *
      * THE DISPLACEMENTS OF THE SYMBOLS.  THESE LINES WERE SHIFTED   *
      * TO THE RIGHT TO MAKE COBOL COMMENTS.  SHIFT THEM BACK TO THE  *
      * LEFT SO THAT THE WORD 'TITLE' IS IN COLUMN 10.                *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *              TITLE 'PREFIX STORAGE AREA'                      *
      *              IHAPSA LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'TASK CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *              IKJTCB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'SECONDARY TASK CONTROL BLOCK'             *
      *              IHASTCB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'DATA EXTENT BLOCK'                        *
      *              IEZDEB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'DATA CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     DCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              DCBD  DSORG=PS                                   *
      *              TITLE 'UNIT CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     UCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFUCBOB                                         *
      *              TITLE 'TASK I/O TABLE'                           *
      *     TIOTDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFTIOT1                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB FILE CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *     JFCBDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFJFCBN LIST=YES                                *
      *              TITLE 'COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'               *
      *              CVT DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                           *
      *              TITLE 'SUBSYSTEM COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'     *
      *              IEFJSCVT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB STEP CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *              IEZJSCB                                          *
      *              TITLE 'SWA QUEUE AREA'                           *
      *              IEFQMNGR                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JES COMMUNICATION TABLE'                  *
      *              IEFJESCT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JSAB'                                     *
      *              IAZJSAB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASCB'                                     *
      *              IHAASCB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASSB'                                     *
      *              IHAASSB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'VSAM ACB'                                 *
      *              IFGACB DSECT=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'REMOTE AREAS'                             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      /
       LINKAGE SECTION.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAPSA)  X'21C' = DECIMAL 540                  *
      *                         X'224' = DECIMAL 548                  *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  TCB-PTR                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  ASCB-PTR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  PSA-ASM REDEFINES PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  PSATOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  PSAAOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IKJTCB)  HAS A 32 BYTE PREFIX AREA             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TCB-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DEB-ADDR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TIOT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  JSCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  STCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB-ASM REDEFINES TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  TCBDEB                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TCBTIO                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  TCBJSCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  TCBSTCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(CVT)                                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  CVT-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  JESCT-POINTER                USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT-ASM REDEFINES CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  CVTJESCT                     USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJESCT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCVT-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JESCT-ASM REDEFINES JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCT                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJSCVT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  SSCVT.
           05  SSCVT-EYE-CATCHER  PIC X(04).
           05  NEXT-SSCVT                   USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SUBSYSTEM-NAME     PIC X(04).
       01  SSCVT-ASM  REDEFINES SSCVT.
           05  SSCTID             PIC X(04).
           05  SSCTSCTA                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SSCTSNAM           PIC X(04).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFTIOT1)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TIOT.
           05  JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-PROC           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-STEP           PIC X(08).
       01  TIOT-ASM REDEFINES TIOT.
           05  TIOCSTPN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCPSTN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCSJSTN          PIC X(08).

       01  TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIO-LEN            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
           05  SWA-V-ADDR         PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  UCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  TIOENTRY REDEFINES TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIOELNGH           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  TIOEDDNM           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOEJFCB           PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  TIOEFSRT           PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJFCBN)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JFCB.
           05  DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  VOL-SER            PIC X(06).
       01  JFCB-ASM REDEFINES JFCB.
           05  JFCBDSNM           PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  JFCBVOLS           PIC X(06).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZDEB)  DEB HAS A 36 BYTE PREFIX AREA         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  NEXT-DEB-ADDR      PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  DEB-ASM REDEFINES DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  DEBDEBB            PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEBDCB             PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(DCBD)                                          *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEVICE-TYPE        PIC X.
               88  DISK-3380-X2E  VALUE X'2E'.
               88  DISK-3390-X2F  VALUE X'2F'.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DSORG              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  RECFM              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DDNAME             PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  BLKSIZE            PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  LRECL              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  DCB-ASM REDEFINES DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCBDEVT            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBDSORG           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBRECFM           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DCBDDNAM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  DCBBLKSI           PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  DCBLRECL           PIC S9(4) COMP.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZJSCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  QMPL-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JSCB-ASM REDEFINES JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  JSCBQMPI           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFQMNGR)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMAT-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  QMPL-ASM REDEFINES QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMADD              USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  QMAT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(12).
           05  QMAT-NEXT-POINTER  USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  SWA.
           05  JFCB-ADDR          USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MODGEN(IHASTCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  STCB-ASM REDEFINES STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  STCBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IAZJSAB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSAB.
           05  JSAB-EYE-CATCHER   PIC X(04).
           05  JSAB-NEXT-PTR      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSAB-FLAG1         PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  COMPONENT          PIC X(04).
           05  JOB-ID             PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-NBR            PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  USERID             PIC X(08).
       01  JSAB-ASM REDEFINES JSAB.
           05  JSABID             PIC X(04).
           05  JSABNEXT           USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSABFLG1           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  JSABSCID           PIC X(04).
           05  JSABJBID           PIC X(08).
           05  JSABJBNM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  JSABUSID           PIC X(08).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASCB-POINTER                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASSB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB-ASM REDEFINES ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASCBASSB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASSB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASSB-ASM REDEFINES ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  ASSBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           PERFORM JOB-STEP-NAME.
           PERFORM SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
           PERFORM JFCB-INFO.
           PERFORM DCB-INFO.
           PERFORM JSAB-INFO.

           GOBACK.
      /
       JOB-STEP-NAME.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    JOB NAME AND STEP NAME                                     *
      *      PSA + X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT                              *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'JOB NAME=' JOB-NAME
                   '  JOB PROC=' JOB-PROC
                   '  JOB STEP=' JOB-STEP.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY SUBSYSTEM NAMES FROM SSCVT CHAIN                   *
      *    CVT -> JSECT -> SSCVT                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'00000010' TO WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT-POINTER TO WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT TO CVT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF JESCT TO JESCT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO JESSSCVT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME.
           PERFORM UNTIL NEXT-SSCVT IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO NEXT-SSCVT
               DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME
           END-PERFORM.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       JFCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
      *    FIND DDNAMES AND ASSOCIATED DSNAMES
      *    PSA+X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT -> TIOT SEG -> SWAREQ(SVA) -> JFCB
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           SET WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER TO TIOT-POINTER.
           ADD 24 TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
           PERFORM UNTIL TIO-LEN = LOW-VALUES
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-JFCB-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-SWA-POINT
               MOVE SWA-V-ADDR TO WS-SWA-LOW-3
               PERFORM SWAREQ
               SET ADDRESS OF JFCB TO  WS-POINTER
               DISPLAY 'DDNAME=' DD-NAME
               DISPLAY 'DSNAME=' DS-NAME
               DISPLAY 'VOL=SER=' VOL-SER
               DISPLAY '********************************************'
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-LEN
               MOVE TIO-LEN TO WS-LENGTH(2:1)
               ADD WS-LEN TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT
               SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
           END-PERFORM.
      /
       SWAREQ.
           DIVIDE WS-SWA-PTR BY 16
               GIVING WS-WORK
               REMAINDER WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT.

           IF WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT NOT = 15
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + 16
           ELSE
               MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF JSCB TO JSCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMPL TO QMPL-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-POINTER
               SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-POINTER
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-SWA-PTR <= 65536
                   SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   COMPUTE WS-SWA-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR - 65536
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + WS-QMAT-PTR + 1
               SET ADDRESS OF SWA TO WS-POINTER
               SET WS-POINTER TO JFCB-ADDR
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-PTR + 16
            END-IF.
      /
       DCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY DCB INFORMATION                                    *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> DEB -> DCB                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           OPEN INPUT TEST-FILE.
           MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DEB-POINT.
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET WS-DEB-POINTER TO DEB-ADDR.
           PERFORM UNTIL WS-DEB-POINTER IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF DEB TO WS-DEB-POINTER
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DCB-POINT
               MOVE DCB-ADDR TO WS-DCB-LOW-3
               SET ADDRESS OF DCB TO WS-DCB-POINTER
TEST***********DISPLAY 'DCB-PTR=' WS-DCB-POINTER
               DISPLAY ' DDNAME=' DDNAME
               DISPLAY '  DSORG=' DSORG
               DISPLAY '  RECFM=' RECFM
               DISPLAY 'BLKSIZE=' BLKSIZE
               DISPLAY '  LRECL=' LRECL
               IF DISK-3380-X2E
                   DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3380'
                   DISPLAY '**************************************'
               ELSE IF DISK-3390-X2F
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3390'
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    ELSE
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=????'
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=' DEVICE-TYPE
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    END-IF
               END-IF
TEST***********DIVIDE WS-LEN BY WS-TEST GIVING WS-WORK
               MOVE NEXT-DEB-ADDR TO WS-DEB-LOW-3
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TEST-FILE.
      /
       JSAB-INFO.
           SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO NULL.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY JSAB INFORMATION                                   *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> STCB -> JSAB                         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF STCB TO STCB-POINTER.
           IF JSAB-POINTER OF STCB IS NOT = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
               SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    PSA+X'224' - > ASCB -> ASSB -> JSAB                        *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               MOVE X'00000224' TO WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB-POINTER TO WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB TO ASCB-POINTER
               IF ASSB-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
                   SET ADDRESS OF ASSB TO ASSB-POINTER
                   SET WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER TO ASSB-POINTER
                   IF JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB IS NOT = NULL
                       SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                       SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                   END-IF
               END-IF
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    IF JSAB-FLAG1 > 127 THE JSAB IS INVALID                    *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               CONTINUE
           ELSE
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
               MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-FLAG <= 127 OR
                             WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL OR
                             JSAB-EYE-CATCHER NOT = 'JSAB'
                   SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-NEXT-PTR
                   SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER
                   MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
                   MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               END-PERFORM
           END-IF.

           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
               DISPLAY 'EYE-CATCHER=' JSAB-EYE-CATCHER
               DISPLAY '  COMPONENT=' COMPONENT
               DISPLAY '     JOB ID=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY ' JOB NUMBER=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY '     USERID=' USERID
           ELSE
               DISPLAY 'JSAB INFORMATION NOT FOUND'
           END-IF.


> found an old thread that explained how to use an assembler program to

```

#### ↳ Re: JFCBEXAD

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T02:31:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020910223125.06896.00000857@mb-mv.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com>,
jeffreymcfarland@yahoo.com (Jeff McFarland) writes:

>I am trying to retreive more than 5 volsers from a COBOL program.  I
>found an old thread that explained how to use an assembler program to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Are you looking for how to be able to write to more than 5 carts 
for a dataset?
Isn't that something that can be changed on the DD statement?
UNIT=CART,LABEL=RETPD=007,VOL=(,RETAIN,,10)
If you are reading a previously created dataset, there should be no
problem regardless of how many CARTs.  (That is assuming the dataset
is cataloged.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JFCBEXAD

- **From:** jeffreymcfarland@yahoo.com (Jeff McFarland)
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T06:42:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<597cbd54.0209110542.5c55ea31@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com> <20020910223125.06896.00000857@mb-mv.aol.com>`

```
sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote in message 

> Are you looking for how to be able to write to more than 5 carts 
> for a dataset?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is cataloged.)


We have no problem writing the carts.  We have a new industry standard
that has substantially increased the amount of data that we have to
send to customers.  These are outgoing carts that need a report that
includes a list of all the volsers.

Does anyone know how to go about pointing to the JFCBEXAD as indicated
in the message above.  We have very limited assembler experience in
the shop.

Thanks

Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JFCBEXAD

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T18:29:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MsPf9.668$e44.17400@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **References:** `<597cbd54.0209101246.57913669@posting.google.com> <20020910223125.06896.00000857@mb-mv.aol.com> <597cbd54.0209110542.5c55ea31@posting.google.com>`

```
Jeff McFarland wrote:
> sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote in message 
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> the shop.

Here's something off the top, not the most elegant solution, I guess; 
but you could write a simple REXX program to OUTTRAP a listcat with the 
VOLUME option, parse that & create your report. That'll definitely give 
you all volumes. You could make it general purpose and have it determine 
the DSN from the listcat output.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JFCBEXAD

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-09-12T01:29:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020911212920.22428.00002018@mb-ch.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<597cbd54.0209110542.5c55ea31@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <597cbd54.0209110542.5c55ea31@posting.google.com>,
jeffreymcfarland@yahoo.com (Jeff McFarland) writes:

>send to customers.  These are outgoing carts that need a report that
>includes a list of all the volsers.
>

I vaguely remember doing this sort of thing for one of our clients.
It involved listing all the 'potential' VOLSERs on the DD Statement 
and then OPS would have to report on how many were actually created.

//           VOL=SER=(SIGM01,SIGM02,SIGM03,SIGM04,SIGM05,
//           SIGM06,SIGM07,SIGM08,SIGM09,SIGM10)
```

---

<a id="t-3c087f7f72"></a>
## Thread: Presidential eligibility. — 7 msgs

### Re: Presidential eligibility.

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T00:52:14-04:00

On Thu, 12 Sep 2002 10:19:00 GMT, Peter Lawrence
<peterl@netlink.com.au> enlightened us:

>Liam Devlin wrote:
>> 
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>that he described in it? PML.

Christopher Columbus discovered the islands, but it was the Danish in
the 1600's that established a colony and battled with the British for
dominance of the chain.  Britain, wanting to keep good relations with
Denmark, let them have the islands.


The United States bought the islands in 1917, as part of its military
defense strategy, for $25 million in gold. The US wanted to prevent
the islands from becoming a German sub base. But not until 1927 were
residents granted U.S. citizenship. During World War II, St. Thomas
was developed as a defense base. 

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Three things are certain! :
Death, taxes and lost data.
Guess which has occurred.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Presidential eligibility.

- **From:** Peter Lawrence <peterl@netlink.com.au>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D829640.97B@netlink.com.au>`
- **References:** `<s9jiqLA7q6c9EwLc@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <DFVc9.55011$vY2.1335831@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <YFnvkKAteid9EwGy@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3Cvd9.52871$0u4.879375@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <uiJSNtAWh1d9EwTX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <XuYd9.65$rU2.8408@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D7C06BA.1CF@netlink.com.au> <0vTe9.47507$rU2.1497758@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D7F4EDE.4565@netlink.com.au> <3_zf9.24993$ck2.895283@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <3D81575A.1768@netlink.com.au> <7B0663FB499C428D.1AF458CA96BE992B.12793FFFC7799895@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 12 Sep 2002 10:19:00 GMT, Peter Lawrence
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >>Peter Lawrence wrote:.
.
.
while everybody else was distracted, the pacifist Woodrow Wilson
> >> >>>DID seize other Danish possessions on a claim of emergency (and never
> >> >>>returned them).
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Denmark, let them have the islands.

No. At that date, England was a distinct country; the terms "Britain" and 
"British" do not apply.

> 
> The United States bought the islands in 1917, as part of its military
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> was developed as a defense base.

This is the way subtle propaganda develops; by people omitting unpleasant 
facts, suppressio veri rather than suggestio falsi. I am sure this poster was 
himself unaware of them, himself deceived. While this is all true as far as 
it goes, it omits serious and highly relevant information. It's like saying 
that the USA annexed Hawaii with the full consent of its government and 
omitting the fact that that government was itself the stalking horse used to 
destabilise independent Hawaii - anyone reading that without knowing the 
whole story would come away thinking there was no aggression, that the USA 
and Denmark had simply got together peacefully and made a deal.

The omission is the US seizure and the run up to that (i.e., the seriousness 
of the threat of German infiltration). The purchase was just the settlement 
wrapping that up. The purchase was made under even more duress than the 
Gadsden Purchase. As far as violating neutrality went, the actuality of US 
actions exceeded the hypothetical contingency of German action. It was a 
"cannot refuse" kind of offer, at bayonet point.

By the way, "granting citizenship" is itself a weasel worded way of 
describing deprivation of rights - people get citizenship thrust upon them 
regardless, with no effective means of exercising it as against their 
conquerors or of maintaining their former ways (we saw the Argentinians try 
that one as a pretended way of looking after the Falkland Islanders). The 
correct way of handling these things, the one that respects rights and was 
fully understood at the time, is the "optant" method. I regret that Australia 
did not offer it to the Aborigines.

What remains outstanding in this matter, as before, is the full detail; I had 
thought that the seizure was in 1915 and was closely followed by the 
annexation. I now find that the latter took place in 1917; so I still have to 
check out whether my first recollection was correct, or if the seizure took 
place in 1917 too - which would have been less reprehensible than doing it in 
1915 or thereabouts, though still unjust.

In the circumstances, I will have to check some primary (and non-US) sources. 
I'll try and let you know how it goes. Oh, and I do not wish to suggest that 
the USA is the only perpetrator of this sort of thing - just the one that 
matters directly in this day and age. PML.
```

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
> SkippyPB wrote:
> > On Thu, 12 Sep 2002 10:19:00 GMT, Peter Lawrence
> > <peterl@netlink.com.au> enlightened us:

.
.
.
> >>By the way, I take it you are familiar with de Valera's back handed
> >>compliment to Churchill for not violating Irish neutrality, in his famous
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> PS for PML: not familiar with de Valera's compliment to Churchill, either.

Ah. He made a speech right after the end of hostilities, in response to one 
of Churchill's in which, as well as celebrating a job seen through to the 
end, Churchill made a back handed swipe at Ireland for taking a free ride (he 
distinguished that from the behaviour of various individual Irishmen).

Anyhow, de Valera made a dignified rebuttal speech in which he pointed out 
how meaningless sovereignty was if it could be overridden at pleasure or need 
of others, and how valuable it was for the whole world that greater powers 
should in fact respect the sovereignty and even neutrality of smaller ones. 
Right there, he made a back handed compliment to Churchill by approving the 
way that, despite great temptation, he had not after all violated Irish 
neutrality.

I believe the text is available on the internet, though no doubt edited and 
(as ever) not as trustworthy as a library source. PML.
```

## Thread: cobol runtime error 46 for cobol 85 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### cobol runtime error 46 for cobol 85

- **From:** dannyk@kayafmmail.co.za (daniel)
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T05:32:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cbd9fc28.0209110432.5fa82aee@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi, 

When I run my COBOL program I get a file status code 46. How do I go
about solving that error. I am using ACUCOBOL-GT on  UNIX .

Please Help!

Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol runtime error 46 for cobol 85

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T15:41:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-LHloyCwLd6zD@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<cbd9fc28.0209110432.5fa82aee@posting.google.com>`

```
On Wed, 11 Sep 2002 13:32:29 UTC, dannyk@kayafmmail.co.za (daniel) 
wrote:

> Hi, 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please Help!

I believe that  a file status code of 46 indicates (quote from the 
manual)

Status 4/6    A sequential READ statement was attempted on a file 
open in the input or I-O mode and no valid next     
record had been established because:   
1.  The preceding READ statement was unsuccessful   
but did not cause an at end condition               
2.  The preceding READ statement caused an at end   
condition.                                          

You are probably trying to read the file after an "AT END" has 
happened.

Check your code to see what the problem is.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol runtime error 46 for cobol 85

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T14:43:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209111343.505a92b5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cbd9fc28.0209110432.5fa82aee@posting.google.com>`

```
dannyk@kayafmmail.co.za (daniel) wrote in message news:<cbd9fc28.0209110432.5fa82aee@posting.google.com>...
> Hi, 
> 
> When I run my COBOL program I get a file status code 46. How do I go
> about solving that error. I am using ACUCOBOL-GT on  UNIX .

File status '46' is a logic error in your program.  You are trying to
do a sequential READ of the 'next' record when there is no 'current'
record at that time.

When a file is OPENed the 'current' record is just before the first
record in the file so a sequential read will read the first, and set
this to 'current'.

After reading the last record on the file another sequential read will
give an 'AT END' and the 'current' record will be undefined.

After a random read or a START that failed 'INVALID KEY' the 'current'
position is also undefined.

So 46 is indicating that you have tried to do a sequential read after
'AT END' or 'INVALID KEY' has occurred.
```

## Thread: Acucobol and Oracle — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Acucobol and Oracle

- **From:** pivanushka@lakeshorelearning.com (pivan)
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T10:15:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2d58ed45.0209110915.2f3b6da8@posting.google.com>`

```
I am doing some research on Acucobol and am trying to find answers to
three questions.

1) What versions of Oracle does Acucobol / Acu4g1 (Unix platform)
support?

2) Can I run my Oracle database on a server other then the one that I
am running my Acucobol applications on?

3) What kind of challenges are there in upgrading from Oracle 8.1.6 to
Oracle 8.1.7?

Thanks in advance for your help.

pivan
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol and Oracle

- **From:** Daniel Morgan <dmorgan@exesolutions.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-11T19:49:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D7F9E31.4FB78812@exesolutions.com>`
- **References:** `<2d58ed45.0209110915.2f3b6da8@posting.google.com>`

```
pivan wrote:

> I am doing some research on Acucobol and am trying to find answers to
> three questions.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> pivan

Can't help you with questions 1 and 2. But number three is completely
lacking in challenge if you take 20 minutes to read the upgrade
instructions on the CD or at http://docs.oracle.com before jumping into
it.

Be sure to make sure that shared memory and semaphore settings are correct
for 8.1.7 and your environment. Don't just trust that the SysAdmin that
did it for 8.1.6 did it correctly for 8.1.7. In Solaris set them in
/etc/system. In HP/UX using SAM.

Daniel Morgan
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol and Oracle

- **From:** "Follana Gustavo" <follana.gustavo@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-09-14T22:05:27+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<am052q$qpv$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<2d58ed45.0209110915.2f3b6da8@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello, I am new on the group, but I think I can give you a hand.
1) All depends on the version of Acu4GL that you are using. If it is the
last one
(5.2) then you can use Oracle 7 , 8 and 8i. You can do the 9(I have done it)
But it is not supported officialy yet (but it works)
2) Yes, you can... but, and there is a but, you need to install Oracle
client on
the second machine.
3) The biggest challenge here it will be that you need to relink again.
I don't know if you know, but to have the Acu4GL work with Oracle you
need to relink it with some libraries. (It is not too hard if you know what
you
are doing). So, when you change Oracle 8.1.6 for Oracle 8.1.7 you will
have to relink to produce a new Acu4GL (libraries do change from one to
another).

Anything else?

Gustavo

"pivan" <pivanushka@lakeshorelearning.com> a �crit dans le message news:
2d58ed45.0209110915.2f3b6da8@posting.google.com...
> I am doing some research on Acucobol and am trying to find answers to
> three questions.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> pivan
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-09-12T11:57:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1031831774@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?

<a id="t-74aa7d75bf"></a>
## Thread: Final comment on draft (probably soon to be official) COBOL Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Final comment on draft (probably soon to be official) COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-13T18:55:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<am0rm7$pcl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those of you who might be interested,  my "US public review comments" on
the final draft of the COBOL Standard can be seen from the INCITS (US
Standards body) web page at:

 http://www.incits.org/archive/2002/it02818r/it02818r.htm

That page links to
  http://www.incits.org/archive/2002/it02818r/prcmt2_fdis1989.pdf


FYI - Despite what I sent in (and what the only other public review comment
received by the "due date" of Sep 9th said), it is my STRONGEST possible
expectation that this will be an "official" ANSI and ISO Standard "around"
year end (of this year).

NOTE WELL:
  (This was sent to both comp.lang.cobol and IBM-MAIN).
For those of you in an IBM environment (mainframe *or* workstation),  all
current indications are that IBM has no *current* plans to implement this
full Standard.  They have indicated that they will "consider" implementing
specific features - based on user demand vs implementation resource
requirements vs expected revenue.

Although late public review responses need not be "fully responded to" - if
you really DO want to send in a "late comment", you should follow the
instructions at:

  http://www.incits.org/prsweb.htm

I can't (personally) think of any "good reason" to do this, but that is up
to every individual "COBOL interested" person.

Final Note:
  This information ONLY applies to the ANSI (US) review process.  If you are
"involved" in COBOL in another country, you will need to contact your
national Standards body to find out what (if anything) they have done about
this final "approval step" of the next COBOL Standard.
```

What a great language is COBOL: With it one can call a spade 'A
Spade'; Or one can call a Dunning file a DUNNING-FILE.

To quote The Concise Oxford Dictionary:
   dun (noun and verb) 1. a debt collector, 2. a demand for payment 
   (verb transitive (dunned, dunning) importune for payment of a debt.

So  David was spot on. A dunning file is indeed a list of debtors for
whom the bell is about to toll.

Have a nice day!
Gareth

Seeing that the topic of N. American Indians has been covered recently,
thought some of you might be interested in the following :-

        www.treaty7.org

We are at the 125th anniversary of the Canadian Treaty 7, carefully
referred to as an 'anniversary, NOT a 'celebration'. There's a week of
activities starting Monday some 85 kms east of Calgary.

(Historically - NWMP (RCMP) - 1873 Fort Macleod near our southern
border, 1875 Fort Calgary, 1877 - Treaty 7).

We avoided tragedies like Little Big Horn and Wounded Knee - but I don't
think the  Indians are any better off. Their way of life has gone
forever.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-ef0bbbf6e6"></a>
## Thread: COBOL complier — 3 msgs

### COBOL complier

- **From:** sophia <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-15T21:30:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d854240_5@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Any complier to recommend? I have tried the Fujitsu COBOL using my
college days source code which was complied properly w/o errors. I tried
it on Fujitsu COBOL it came out with errors which I do not understand at
all. Any other complier I could try? I learn from somewhere that MS
Visual Studio can complie COBOL source codes. Anyone tried it before?
How does it works? Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL complier

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-16T03:35:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dkch9.56657$z91.2254124@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3d854240_5@news.onlynews.com>`

```

"sophia" <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message
news:3d854240_5@news.onlynews.com...
> Any complier to recommend? I have tried the Fujitsu COBOL using my
> college days source code which was complied properly w/o errors. I tried
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> How does it works? Thanks.


Post the source code and the errors generated by the Fijutsu compiler and
we'll be happy to diagnose them for you. It is equally likely that the
college compiler you were using was non-standard or that you overlooked
something quite trivial (like a copy book).

The Fujitsu compiler is full ANSI-85 compliant. Virtually any errors
resulting from a FJ compile will also occur on someone else's compiler.

There is no COBOL compiler within Visual Studio.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL complier

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-15T22:32:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209152132.77b71402@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3d854240_5@news.onlynews.com>`

```
sophia <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote 

> Any complier to recommend? I have tried the Fujitsu COBOL using my
> college days source code which was complied properly w/o errors. I tried
> it on Fujitsu COBOL it came out with errors which I do not understand at
> all. 

Most errors of that type are because the source contains actual errors
which are allowed by some other compiler.  That is you have not
constructed your program correctly but the compiler you used didn't
enforce correctness.  Alternately you used extensions that were not
implemented by Fujitsu.

Get the downloadable Fujitsu manuals.  The error message will tell you
the line number, look at that line in the source code, compare that
with the syntax in the manual, see where the code is wrong - or should
be different.  This is a basic programmer skill which you should be
trying to develop before moving on to more advanced issues.

> Any other complier I could try? 

Fujitsu is OK.  There is a free student edition.  For others, how much
are you willing to spend ?

> I learn from somewhere that MS
> Visual Studio can complie COBOL source codes. Anyone tried it before?
> How does it works? Thanks.

It uses Fujitsu NetCobol for .NET.  Demo available at www.adtools.com
```

---

<a id="t-67b022389a"></a>
## Thread: Question regd Symbolics and Instreams — 4 msgs

### Question regd Symbolics and Instreams

- **From:** arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath)
- **Date:** 2002-09-16T10:26:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3ef804e.0209160926.7553c2b7@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all, 

I am trying to pass some information instream through the SYSIN DD to
a rexx program by using symbolics. However, the symbolics are not
expanding when instream.

//ERRORLG EXEC ERRORLG, 
// IMEM='INPUT MEMBER', 
// OMEM='OUTPUT MEMBER',
// FLG='FLAG',          
// DBUG='DEBUG'         
       :
       :
//SYSTSIN    DD   *                           
  %TSTADDJB &IMEM &OMEM 'TYPE1ERR' &FLG &DBUG 
/*

Can anyone suggest a way out?

Thanks in advance
Arun Prasath
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regd Symbolics and Instreams

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-09-16T23:48:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020916194829.06896.00001305@mb-mv.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0209160926.7553c2b7@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <a3ef804e.0209160926.7553c2b7@posting.google.com>,
arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath) writes:

>//ERRORLG EXEC ERRORLG, 
>// IMEM='INPUT MEMBER', 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know as I have ever encountered an instance where the JCL is able to 
translate replacement variables in the instream data.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question regd Symbolics and Instreams

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-09-17T10:47:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UKDh9.9823$yt3.4761248@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0209160926.7553c2b7@posting.google.com> <20020916194829.06896.00001305@mb-mv.aol.com>`

```


alsystems.com
"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20020916194829.06896.00001305@mb-mv.aol.com...
> In article <a3ef804e.0209160926.7553c2b7@posting.google.com>,
> arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath) writes:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't know as I have ever encountered an instance where the JCL is able
to
> translate replacement variables in the instream data.

JCL cannot handle this. Most shops have a utility program "somewhere" which
takes a PARM and writes it to a disk file which can be passed to the next
job step. JCL _does_ expand the symbolics when passed as a parm.

EXEC PGM =PARMWRIT,PARM='%TSTADDJB &IMEM &OMEM 'TYPE1ERR' &FLG &DBUG '
OUTF  DD    DSN=&&PARM,DISP=(NEW,PASS)

EXEC ERRORLG
...
>>//SYSTIN DD DSN=*.OUTF,DISP=(OLD,PASS)

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question regd Symbolics and Instreams

- **From:** arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath)
- **Date:** 2002-09-18T20:20:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3ef804e.0209181920.495807f5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0209160926.7553c2b7@posting.google.com> <20020916194829.06896.00001305@mb-mv.aol.com> <UKDh9.9823$yt3.4761248@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
> JCL cannot handle this. Most shops have a utility program "somewhere" which
> takes a PARM and writes it to a disk file which can be passed to the next
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...

Thanks a lot guys! It worked by passing all the parameters through
PARM instead of as instream.
Arun
```

<a id="t-f117d78e02"></a>
## Thread: ISO COBOL Standard - It's FINAL — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### ISO COBOL Standard - It's FINAL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-16T13:56:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<am59g8$tg3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,
  The ISO COBOL Standard was approved with NO negative votes.  See the
following information that was just distributed.

"Information technology -- Programming languages -- COBOL
 Document:ISO/IEC FDIS 1989

RESULT OF VOTING

P-Members voting: 16 in favour out of 16 = 100 % (requirement >= 66.66%)

(P-members having abstained are not counted in the vote)

Member bodies voting: 0 negative votes out of 22 = 0 % (requirement <= 25%)

APPROVED
"

(FYI, for those of you "used to" ANSI COBOL numbers, don't be surprised by
the "1989" in the official number of this document.  This is a "random"
number - like the 10646 in the "character" Standard - and has NOTHING to do
with a date/year.  I believe it is now "safe" to call this a 2002 Standard.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ISO COBOL Standard - It's FINAL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-18T16:13:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<amaqag$qnp$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<am59g8$tg3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <amaom80bsp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
The last full revision was approved in 1985.  The last amendment adding
functionality was approved in 1989.  The last amendment (corrections only -
at least in theory) was approved in 1993.  You can calculate accordingly.

To calculate when you can expect to see many implementations of the FULL
Standard (not just "bits and pieces") take the number 1 and divide by ZERO.
You may get a SLIGHTLY "quicker" number depending on your operating system
and "COBOL vendor of choice".
```

- **From:** andrade <member@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-17T17:42:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1825859.1032284560@dbforums.com>`

```

Good Morning,


      I've some programs developted in MicroFocus Cobol Netexpress 3.1
      and I use IDXFORMAT "4" for my database files but I don't have the
      source code neither the FD cobol files, but I'd like to access
      this database for another aplications developed in VB6, is it
      possible ?
      Is there any program or tool that could read and generate the
      "*.fdd" files form my database files ?


I hope that could have some solution for my problem !


Thank you !
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol ".FDD" Files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-09-17T21:04:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D879AC7.E1C7467F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<1825859.1032284560@dbforums.com>`

```


andrade wrote:

> Good Morning,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com

For what it's worth to you :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. IDXFORMAT :

Specifies the format to use when creating indexed files.

$set IDXFORMAT "integer"

 Parameters:

integer An integer representing the type to use.

Properties:

Default: IDXFORMAT"0"

Phase: Syntax check
$SET: Any

Dependencies:

Set to IDXFORMAT"integer" immediately by FILETYPE"integer".

Comments:

The possible values of integer are:

0 System-specific default (for this COBOL system, same as 3).

1 Reserved.
2 Micro Focus Level II format.
3 The format used by this COBOL system.
4 An optimized form of the format used by this system, for fast duplicate
key handling.
5 Btrieve with ANSI
6 Btrieve without ANSI
7 Reserved.
8 Large indexed.
9-255 Reserved.

Existing files in any of the given formats are processed correctly without
the need for this directive. This directive controls the format used when
creating new files.

Specifying 3 always causes the format used by this COBOL system to be
created; if you specify 0, and you are using your program with a file
handler from a different system, the default for that system is created.

Specifying 4 might make the files larger than their IDXFORMAT"3"
equivalents.

Micro Focus Level II format files are compatible with Micro Focus products
such as Level II COBOL, Professional COBOL V1.2, and VS COBOL Workbench
versions up to and including V1.3.

You must not use the ANS85 directive to enable ANSI'85 behavior when using
IDXFORMAT"2". However, you can use ANS85"SYNTAX" to enable ANSI'85 syntax.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

That's merely setting the 'efficiency' approach for the indexing. Note so
far as importing to VB, IDXFORMAT"4" implies the file contains alternate
keys (additional IDs) that can be duplicated, e.g. :-

A/C ID    AltKey(Lastname)    Firstnames
12345     Smith                        Alan
12434    Smith                         Arthur
12787    Smith                         Mary

2. FILE - RECORD FORMATS

 As to reading the files, no doubt Ed Guy will jump in and mention his Parse
Rat, ($49.95). But what are you wanting to do. You want to access the COBOL
file system to read into VB. Is this something you want to do on a regular
basis or is this a 'once only' converting to VB ?

If you will be jumping backwards and forwards, between the two, it would
make sense for the person who wrote it originally in Net Express to provide
record formats and a Line Sequential File (CSV text file) that you could
import into VB.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DAT and IDX files

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-17T22:24:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209172124.510c0978@posting.google.com>`

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DAT and IDX files

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-09-23T04:19:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0209230319.66319da3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ajvgid$gu3$2@news.tpi.pl> <1826391.1032292053@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0209172124.510c0978@posting.google.com>`

```
You could probably read the .DAT file with a C program, or in any
other language.  Simply add up the total lenght of the record, say
100.  Then write a program in whatever language you use to read 100
characters and display it, then read another 100 and display, etc.  If
you can see a pattern, maybe offset on each record, then simply change
the 100 to some other number.  By trial and error you may be able to
set the read so that the same data is always in the same position on
each record read.

Compressed numeric data may be a problem - but if you know the answer
you may be able to determine the conversion.  Or initially miss the
numerics out and try to extract just the text.

Compressed files are more of a problem, as the resultant records can
be variable length.  But even this is doable, if each record ends in a
recognizable character - simply read one byte at a time until you
recognize the character, process that record, then continue reading
...

It is doable.  I converted files from a Unix system, written in basic,
to text files - with no help from the originator of the data.  These
had variable length records, compression, and all sorts of problems.

Ask, or send a sample, if you need any help.

All the best.
Phil Hickling.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DAT and IDX files

- **From:** andrade <member@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2002-09-23T13:00:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1846682.1032786059@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<ajvgid$gu3$2@news.tpi.pl> <1826391.1032292053@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0209172124.510c0978@posting.google.com>`

```

         the database structure file  of my DAT, IDX files...



Thank you Amigos !

#### ↳ Re: I haven't the FD description of my DB

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-09-17T22:21:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0209172121.7611a62a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1826650.1032297055@dbforums.com>`

```
andrade <member@dbforums.com> wrote 

>          I just like to know how can I  extract the field description of
>          my Database files, is there any way to do it some tool or

The field descriptions are not in the database files, they are in the
program source code, probably in a 'copy' file.

>          program ? my intention's access the database files to read and
>          write, I know how to generate a Ascii or c-Isam format by
>          rebuid, but I need to write directly in the file without change
>          the database structure file  of my DAT, IDX files...

You are more likely to corrupt the data and/or index unless you use
the exact Cobol mechanism.  For example adding a new indexed file
record may require an index block split and index rotation.  You also
need to have the same exact locking mechanism otherwise you may be
updating at the same time as a Cobol program is and the result would
be corruption.

If you have a developer licence then you should have a callable file
handler that you could use.
```

- **References:** `<1826650.1032297055@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0209172121.7611a62a@posting.google.com>`

    Microfocus has a callable file handler, echich can be called
from C. In addition, MF, as well as other compilers can be
caused to use Btrieve with almost no source changes.
```

andrade <member@dbforums.com> wrote

>          - I can generate a output file Rebuilding in LII or C-Isam
>            format,
>            and then to use this file. ( the problem is that I have
>            to generate the output file whenever the database file

you can read the LII format then rebuild to LII, backup and substitute
these for the existing files and test.
